Drought tolerant and low-maintenance, it attracts pollinators and self-sows freely for naturalized hillsides of color. Grow in full sun and lean, well-drained soil.

plant_note: Start indoors early; surface sow as light aids germination.
directions: Start indoors six to eight weeks before the last frost, pressing seed onto the surface as light aids germination. This variety wows with peachy-orange blossoms brushed with pink above lush green foliage on long stems. Pinch young plants for bushier growth and cut freely for long-lasting bouquets. The blooms are edible and make a whimsical garnish for salads and desserts.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring as soon as the ground can be worked; fall planting is preferred in zones 4–7.
directions: Plant bulbs 6–8 in deep (3 times the bulb diameter) in well-drained, organically rich, slightly acidic soil; apply mulch to keep roots cool. Oriental lilies prefer to have their heads in the sun and their feet in the shade — interplant with low perennials to shade the root zone. The large, intensely fragrant blooms appear in mid- to late summer; deadhead after flowering but allow foliage to remain until it yellows naturally. Avoid overhead watering to reduce the risk of botrytis; stake tall cultivars against wind damage.

plant_note: Surface sow in early spring; light aids germination.
directions: Surface sow in early spring, pressing the fine seed onto the soil since light aids germination. This variety forms a low, spreading carpet of fragrant deep purple blooms that flower all season and spill nicely over container edges. Withstanding heat and drought, it is an ideal rock garden and edging plant that draws pollinators. Shear lightly midseason if flowering slows.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; seeds need cool soil and germinate best when sown directly outdoors.
directions: Surface-sow seeds in early spring on well-prepared, well-drained soil; seeds need cool temperatures and light, so press firmly without covering. Oriental poppies bloom for a brief but spectacular 2–3 weeks in late spring to early summer with crepe-paper petals in orange, red, pink, or white up to 6 in across. Plants go dormant after bloom leaving a bare spot — interplant with late-emerging annuals like baby's breath or dahlias to fill the gap. Divide clumps in late summer while dormant; established plants dislike being moved during the growing season.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost, or sow in fall where winters are mild.
directions: Sow seeds on the soil surface in early spring as they need light and cool temperatures to germinate; do not cover seeds. Orlaya produces flat-topped umbel flowers with large, irregular outer florets that create a delicate lace effect in pure white, ideal as a cut flower filler. It prefers lean, well-drained soil — overly rich conditions produce lush foliage at the expense of flowers. Plants self-sow readily where happy, returning reliably if the ground is not disturbed; collect seed or allow them to fall for the following year.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall, 4–8 weeks before the ground freezes, for late-spring to early-summer bloom.
directions: Plant bulbs in fall at a depth of 3 times their diameter in sharply drained soil; the large-flowered types like 'Globemaster' can be planted 12 in deep for stability. Foliage often begins to yellow while the flowers are still in bloom — interplanting with perennials helps mask the dying leaves. Deer and rodents avoid alliums, making them a good choice for wildlife-prone gardens. Spherical seedheads are ornamentally interesting and can be dried for arrangements.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after soil has warmed thoroughly.
directions: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost at 70–75°F; transplant after frost danger passes into full sun and fertile, well-drained soil — warm soil is essential for strong establishment. Grown as an annual for its striking deep burgundy-purple foliage and the large, cylindrical flower/grain heads that arch gracefully under their own weight in late summer. An excellent anchor plant in tropical-style or mixed annual borders, and the dried seed heads are useful in arrangements. Highly attractive to birds in fall — leave some plants intact for wildlife value.

plant_note: Direct sow spring or fall; biennial that blooms in year two.
directions: Direct sow in spring or fall against a wall or fence that can support the towering spikes; as a biennial it typically blooms the second year. Barely cover the seed and keep moist until germination. This old cottage-garden hollyhock reaches several feet tall with a long bloom period, and the flowers are edible though bland. Stake in windy sites and allow some plants to self-sow for continuous stands.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; tolerates light frost.
directions: Direct sow in early spring, covering seed since darkness aids germination; calendula thrives in cool weather and tolerates light frost. This classic cottage-garden blend bears gold and orange flowers from spring to fall on drought- and heat-tolerant plants. Deadhead to prolong bloom, and save the edible petals for teas, salads, and salves. Grow in full sun for the fullest flowering.

plant_note: Start indoors early; chilling seed improves germination.
directions: Start seed indoors early, chilling it briefly to improve germination for this award-winning heirloom perennial. Set plants in rich, well-drained soil and grow in groups of three or more for impact. Tall, elegant spires pack large blossoms in pink, violet, and many shades of blue; stake the stems against wind. Cut back after the first flush to encourage a second bloom, and provide steady moisture.

plant_note: Sow in place in early spring for summer color.
directions: Direct sow annual painted daisy in early spring where plants are to bloom. The colorful banded daisies with contrasting central rings make superb long-lasting cut flowers. Grow in full sun and average, well-drained soil, staking taller plants in windy sites. Deadhead frequently to prolong flowering, and the plants often self-sow lightly in favorable conditions.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant 2–3 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate light frost.
directions: Sow seeds in the dark (cover lightly and keep the tray in darkness) at 60–70°F; darkness improves germination for salpiglossis. Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; the petunia-like flowers with intricate veining in yellow, red, purple, maroon, and bicolor thrive in cool weather. Provide light support with twiggy stakes since plants can flop in wind; pinch tips once for bushier plants. Plants decline in summer heat — treat them as a spring and fall flower in warm climates or plant where afternoon shade moderates temperatures.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost when soil is warm, or start indoors.
directions: Direct sow after frost into warm soil, or start indoors a few weeks earlier, pressing the fine seed onto the surface for light. This blend produces large, feathery plumes ranging from golden-yellow to scarlet on long, straight stems that attract pollinators. The multi-branching plants have handsome foliage and love heat. Harvest for fresh bouquets or hang to dry for lasting arrangements.

plant_note: Surface sow in early spring or fall; dislikes transplanting.
directions: Surface sow the tiny seed in early spring or fall, as poppies need light to germinate and resent transplanting. Tall, slender stems are topped with two- to three-inch silky double and semi-double blooms that shift from deep burgundy to soft pinkish-red, each striped with silver. Pollinators love them, and no two flowers look alike. Thin seedlings and let some self-sow for next season.

plant_note: Start indoors early in darkness; cool-season bedding plant.
directions: Start seed indoors in darkness eight to ten weeks before setting out, since pansies germinate best without light and prefer cool conditions. This gold-standard performer brightens cool-season gardens with rosy-red blooms and yellow centers on a compact, well-branched habit that resists winter weather. Grow in full sun to part shade in borders, containers, or mass plantings. The flowers are edible and hold up to frost.

plant_note: Start indoors 12–14 weeks before last frost for spring planting, or start in late summer for fall transplanting. Plants tolerate hard frost once established.
directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep in a moist starting mix; cover the tray with dark plastic as darkness aids germination. Transplant outdoors several weeks before the last frost, as cool weather brings out the richest colors and most abundant bloom. Deadhead spent flowers regularly to prevent seed set, which stops flowering. Plants decline as summer heat arrives; remove and replace with heat-tolerant annuals.

plant_note: Start indoors; cold stratification aids germination.
directions: Start seed indoors, giving it a cold-moist stratification period to improve germination for this first-year-flowering dwarf echinacea. Set plants in full sun and well-drained soil where the vibrant blooms will draw pollinators. Nearly care-free once established and drought tolerant, it makes a fine fresh or dried cut flower. Leave the seed cones standing for winter interest and to feed birds.

plant_note: Plant in fall in a sheltered spot; blooms are heavy.
directions: Plant parrot tulip bulbs in autumn six to eight inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil and a spot sheltered from wind. The flamboyant fringed and twisted petals are streaked and feathered in bold colors, making dramatic cut flowers. Because the large blooms can flop, choose a protected location and stake if necessary. Let foliage die back naturally, and in mild-winter areas chill bulbs before planting.

plant_note: Sow fresh seed in fall; sap and foliage can irritate skin — handle with gloves and avoid contact with eyes.
directions: Sow fresh seed in fall directly in a sunny, gravelly, or rocky, very well-drained site; pasque flower germinates best from fresh seed with natural cold stratification and is slow to establish but extremely long-lived once settled. Silky, nodding purple, lavender, or white flowers emerge in early spring from the fuzzy, hairy foliage and are followed by fluffy, ornamental seedheads similar to clematis. Perfectly drained, lean, alkaline or neutral soil is essential — clay or continuously wet conditions are fatal. Resents transplanting; sow in situ or plant container-grown specimens when small.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; dislikes root disturbance.
directions: Direct sow the large seed about half an inch deep after frost, as nasturtiums dislike transplanting; soaking overnight speeds sprouting. This compact variety bears creamy peach blooms with red centers above rounded leaves. Both flowers and leaves have a peppery, watercress-like flavor that livens up salads. Grow in lean soil and full sun, since rich soil promotes leaves over flowers.

plant_note: Set out dormant or potted plants in spring after hard frost.
directions: Plant this vigorous climber from a potted or bare-root plant in spring, setting it in full sun with rich, well-drained soil at the base of a sturdy trellis or fence. It offers a cascading display of soft pink blooms and is renowned for thriving in tough conditions with minimal care, even shrugging off common rose troubles. Train the long canes horizontally to maximize flowering and water deeply while establishing. A fast grower that rewards patience with abundant bloom.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; seeds benefit from 4 weeks of cold stratification.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds for 4 weeks in damp medium in the refrigerator before sowing indoors for best germination; transplant after the last frost into well-drained to dry soil. Penstemon thrives in poor, gravelly soil and is one of the most important native nectar plants for hummingbirds and native bees. The tubular white to pale pink flowers appear in late spring to early summer; deadhead to a basal rosette of leaves after bloom. Excellent drainage is critical — wet winter conditions are the leading cause of plant loss.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist fine-textured mix and provide light at 70–80°F; germination occurs within 7–14 days. Pentas is one of the best butterfly and hummingbird plants for hot, humid gardens, blooming continuously from transplant to frost without deadheading. Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile soil and fertilize monthly to sustain the heavy flower production. Shear plants back by one-third in midsummer if they become leggy; they recover quickly in heat and resume flowering within 2–3 weeks.

plant_note: Plant bare-root divisions in fall 4–6 weeks before the ground freezes; eyes no deeper than 1–2 in below soil surface.
directions: Plant roots with the pink eyes no more than 1–2 in below the soil surface — planting too deeply is the most common reason peonies fail to bloom. Peonies require 6 weeks of temperatures below 40°F to set flower buds, so they need cold winters and are not suited to zones 9+. Expect minimal bloom the first 1–2 years as the plant establishes; mature plants can live 50+ years with little care. Cut foliage to the ground in fall to remove potential disease inoculum.

plant_note: Surface sow in early spring or fall; dislikes transplanting.
directions: Surface sow the fine seed directly in early spring or fall, as these poppies need light to germinate and resent root disturbance. Fully double, three- to five-inch ruffled flowers in many colors rise above soft, blue-green foliage that pollinators adore. Thin seedlings to give each plant room, and leave the decorative seed pods for dried arrangements. Fairly low-maintenance and self-sowing.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost; loves heat.
directions: This zinnia produces cheerful 2 to 4 inch blooms striped and speckled in bright colors, with no two flowers exactly alike. Heat-loving and easy to grow, it brightens beds, borders, and containers until fall frost and makes an excellent cut flower. Sow directly in warm soil or start indoors, and deadhead spent blooms to keep flowering. Give it full sun and good airflow to limit powdery mildew.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; nick and soak seed to aid germination.
directions: Nick and soak the hard seed, then direct sow in early spring at the base of a trellis, fence, or arbor for this everlasting perennial sweet pea. The long, lush trailing vines ramble beautifully over shrubs or serve as a groundcover and return each year. It withstands summer heat well, is fairly drought tolerant, and attracts pollinators, though unlike annual sweet peas the flowers are not fragrant. Note that the seeds are not edible.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost danger passes in well-drained soil; established plants are drought-tolerant.
directions: Plant in well-drained soil — perennial salvia is exceptionally heat- and drought-tolerant once established and will rot in soggy conditions. Shear plants back by one-third to one-half after the first flush of deep violet-blue spikes fades to trigger a strong second (and sometimes third) bloom. The aromatic foliage deters deer and rabbits, making it a reliable choice for wildlife-challenged gardens. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in early spring to keep them vigorous.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; nick and soak seed to aid germination.
directions: Nick and soak the seed, then direct sow in early spring at the foot of a trellis, as sweet peas relish cool weather and need support to climb. This rainbow blend of large, ruffled flowers in purple, red, rose, scarlet, white, blue, cream, and bicolors carries an exquisite old-fashioned fragrance. More heat resistant than many strains, it still fades in high summer, so sow early. Cut often to keep the fragrant blooms coming.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost when soil is warm.
directions: Direct sow into warm soil after frost; this narrow-leaved Mexican zinnia handles hot, dry conditions with ease. It bears bicolored single and double flowers in gold, burgundy, cream, red, and orange that bloom until first frost. Compact and reliable, it suits beds, borders, and containers and needs little water once established. Deadhead to extend the display and draw pollinators.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost when soil is warm.
directions: Direct sow into warm soil after frost; adapted to hot, dry climates, this species-type zinnia asks little water once established. Its simple, open, single flowers make ideal landing pads for pollinators and self-sow to return year after year. Give full sun and good airflow to limit mildew, and cut or deadhead to keep the airy plants blooming. A charming, drought-tolerant choice for naturalized plantings.

plant_note: Plant tuberous roots in spring; superb cut flower.
directions: Plant Peruvian lily tuberous roots in spring about six inches deep in rich, well-drained soil, handling the brittle roots carefully. The orchid-like flowers in warm oranges, pinks, and reds are among the longest-lasting of all cut flowers. Grow in full sun to light shade with consistent moisture, and pull rather than cut spent stems to stimulate rebloom. In colder zones mulch heavily for winter or lift the roots for storage.

plant_note: Start indoors early; surface sow as light aids germination.
directions: Start seed indoors eight to ten weeks before the last frost, pressing the tiny seed onto the surface since light is essential for germination. This compact blend blooms prolifically all summer in red, rose, scarlet, salmon, violet, and white, attracting butterflies and pollinators. Set out after frost in full sun in window boxes, borders, and containers. Deadhead and feed regularly to sustain the heavy flowering.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow tiny seed on the surface of moist starting mix — do not cover, as petunias need light to germinate. Pinch transplants by one-third when planting to encourage branching. Deadhead or shear back leggy plants by half in midsummer to renew vigor and flush of bloom. Feed every two weeks with a balanced liquid fertilizer for best performance.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost or start indoors 4-6 weeks earlier.
directions: Direct sow after frost or start indoors four to six weeks earlier for this tall African marigold. It produces large, bright yellow, fully double blooms on sturdy stems that make excellent cut flowers. Give it full sun and space to reach its mature height, staking if needed in exposed sites. Deadhead to keep the big, cheerful flowers coming from summer until frost.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant 2 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate light frost.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in a well-drained mix; germination is reliable at 60–70°F and seedlings grow quickly. Plant in full sun in well-drained, slightly alkaline soil — scabiosa performs best in cool to moderate temperatures and fades in intense summer heat. Cut flowers frequently to encourage continuous bloom from early summer until fall; flowers are excellent for cutting with long, wiry stems and last 7–10 days in a vase. In zones 7–10 try sowing in fall for earlier spring bloom before heat sets in.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger has passed into a sunny, alkaline-friendly site.
directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost, sowing 1/4 in deep in a light germination mix; transplant outdoors after frost into well-drained, slightly alkaline or neutral soil. The domed, pincushion flowers in rich maroon, lavender, white, pink, and burgundy are magnets for butterflies and superb for cutting. Deadhead consistently to prevent self-seeding and extend the long bloom period from early summer through frost. Avoid rich, wet soils — scabiosa thrives in moderately lean conditions and is drought-tolerant once established.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in spring after soil warms; lift where not hardy.
directions: Plant pineapple lily bulbs in spring about five inches deep in rich, well-drained soil once the ground has warmed. Each stout stem produces a dense spike of starry flowers topped by a tuft of leafy bracts resembling a pineapple. Grow in full sun with regular summer moisture, and it also performs beautifully in large containers. In cold climates lift the bulbs before frost or grow them in pots moved indoors for winter.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring; avoid fall planting in zones 6–7 as plants need a full season to establish before winter.
directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained, lean to average soil; muhly grass is tolerant of sandy soil, heat, humidity, and drought once established and is an outstanding choice for hot, dry southern landscapes. The main attraction is the spectacular cloud of airy, rose-pink flower plumes in September–October that transform the planting into something otherworldly when backlit by low autumn sun. Cut back to 4–6 in in late winter before new growth begins; avoid heavy organic mulching which can cause crown rot in humid climates. Divide every 4–5 years in spring to rejuvenate large clumps.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring; dislikes transplanting.
directions: Direct sow in spring where plants will grow, as rose mallow dislikes transplanting, and expect blooms in about sixty-five days. The lush plants stand two to four feet tall and cover themselves in pink, hibiscus-like flowers that magnetize pollinators. Give full sun and average soil, thinning seedlings for good air movement. It reseeds readily, so leave a few flowers to set seed for next year.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; nick and soak seed to aid germination.
directions: Nick and soak the hard seed, then direct sow after frost at the base of a trellis or fence for these quick-growing heirloom vines. Heart-shaped leaves and pinwheel-patterned blossoms open to greet the morning, with a fresh set of flowers each day. Give full sun and support to climb, and avoid overly rich soil, which favors leaves over blooms. Note that morning glory seeds are toxic if ingested.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring; nick and soak seed to aid germination.
directions: Nick and soak the seed, then direct sow in spring where plants will grow, as lupines resent transplanting. This compact variety stays just twelve to eighteen inches tall with loose eight-inch spikes in pink, blue, purple, and white, some bicolored. Plant in groups for a splash of color in smaller gardens or containers. Provide full sun and well-drained soil, and deadhead spent spikes to prolong bloom.

plant_note: Sow directly; a top pollinator and self-sowing native.
directions: Direct sow plains coreopsis on the soil surface, as light aids germination. This North American native bears masses of yellow daisies banded in mahogany-red that attract bees and butterflies. It tolerates poor, dry soils and full sun, making it excellent for meadows and low-maintenance borders. Allow some seed to ripen for dependable self-sowing, or deadhead to extend the bloom period.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; cover seed as darkness aids germination.
directions: Direct sow in early spring, covering the seed since darkness aids germination and plants tolerate light frost. This dwarf mix averages sixteen to twenty-four inches and blooms in blue, purple, pink, red, and white, making it great for containers. Drought tolerant once established, it flowers from spring into summer and attracts pollinators. Deadhead to extend bloom and let some flowers set seed to self-sow.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 14–16 weeks before last frost after surface-sowing and cold-stratifying at 35–40°F for 3–4 weeks.
directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist, sterile mix and cold-stratify at 35–40°F for 3–4 weeks to break dormancy, then move to 65°F for germination. Grow on at cool temperatures (55–60°F) since heat causes etiolation; transplant into partial shade in humus-rich, moist, well-drained soil in early spring. Primroses bloom best in cool, moist conditions — they are outstanding spring plants but typically go dormant in summer heat. Divide crowded clumps after bloom in spring and replant divisions in refreshed, compost-amended soil.

plant_note: Plant tubers after frost; excellent long-lasting cut flower.
directions: Plant pompon dahlia tubers after the last frost about four to six inches deep in rich, well-drained soil. The small, perfectly spherical, tightly quilled flowers are outstanding long-lasting cut flowers. Grow in full sun, stake the stems, and pinch young plants to encourage more blooms. Deadhead regularly and, in cold zones, dig and store the tubers over winter after the first frost blackens the foliage.

plant_note: Direct sow on the soil surface after last frost when soil is warm; seed needs warmth and light to germinate.
directions: Scatter seed on the surface of sandy or gritty soil and press lightly — do not cover seed, as light is needed for germination. Portulaca is one of the most drought- and heat-tolerant annuals available, thriving in poor, dry soil where other flowers fail. Blooms open in full sun and close at night or in cloudy weather; modern varieties stay open longer. Self-sows readily in warm climates.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost; very slow to establish from seed — divisions or plugs reach ornamental size much faster.
directions: Sow seeds in full sun in lean, well-drained, sandy or rocky soil after last frost; prairie dropseed is a slow-growing native that takes 2–3 years from seed to develop into an ornamental clump. The extremely fine-textured, emerald-green foliage forms a perfect arching fountain shape, and open, airy flower panicles in late summer emit a distinctive fragrance of warm popcorn or cilantro. Fall color is a beautiful tawny orange-gold and the foliage persists attractively through winter. Once established it is extremely drought- and heat-tolerant and virtually maintenance-free.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification; or start indoors in late winter with 8–10 weeks of cold moist stratification.
directions: Sow seeds in fall and allow winter cold to naturally stratify them, or cold-stratify indoors for 8–10 weeks before spring sowing; plant in lean, well-drained to dry soil in full sun. The nodding, burgundy-pink flowers in mid-spring are followed by the spectacular feathery, smoke-like seed plumes that give the plant its name and provide ornamental interest for weeks. Prairie smoke is a rugged, drought-tolerant native wildflower that thrives in rocky or sandy soils where more demanding plants struggle. It spreads slowly into a weed-suppressing groundcover in open, sunny areas.

plant_note: Start indoors 10 weeks early; slow to establish.
directions: Start purple bell vine indoors ten weeks before the last frost, surface sowing the fine seed and providing warmth. This delicate climber produces dangling flowers with rosy-pink parasol-shaped calyces and dark tubular petals. Grow on a light trellis or in hanging baskets in full sun to partial shade with rich, moist soil. It is grown as a tender annual in most climates and blooms until frost.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep; cold stratification for 4–8 weeks improves germination rates significantly. Transplant after last frost into well-drained soil — established plants tolerate drought well. Flowers typically appear in year 2 from seed; deadhead spent blooms to extend the season or leave seedheads for birds. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in spring to maintain vigor.

plant_note: Direct sow after soil warms, or cold stratify seed for 4 weeks to improve germination.
directions: This native heirloom is a long-lived perennial that blooms for a month or more from midsummer, with pinkish-purple petals surrounding a large dark cone. Sow seed in spring after the last frost, or cold stratify beforehand to break dormancy. Space plants a foot or more apart in well-drained soil and full sun to part shade. Once established it is drought tolerant and self-sows. Leave spent cones standing to feed goldfinches through fall and winter.

plant_note: Direct sow after danger of frost has passed; nasturtiums resent transplanting.
directions: This trailing variety carries deeply veined petals in shades of purple fading to lavender and dusty rose, making it ideal for hanging baskets or as a flowering ground cover. Sow the large seeds directly where they are to grow once the soil has warmed, planting about half an inch deep. Avoid rich soil and heavy feeding, which produces leaves at the expense of blooms. It is fairly drought tolerant once established. Both the peppery blossoms and leaves are edible and add a colorful kick to salads.

plant_note: Plant container-grown transplants after last frost; this cultivar does not come true from seed and is propagated by division.
directions: Plant after last frost in full sun and very well-drained soil; purple fountain grass is grown as an annual north of zone 9 for its arching burgundy-red foliage and showy, rose-tinged plumes from midsummer through frost. It thrives in heat and drought once established but requires consistent moisture the first few weeks after transplanting. In frost-free climates it is a perennial that can become invasive — avoid planting near natural areas and remove flower heads before seeds ripen. Bring container-grown specimens indoors before frost to overwinter as a houseplant in colder zones.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; soak seed overnight to speed germination.
directions: This unusual heirloom prized since 16th-century England bears nodding, tear-shaped purple bracts on blue-green foliage that pairs beautifully with bronze and deep-purple plants. Soak seed overnight, then sow directly into the garden in early spring for the strongest plants. It thrives in heat and makes a long-lasting cut flower. Give it well-drained soil and full sun. Plants often self-sow to return the following year.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after all danger of frost has passed.
directions: This blend produces bright, papery globe-shaped blooms in warm sunrise tones that add bold color and unique texture to the summer garden. Start seed indoors about six to eight weeks before the last frost, as germination is slow and heat-loving. Transplant into full sun after the soil warms, where the plants tolerate heat and drought with ease. The more you cut, the more it blooms, making it excellent for fresh and dried arrangements. Pollinators are drawn to the long-lasting flowers all season.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; seed germinates best in cool soil and darkness.
directions: Pure white lacy umbels top plants reaching two feet or more, creating an airy, romantic filler for cut-flower bouquets. Sow seed directly in cool early-spring or fall soil and cover lightly, as it germinates best in darkness. Plants produce one strong flush of blooms beloved by pollinators and beneficial insects. Give them full sun to part shade and well-drained soil. Succession sow for a longer supply of the delicate white flowers.

plant_note: Direct sow after the last frost; safflower has a taproot and dislikes transplanting.
directions: This ornamental safflower opens creamy yellow and matures to deep saffron orange, giving evolving color and thistle-like texture through midsummer. Sow seed directly where plants are to grow, since the taproot resents disturbance. Sturdy upright stems make excellent fresh and dried cut flowers. Full sun and lean, well-drained soil suit it best, and it is quite drought tolerant. The edible petals can be used as a saffron substitute.

plant_note: Direct sow after all danger of frost; zinnias grow quickly in warm soil.
directions: This tall standout carries an extraordinary lime-and-orange color blend never before seen in a zinnia, with long-lasting blooms on sturdy stems. Sow directly into warm soil after frost, or start indoors a few weeks early in short-season areas. Pinch young plants to encourage branching and cut often to prolong bloom. Full sun and good air circulation help prevent powdery mildew. The flowers attract pollinators and make exceptional cut flowers all summer.

plant_note: Direct sow after all danger of frost; zinnias grow quickly in warm soil.
directions: Sturdy plants produce long-stemmed blossoms in a rich lime-and-red blend all summer, drawing in pollinators. Sow seed directly into warm soil after the last frost for fastest growth. Deadhead and cut regularly to keep the plants blooming into fall. Provide full sun and space plants for airflow to reduce mildew. The colorful, edible petals make a pretty garnish for salads and desserts.

plant_note: Start indoors and surface sow; seed needs light to germinate. Transplant after frost.
directions: Grown for its dazzling foliage in white, bronze, gold, copper, yellow, pink, red, and purple, this blend brightens shady spots where flowers struggle. Start seed indoors eight to ten weeks before the last frost, pressing it onto the soil surface since it needs light to sprout. Transplant after all danger of frost into partial shade and rich, moist soil. Pinch out flower spikes to keep the leaves vibrant and the plants bushy. It also makes an excellent houseplant to add color through winter.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring or start indoors; often blooms the first year from an early start.
directions: In late spring a blue-green mound of foliage is covered with heavily fringed, fragrant, edible blooms in white, pink, lilac, and rose that butterflies adore. Sow seed directly in the garden in spring, or start indoors early for first-year flowers. Give plants full sun to part shade and well-drained soil. Shear after the first flush to encourage a repeat bloom. The lacy flowers make charming, sweetly scented cut arrangements.

plant_note: In mild-winter climates (zones 8–10) plant corms in fall for late-winter/spring bloom; in cold climates start indoors and transplant after last frost.
directions: Soak corms in cool water for 4 hours before planting, then set them claw-side down about 2 in deep in well-drained, fertile soil. Ranunculus thrives in cool temperatures (45–65°F) and will decline when heat arrives, so timing is critical. In zones 9–10 plant October through January for blooms from February through May; provide afternoon shade in the warmest areas. After foliage yellows, allow corms to dry in situ or lift, cure, and store in a cool dry location for replanting.

plant_note: Direct sow fresh seed in fall for natural cold stratification; transplants poorly due to deep taproot.
directions: Sow fresh seed in fall directly in a sunny prairie or meadow garden in lean, well-drained soil; rattlesnake master develops a deep taproot and resents being moved after establishment. The dramatic, yucca-like basal rosette of spiny blue-green leaves is topped by tall branched stems bearing small, spiny, globe-shaped white flower heads in midsummer that are highly attractive to native bees. It is extremely drought-tolerant and actually performs better in poor, sandy or rocky soil than in rich garden beds. The sculptural seedheads provide winter interest and are prized for dried arrangements.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors 4-6 weeks early for earlier bloom.
directions: Each medium bloom showcases deep, sultry red petals around a vivid yellow center on compact, deer-resistant plants only six to ten inches tall. Sow directly after the last frost or start indoors a few weeks early for a quicker show. These marigolds thrive in full sun and average, well-drained soil. Deadhead regularly to keep them flowering until frost. Their compact size makes them ideal for edging beds, borders, and containers while drawing in pollinators.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed in spring.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep and maintain even moisture at 65–75°F until germination. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil — good drainage is critical, especially in winter, as plants rot in waterlogged conditions. The torch-like flower spikes transition from red or orange in bud to yellow as the florets open, creating a two-toned effect that attracts hummingbirds throughout summer and fall. In zones 5–6 tie the leaves together and mulch deeply around the crown before the first hard freeze to protect the growing point.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors 4-6 weeks early for earlier bloom.
directions: This color-shifting marigold is deep burgundy in cool weather, then develops splashes of yellow as temperatures warm and reverts to solid burgundy when it cools again. Sow directly after the last frost or start indoors for earlier blooms. Full sun and well-drained soil produce the sturdiest, most colorful plants. Deadhead often to prolong flowering through the season. It makes a striking garden hedge and attracts pollinators.

plant_note: Plant tubers after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: Plant Red Runner tubers 4-6 inches deep once soil warms, laying each tuber horizontally with the eye pointing up. This decorative variety produces dark romantic-red blooms on strong stems well suited for cutting. Pinch the growing tip at 12 inches to encourage bushier plants and more flowers. In zones colder than 8, lift and store the tubers in a cool, dry place over winter. Water deeply once sprouts emerge and avoid overwatering newly planted tubers.

plant_note: Set tubers out after the last spring frost when the soil is warm.
directions: Rip City bears dramatic dark red-black blooms on long, easy-to-cut stems from early in the season. Plant tubers 4-6 inches deep in well-drained soil after frost danger passes, and stake the plants early since the tall stems benefit from support. This variety is a strong tuber producer, so plants can be divided at season's end. Deadhead spent flowers to keep new blooms coming through fall. Lift tubers before hard frost in cold-winter regions.

plant_note: Plant after frost when soil reaches about 60 F.
directions: Robin Hood produces large salmon-orange blooms whose saturation shifts through the growing season, making each flower slightly different. Because the big blooms are heavy, stake or corral this variety early so stems stay upright. Plant tubers 4-6 inches deep in loose, fertile, well-drained soil once frost danger has passed. Feed with a low-nitrogen fertilizer to favor flowers over foliage. Cut blooms freely to encourage continued production.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring; plants usually bloom the second year.
directions: This perennial painted daisy, also called Persian daisy, produces bright long-stemmed blooms that make excellent cut flowers and pair beautifully with columbine and delphinium. Sow seed in spring in full sun to part shade and well-drained soil. Plants typically flower in their second season and attract pollinators. Divide clumps every few years to keep them vigorous. Some people may experience contact dermatitis, so handle the foliage with care.

plant_note: Plant tubers after the last frost when soil has warmed.
directions: Rock Run Ashley is a smaller smoky-salmon dahlia that blends beautifully in mixed bouquets and is a favorite with florists. Plant tubers horizontally 4-6 inches deep after the soil warms, spacing plants about 12-18 inches apart. Pinch young plants to promote branching and more cut stems. Keep soil evenly moist during bloom but let it dry slightly between waterings to prevent tuber rot. Harvest flowers in the cool of the morning for the longest vase life.

plant_note: Plant tubers outdoors once the danger of frost has passed.
directions: Rockstar is a dark anemone-type dahlia with distinctive foliage that blooms continuously through the season and holds well in the vase. Plant the tuber 4-6 inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil after frost, positioning the eye upward. Provide full sun and support tall stems as needed. Deadhead regularly and cut blooms often to keep the plant productive. In cold climates dig and store tubers before the first hard freeze.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; cold stratification improves germination.
directions: The state flower of Colorado, this heirloom columbine bears long-lasting pale violet and white blossoms above delicate blue-green, fern-like foliage. Sow seed in early spring or fall and expose it to a cold period, as chilling greatly improves germination. It is at home in part shade and moist, well-drained soil. Plants bloom in their second year and self-sow readily. Leave a few seed heads to naturalize the patch over time.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall or early spring; seed benefits from cold stratification.
directions: This long-lived heirloom penstemon sends up exquisite blue-violet flower spikes that bloom for about a month in early summer and attract pollinators. Sow seed in fall or early spring and give it a cold, moist period to break dormancy. Provide full sun and lean, sharply drained soil, as it is drought tolerant once established. Evergreen basal rosettes carry the plant through winter. Leave the satiny brown seedpods for interest and self-sowing.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring around last frost date; plants may bloom the first fall from an early sowing or establish and bloom their second year.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in well-drained soil; germination is fast and reliable. Plant in full sun in poor to average, very well-drained soil — rose campion thrives under dry, sunny conditions that would stress many perennials. The intensely magenta-crimson flowers glow against the woolly, silver-white felted stems and leaves, which are ornamental throughout the growing season. Individual plants are short-lived (2–3 years) but self-sow abundantly so colonies persist indefinitely; thin or transplant self-sown seedlings to prevent overcrowding.

plant_note: Direct sow after all danger of frost; zinnias grow quickly in warm soil.
directions: Abundant four- to five-inch rosy pink blooms range from dusty rose to warm pink, evoking an old-fashioned cottage garden when planted in drifts. Sow seed directly into warm soil after the last frost. Cut and deadhead frequently to keep the plants producing into fall. Full sun and good spacing for airflow help avoid powdery mildew. Pollinators, including butterflies, love the long-lasting flowers.

plant_note: Set out nursery plants in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil.
directions: This hardy evergreen shrub produces clusters of soft pink, trumpet-shaped blooms in spring above lush foliage. Plant it in spring or fall in acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil and site it in dappled shade or morning sun. Keep the shallow roots cool and evenly moist with a mulch of pine bark or leaves. Avoid deep planting, as the crown must stay near the surface. Prune lightly right after flowering to shape the plant.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; surface sow as seed needs light to germinate.
directions: This sweetly scented alyssum forms low mats covered in tiny rose-pink flowers in mini-rosettes, perfect for rock gardens, walls, paths, and container edges. Sow seed directly in early spring, pressing it onto the surface since it needs light to germinate. Full sun to light shade and well-drained soil keep it blooming. Shear lightly if it fades in summer heat to spur a fresh flush. The mildly grassy, mustardy flowers are edible and draw in pollinators.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall in mild climates; clarkia dislikes transplanting.
directions: Graceful branching stems carry a profusion of delicate pink, white, and salmon blooms from spring into summer, swaying in the breeze. Sow seed directly where it is to grow, as clarkia resents root disturbance. It prefers cool weather, so sow in early spring or in fall where winters are mild. Give it full sun to part shade and lean, well-drained soil. Pollinators love the flowers, which also last well when cut.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; sunflowers grow best sown where they will bloom.
directions: This stunning true-red sunflower blooms on multi-branching plants, offering a dramatic accent in beds, borders, and bouquets. Sow seed directly into warm soil after frost, spacing plants closer together for longer stems and smaller flowers ideal for cutting. Full sun and steady moisture produce the best display. Cut stems just before the flowers fully open for the longest vase life. Successive sowings extend the bloom season into fall.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; soak seed first.
directions: The Royal series offers a majestic mix of colors with larger flowers and better heat tolerance for a longer bloom period than most sweet peas. Soak the seed, then sow directly in very early spring as soon as the soil can be worked, since they love cool weather. Provide a trellis, fence, or netting for the climbing vines. Keep them well watered and cut frequently to prolong flowering. The fragrant blooms lure pollinators and perfume the house when brought indoors.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in average soil; overly rich soil reduces blooms.
directions: This Fleuroselect award winner bears deeply colored three-inch blooms that open red-wine and age to dusty rose, striking against white flowers and silver foliage. Sow directly after frost into average, well-drained soil, as rich soil produces foliage at the expense of flowers. Full sun and modest water suit it best; it is drought tolerant once established. Deadhead to prolong bloom or leave a few seed heads to self-sow. Butterflies flock to the flowers, which also cut well.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost when soil is warm; can start indoors 3-4 weeks early.
directions: Grown by Thomas Jefferson at Monticello, this ornamental vine dazzles with pinkish-purple flowers and shiny dark-purple pods against purple-tinged foliage. Sow directly into warm soil after frost, providing a sturdy fence or trellis for the fast, twining stems to climb. Full sun brings out the richest pod color. Keep it evenly watered through the heat of summer. Note that the raw pods and seeds are toxic and should be treated as ornamental only.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring or fall; nick or soak the hard seed coat to aid germination.
directions: This 1937 English selection offers an array of colors on tall dramatic spikes that shine when planted in masses or mixed borders. Nick or soak the hard seed coat, then sow directly in spring or fall, as lupines dislike transplanting their long taproot. They prefer full sun, cool summers, and well-drained, slightly acidic soil. In zones 4 to 7 they are short-lived perennials; in warmer areas grow them as annuals. Remove spent spikes to encourage more blooms and support pollinators.

plant_note: Plant in spring after last frost in lean, well-drained soil; established plants are extremely drought-tolerant.
directions: Plant in poor to average, sharply drained soil in full sun — Russian sage declines quickly in fertile or moist soil and is highly susceptible to root rot in clay. Cut plants back hard in early spring (to about 6 in) just as new basal growth emerges; do not cut back in fall as the woody framework helps protect the crown. The airy silver stems and lavender-blue flowers create a haze of color from midsummer through fall and are beloved by pollinators. Deer avoid this plant due to its strongly aromatic foliage.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; surface sow as seed needs light.
directions: This Asian wildflower has an unusual spiky habit and rosy color that adds welcome contrast to the landscape and long-lasting cut and dried flowers. Start seed indoors six to eight weeks before the last frost, pressing it onto the surface for light. Transplant into full sun and well-drained soil after frost. It tolerates heat and dry conditions once established. Harvest stems when most of the tiny flowers have opened for the best dried bouquets.

plant_note: Plant tubers after frost when the soil has warmed.
directions: Salmon Runner is a well-balanced salmon-colored dahlia that produces abundant cut flowers without needing staking. Plant the tuber 4-6 inches deep in loose, fertile soil after the last frost, eye facing up. This sturdy variety stands on its own but still benefits from pinching to increase stem count. Water deeply and consistently once growth is established, and cut blooms often to prolong flowering. Lift and store tubers over winter in regions colder than zone 8.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; light cold stratification aids germination.
directions: This southwestern native perennial reaches up to thirty inches and bears deep red flowers that stand out against rich green foliage in pollinator gardens. Sow seed in early spring or fall and give it a cool period to improve germination. It thrives in moderately dry, well-drained soil and full sun to part shade. Once established it is quite drought tolerant. Deadhead to prolong bloom and leave some seed to naturalize.

plant_note: Direct sow in place; short-lived, so succession sow.
directions: Broadcast scarlet flax seed thinly on prepared soil in early spring and rake in lightly. The brilliant red saucer-shaped flowers dance atop wiry stems and are a favorite in wildflower mixes. Because individual plants are short-lived, make successive sowings for continuous color through the season. It thrives in full sun and lean, well-drained soil with minimal supplemental water.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; tap-rooted and resents transplanting — sow where plants will remain.
directions: Sow seeds directly in well-drained, even poor, sandy or gravelly soil; sea holly develops a deep taproot and strongly dislikes being transplanted after establishment. The metallic steel-blue stems, bracts, and thimble-shaped flower heads appear in midsummer and hold their color and structure well into fall. Drought-tolerant once established, sea holly declines in rich, moist soil — lean, infertile conditions produce the best blue coloration. Cut stems for fresh or dried arrangements before flowers fully open to preserve the vivid blue color.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in average soil; overly rich soil reduces blooms.
directions: These charming cosmos have fluted, seashell-shaped petals in white, pink, rose, and crimson that make an excellent cut flower. Sow seed directly after the last frost into average, well-drained soil, since rich soil reduces flowering. They prefer neglect over pampering and become drought tolerant once established. Deadhead to extend the show or leave seed heads to self-sow. Full sun keeps the tall plants sturdy and blooming into fall.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or fall; division is easily done by digging and separating rooted stems.
directions: Plant in any well-drained soil — even poor, gravelly soil suits sedum perfectly; overly rich or moist soil causes floppy, open-centered plants. Tall sedums may flop in midseason; pinch once in late spring or grow through a support ring to keep them upright. Leave the russet seedheads standing through winter as they are attractive and provide structure in the dormant garden. Divide every 3–4 years in spring by separating the fleshy crown sections.

plant_note: Direct sow after all danger of frost; zinnias grow quickly in warm soil.
directions: This distinguished zinnia produces elegant double three- to five-inch blooms in a dreamy, rare salmon-pink on strong, long stems. Sow directly into warm soil after the last frost for fast growth. Cut and deadhead often to keep the plants flowering all summer. Full sun and airflow between plants help prevent powdery mildew. The edible petals are loved by pollinators and brighten the cutting garden.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in average soil; overly rich soil reduces blooms.
directions: This tall, showy blend produces large three- to four-inch white, pink, rose, and crimson flowers ideal for wildflower plantings and cutting. Sow directly after frost into average, well-drained soil, as lean conditions bring more bloom. Cosmos are drought tolerant and need little attention once up. Full sun keeps the plants upright and flowering; stake in windy sites. An excellent butterfly lure that self-sows for the following year.

plant_note: Start indoors; nick or soak the hard seed coat and keep warm. Transplant after frost.
directions: This novelty rapidly folds its fern-like leaves when touched, exposing spines as a defense, delighting children and adults alike. Nick or soak the hard seed and start it indoors in warmth, then grow on as a houseplant or transplant outdoors after frost. Give it bright light, full sun to part shade, and consistently moist, well-drained soil. It is frost tender and typically grown as an annual or potted plant outside the tropics. Handle sparingly to enjoy repeated leaf-folding without stressing the plant.

plant_note: Direct sow in very early spring or fall; seed germinates best in cool soil and darkness.
directions: Tall spikes of light and dark blue blossoms make a compelling backdrop for beds and bouquets and reseed readily. Sow seed directly in very early spring or in fall, as it needs cool soil and darkness to germinate and dislikes transplanting. Larkspur blooms in the cool weather of late spring and early summer. Provide full sun and well-drained soil, and stake in exposed sites. All parts are toxic if eaten, so site it away from grazing animals.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring or fall; surface sow as seed needs light to germinate.
directions: This spectacular perennial sends up multiple graceful spikes of white, pink, deep rose, violet, and purple flowers that attract butterflies. Sow seed in spring or fall, pressing it onto the soil surface since it needs light to germinate. It is fast growing and fairly drought tolerant, excelling in dry landscape gardens or mass plantings. Give it full sun and sharply drained soil. Cut spent spikes to encourage rebloom and use the flowers in arrangements.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Sow seeds on the surface of moist potting mix and keep at 60–70°F for germination; transplant after the last frost into well-drained soil. Shasta daisies often bloom lightly the first year from seed and fully the second year — named cultivars from division or plugs bloom more reliably in year 1. Deadhead spent flowers to encourage repeat blooming from midsummer into fall. Divide clumps every 2–3 years in spring, discarding the woody center and replanting vigorous outer sections.

plant_note: Surface sow on cool soil; do not cover the fine seed.
directions: Scatter the tiny Shirley poppy seed on the surface of cool, moist soil in very early spring, or sow in fall in mild regions. Do not cover the seed, which needs light and cold to germinate well. The delicate crepe-paper flowers in pink, red, and white are classic cottage and meadow plants. Poppies resent transplanting, so always direct sow, and let some pods ripen for self-seeding.

plant_note: Surface sow in early spring or fall; seed needs light to germinate.
directions: Scatter the tiny seed on the soil surface in early spring or fall, pressing lightly but not covering since it needs light to sprout. This 1880s heirloom bears delicate two-to-three-inch blossoms on slender, nodding stems that dance in the breeze. Poppies resent transplanting, so always direct sow where they are to grow and thin gently. As a carefree annual it readily self-sows, making it ideal for naturalized areas and meadow plantings. Water sparingly once established, as these plants tolerate lean, dry conditions.

common_name: Shock Wave Purple Tie Dye Petunia
plant_note: Start indoors early and surface sow; seed needs light. Transplant after frost.
directions: This petite petunia bears one-and-a-half to two-inch variegated purple flowers with tie-dye patterns that shift with temperature and light, so no two blooms match. Start the tiny seed indoors eight to ten weeks before the last frost, pressing it onto the surface for light. Transplant after frost into full sun and well-drained soil. Its compact, mounded, spreading habit makes it ideal for hanging baskets and containers. Feed regularly and pinch to keep it full and blooming all season.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; sunflowers grow best sown where they will bloom.
directions: Yellow-tipped, bronze-red petals and contrasting purple-tinged stems make this well-branched sunflower a striking cut flower. Sow seed directly into warm soil after frost, spacing closer for longer stems suited to bouquets. The blooms are pollenless, so they will not shed on tabletops. Give the plants full sun and steady moisture. The edible petals add a bittersweet pop of color to salads.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall, or cold stratify seed 4-6 weeks before spring sowing.
directions: This robust heirloom perennial hosts monarch butterflies and draws many pollinators with clusters of star-shaped blooms above green to silvery foliage. Sow seed in fall or cold stratify it for several weeks before spring sowing to break dormancy. It grows best in full sun and well-drained soil and is drought tolerant once established. Its deep roots dislike transplanting, so sow it in place. Leave seed pods to spread the patch and support monarch populations.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring as soon as plants can be purchased; also self-seeds moderately in moist, shaded sites.
directions: Plant in partial to full shade in cool, humus-rich, moist but well-drained soil; brunnera is one of the best foliage plants for shade, particularly the silver-variegated cultivars like 'Jack Frost.' Delicate sprays of forget-me-not blue flowers appear in early to mid-spring above the large, heart-shaped leaves. In hot or dry conditions the leaves may scorch — consistent moisture and afternoon shade are essential in warmer climates. Divide clumps every 4–5 years in early spring; variegated cultivars must be divided rather than grown from seed to maintain their markings.

plant_note: Plant divisions in early spring as growth begins, or in late summer after flowering; set rhizomes just below soil surface.
directions: Plant rhizomes just 1–2 in below the soil surface in moisture-retentive, slightly acidic soil; unlike bearded iris, Siberian iris prefers consistently moist conditions and tolerates wet spots. Graceful, narrow foliage forms attractive clumps all season, topped by elegant blue, purple, or white flowers in early summer. Siberian iris are very long-lived and rarely need dividing — only do so when the center of the clump dies out or blooming diminishes, typically every 5–8 years. Division is best done immediately after flowering or in early fall; replant promptly and water well.

plant_note: Plant small bulbs in fall; naturalizes in lawns.
directions: Plant Siberian squill bulbs in fall about three inches deep in well-drained soil in sun to partial shade. The nodding, intensely blue star-shaped flowers appear very early in spring and provide crucial forage for early bees. It naturalizes freely in lawns and under deciduous trees, forming sheets of blue over the years. Plant in generous drifts and leave the foliage to ripen so the colony can expand.

plant_note: Start indoors and surface sow; seed needs light. Transplant after frost.
directions: Grown for its striking silvery-white, felted foliage, this plant adds contrast and texture as edging, in moon gardens, and as bouquet filler. Start seed indoors eight to ten weeks before the last frost, pressing it onto the surface for light. Transplant after frost into full sun and well-drained soil. It is drought tolerant and may overwinter in mild climates when mulched. If allowed to flower, the yellow blooms attract butterflies.

plant_note: Direct sow after danger of frost has passed; nasturtiums resent transplanting.
directions: This blend blooms in single flowers of scarlet, gold, orange, and yellow on long, trailing stems that can be trained up a trellis or fence. Sow the large seeds directly where they are to grow once the soil warms, planting about half an inch deep. Keep the soil lean, as rich conditions favor leaves over flowers. It is drought tolerant once established and thrives in full sun to part shade. Both the peppery blossoms and leaves are edible and add a watercress-like kick to salads.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in early fall, 4 in deep, in moist but well-drained soil; ideal for naturalizing in damp meadows.
directions: Plant bulbs in early fall, 4 in deep and 3–4 in apart, in organically rich, moisture-retentive but not waterlogged soil; perfect for damp meadow or pond-edge plantings. The nodding, checkerboard-patterned bells in deep purple, white, or pink appear in mid-spring and are among the most intricately patterned flowers in the spring garden. Naturalizes freely in damp grassland — avoid mowing until foliage has died back completely in early summer. Once established the bulbs multiply steadily; divide congested clumps in early fall.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant 2–4 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate light frost.
directions: Press seed onto the surface of moist starting mix and do not cover — seed needs light to germinate. Pinch seedlings once to promote multiple spikes. Transplant to the garden a few weeks before the last frost, as snapdragons thrive in cool conditions and can tolerate a light freeze. After the primary spike blooms, cut it off to encourage a second flush from side branches.

plant_note: Plant tubers after frost has passed and soil is warm.
directions: Snoho Doris is a tall, productive dahlia bearing salmon-coral blooms with uniquely patterned petals on very long, easy-to-harvest stems. Plant tubers 4-6 inches deep in well-drained soil after the last frost, and provide light support for the tall plants. Pinch the central shoot early to encourage more branching and stems. Cut flowers frequently to encourage continued blooming into fall. A great choice for beginning dahlia growers thanks to its reliable production.

plant_note: Plant tubers after the last spring frost when soil warms.
directions: Snoho Jojo is a productive, short-statured dahlia with smoky mauve blooms carried on long stems that are excellent for cutting. Plant tubers 4-6 inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil once frost danger passes. The compact plant produces cut-worthy stems from the start of the season. Deadhead and cut regularly to keep blooms coming through fall. In cold climates, lift the tubers before the first hard freeze and store them in a cool, dry place.

plant_note: Plant corms in fall; naturalizes and resists squirrels.
directions: Plant snow crocus corms in fall about three inches deep in well-drained soil and full to partial sun. This early species crocus naturalizes rapidly in lawns and borders and is more squirrel-resistant than large Dutch crocus. The slender lavender flowers are among the first blooms of late winter, providing vital early forage for bees. Leave the foliage undisturbed until it withers to allow the corms to multiply.

plant_note: Direct sow around the last frost; euphorbia dislikes root disturbance.
directions: Crisp white-edged bracts look hand-painted and give a luminous, frosted glow even in summer heat, brightening beds and contrasting with deeper greens. Sow seed directly around the last frost, since this euphorbia resents transplanting. It thrives in full sun to part shade and average, well-drained soil and tolerates heat and drought. Wear gloves, as the milky sap can irritate skin and eyes. The variegated foliage makes a fine cut accent when the stem ends are seared.

plant_note: Plant small bulbs in fall, 3 in deep; alternatively plant 'in the green' (just after bloom) for better establishment.
directions: Plant bulbs 3 in deep in moisture-retentive, humus-rich soil under deciduous trees or in a shaded border; snowdrops are one of the first bulbs to bloom, often in January or February. They establish most successfully when transplanted 'in the green' — lifted and divided just after blooming while foliage is still active. Clumps multiply steadily and benefit from division every 3–4 years right after flowering to prevent overcrowding. Allow foliage to die back naturally; avoid mowing naturalised areas until leaves have fully yellowed.

plant_note: Direct sow around the last frost, or start indoors 4-6 weeks early.
directions: Delicate ivory pincushion blooms with frilled petals appear continuously from early summer to fall on sturdy two-foot stems ideal for cutting. Sow seed directly around the last frost or start indoors a few weeks early. Provide full sun and well-drained soil and deadhead often to keep the honey-scented flowers coming. Bees and butterflies are drawn to the blooms. The strong stems make it a reliable cut-flower workhorse.

common_name: Something Old Something New Morning Glory
plant_note: Direct sow after frost when soil is warm; nick or soak the hard seed coat first.
directions: This blend pairs nostalgic favorites like sky-blue Heavenly Blue with newer starred varieties for a mix of climbing morning glory flowers. Nick or soak the hard seed, then sow directly into warm soil after frost near a fence or trellis for the twining vines to climb. Give the plants full sun and average soil; overly rich soil favors foliage over flowers. Water regularly to keep the vines vigorous through summer. Note the seeds are toxic if eaten, so keep them away from children and pets.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; naturalizes readily in shade.
directions: Plant Spanish bluebell bulbs in fall about four inches deep in moist, well-drained woodland soil in partial shade. The upright stems carry many bell-shaped blue, pink, or white flowers in mid to late spring. It naturalizes vigorously beneath trees and shrubs and can spread aggressively, so site it thoughtfully. Allow the foliage to yellow and die back naturally to feed the bulbs for next season.

plant_note: Soak and nick seed; start indoors 6 weeks early.
directions: Nick and soak the hard seeds overnight, then start Spanish flag indoors six weeks before the last frost. The vigorous vine produces one-sided sprays of tubular flowers that shade from scarlet through orange to creamy yellow. Provide a strong trellis in full sun and fertile, well-drained soil, transplanting after all frost danger passes. Hummingbirds and butterflies flock to the blooms, which continue until first frost.

plant_note: Sow after frost danger passes in warm, well-drained soil.
directions: This South African annual thrives in hot, dry sites and forms a low, spreading carpet of succulent foliage topped with neon 1.5-inch daisy-like blooms. Direct sow into fast-draining soil after the last frost, barely covering the fine seed and keeping it lightly moist until germination. Give plants full sun; flowers open in bright light and close at night or under clouds. Space plants so they can knit together along borders, between pavers, or in rock gardens. Avoid overwatering, as soggy roots quickly rot in this drought-loving species.

plant_note: Surface sow in early spring; seed benefits from cool soil and light to germinate.
directions: Sparky produces charming double and semi-double blooms in warm apricot tones with burgundy undersides on compact 8-to-10-inch plants. Scatter the tiny seed on the soil surface in early spring, pressing it in without covering since it needs light to sprout. Poppies resent transplanting, so direct sow where they are to grow and thin seedlings to prevent crowding. Drought-tolerant and deer-resistant once established, this variety draws bees and butterflies to the garden. Let a few blooms go to seed and it will often self-sow for the following year.

plant_note: Sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; tolerates cool weather.
directions: This vigorous, late-flowering Spencer type bears fragrant ivory ruffled blooms 1 to 2 inches wide that resemble delicate confections. Sow seed directly in early spring in rich, well-drained soil, providing a trellis or netting for the climbing vines to grab. Nicking or soaking the hard seed coat overnight speeds germination. Keep roots cool and evenly moist, and harvest flowers often to keep the plants producing through the season. Its slightly shorter stems and late bloom time extend the sweet pea season for intimate bouquets.

plant_note: Sow in early spring as soon as soil is workable; likes cool roots.
directions: Princess Elizabeth blends peachy salmon and soft cream tones in romantic, lightly fragrant blooms ideal for cottage-style beds and vertical gardens. Direct sow in early spring in cool, fertile soil, soaking or scarifying the seed first to improve sprouting. Set up a sturdy trellis for the vigorous vines and keep the soil consistently moist. Frequent cutting encourages the plant to produce armfuls of blooms over a longer window. Plant in full sun with cool roots, mulching to buffer summer heat.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring around last frost date; cold stratification for 4 weeks improves germination of seed.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds for 4 weeks before sowing; established divisions establish more reliably than seed-grown plants and can be done in spring or fall. Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, average to rich soil; plants tolerate wet conditions and make excellent rain garden plants. The three-petaled flowers in purple, blue, pink, or white open only for a single morning but are produced in rapid succession over many weeks. Shear the entire plant to 8 in after the main bloom flush to stimulate fresh foliage and a second round of flowers in fall.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Spring Green is an eye-catching celosia standing about 40 inches tall with fresh lime-green plumes that thrive through late summer heat. Start seed indoors 6 to 4 weeks before the last frost, barely covering it and keeping soil warm around 75 degrees F for best germination. Transplant into full sun after frost danger passes, spacing plants for good airflow. The long, sturdy stems make excellent fresh or dried cut flowers and attract pollinators. Deadhead or harvest regularly to keep the display coming through the growing season.

plant_note: Start indoors in cool conditions; transplant in early spring or fall.
directions: Midnight Glow features rich velvet-purple petals with glowing yellow centers that fade into deep purple edges, producing large 3-inch blooms on uniform, well-branched plants. Start seed indoors in cool conditions and dark until germination, then grow on in bright, cool light before transplanting. Pansies thrive in cool, short-day weather, making them ideal for early spring and fall displays. Set plants in full sun to part shade in rich, moist soil and deadhead to prolong bloom. The edible flowers make a colorful garnish for salads and desserts.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; foliage smells of onion when crushed.
directions: Plant spring starflower bulbs in fall about three inches deep in well-drained soil in full sun to light shade. The fragrant, star-shaped, pale blue flowers appear over grassy foliage that smells faintly of onion when bruised. It naturalizes readily in mild climates, forming spreading clumps in rock gardens and borders. Keep the site dry during summer dormancy and divide crowded clumps every few years.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; huge seed heads dry beautifully.
directions: Plant star of Persia bulbs in fall about six inches deep in sharply drained soil and full sun. Each stem bears an enormous globe up to ten inches across of metallic star-shaped violet florets in late spring. The dried seed heads persist for weeks and are prized for arrangements. Site it in a hot, dry spot and let the bulbs bake during summer dormancy to ensure reliable return.

plant_note: Direct sow; drought tolerant and heat loving.
directions: Direct sow star of the veldt in warm, well-drained soil after frost danger passes. This South African annual bears glowing orange and apricot daisies that open in bright sun and close on cloudy days. It thrives in heat and lean soil with minimal water, making it excellent for dry sunny beds and containers. Deadhead spent blooms and avoid overwatering, which can cause rot.

plant_note: Direct sow around the last frost in full sun.
directions: Starflower opens delicate pale blue and white blooms, but its real magic is the spherical seedhead covered in papery, striped cups each cradling a five-pointed dark star. Direct sow in spring around the last frost, lightly covering the seed and keeping it evenly moist until seedlings emerge. Grow in full sun to part shade in average, well-drained soil. Cut blooms for fresh arrangements or leave them to mature into the ornamental seedheads prized for dried bouquets and crafts. The flowers draw bees and butterflies through summer.

plant_note: Direct sow after all frost danger passes in warm soil.
directions: State Fair Mix is a pollinator magnet producing immense flowers dominated by reds and purples with orange, pink, white, and yellow scattered throughout. Direct sow after the last frost in warm soil and full sun, thinning seedlings to give these tall plants room to branch. Zinnias grow quickly and bloom heavily until fall frost when kept deadheaded or cut. The long, sturdy stems make outstanding cut flowers for arrangements. Provide good airflow and water at the base to reduce foliar disease.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant 2–3 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate mild frost.
directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep in moist starting mix; seeds come in a papery casing and germination is slow. Statice prefers cool conditions early in the season. Harvest flower stems for drying when 75% of the florets on each branch have opened. The papery calyces — which hold their color dried — are the ornamental part; the true petals are tiny and brief.

plant_note: Seed benefits from cold stratification; transplant seedlings in spring.
directions: Sea holly brings striking architectural form with steel-blue thistle-like flower heads and spiny bracts that shimmer in moonlight. Seed germinates best after a period of cold, moist stratification, so chill sown flats or sow outdoors in late fall. Transplant carefully, as the deep taproot dislikes disturbance, into lean, well-drained soil in full sun. Drought-tolerant once established, it blooms profusely through summer and provides winter interest with long-standing seedheads. Cut stems dry beautifully for everlasting arrangements.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant 2–4 weeks before last frost as young plants tolerate light frost.
directions: Sow seed on the surface of moist starting mix — light aids germination, so cover with only the thinnest layer of vermiculite if at all. Stock thrives in cool weather (55–65°F nights) and the fragrance is intensified by cool temperatures. Transplant to the garden in early spring; plants begin to decline when daytime temperatures consistently exceed 80°F. Cut flowers when one-third of the buds on the spike are open for the longest vase life.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost after cold-stratifying for 4–6 weeks; transplant after last frost.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds in moist medium at 35–40°F for 4–6 weeks before sowing indoors; germination is slow but improved by stratification. Plant in full sun in well-drained, sandy or loamy soil; Stokes' aster is native to the American Southeast and tolerates heat and humidity well but dislikes wet winter conditions. The large, cornflower-like blooms in blue, lavender, or white appear from early summer to fall and are excellent for cutting. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in spring to maintain vigor; plants spread slowly and are well-behaved in the border.

plant_note: Biennial; sow in spring or summer for bloom the following year.
directions: Canterbury bells, also called bellflower, is an elegant, tall biennial that makes a dramatic backdrop for shorter annuals and perennials. Surface sow the fine seed, which needs light to germinate, and grow seedlings on before transplanting into rich, moist, well-drained soil. As a biennial, plants form a leafy rosette the first year and send up their cup-and-saucer bloom spikes the second season into early summer. Site in full sun to part shade and stake tall stems in windy spots. The nectar-rich flowers attract bees and other pollinators.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed.
directions: Sow seed on the surface of moist starting mix and press lightly — light aids germination, so barely cover seed. Strawflowers tolerate drought and poor soils remarkably well. For drying, harvest stems when the central button of each head is still closed, before the outer bracts fully open; they will continue to open as they dry. Deadhead garden plants regularly to maintain continuous bloom.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost danger in warm soil and full sun.
directions: Summer Lovin bears a mix of creamy lemon-tipped petals with burgundy or strawberry-pink centers, often several color variations on a single plant. Direct sow the large seed after the last frost in warm, well-drained soil and full sun, planting about an inch deep. The tall, branching stems are pollenless and stiff, perfect for clean cut-flower bouquets. Thin seedlings to give plants room and stake if needed in exposed sites. Harvest often to encourage more blooms across the summer.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; tolerates wet soils.
directions: Plant summer snowflake bulbs in fall about four inches deep in moist, humus-rich soil in sun to partial shade. Despite its name it blooms in mid to late spring, bearing nodding white bells tipped with green on arching stems. Unusually among bulbs it tolerates heavy, wet soils and naturalizes along ponds and in damp borders. Leave the strappy foliage to ripen fully, and the clumps will slowly increase over the years.

plant_note: Fall sow or cold-stratify seed; taproot resents transplanting.
directions: Sundial lupine, also called wild lupine, is a heirloom perennial native to the eastern U.S. and a vital host plant for the Karner blue butterfly. Scarify or cold-stratify the hard seed and direct sow in fall or early spring, since the deep taproot dislikes transplanting. Grow in full sun in lean, sandy, well-drained soil where it naturalizes in wildflower gardens and meadows. Its blue flower spikes appear in late spring to early summer and draw many pollinators. Avoid rich soil and excess water, which this drought-tolerant native does not need.

plant_note: Direct sow at or just after last frost when soil is warm; succession sow every 2–3 weeks for extended bloom.
directions: Direct sow seed 1 in deep after the last frost date, thinning to final spacing once seedlings reach 6 in tall.
