Sunflowers prefer poor-to-average soil and resent transplanting due to a taproot. Single-stemmed types bloom once; branching varieties produce multiple heads if cut regularly. Harvest seed heads when the back of the head turns yellow-brown and seeds feel firm.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; soak or nick seed to speed germination.
directions: Sunrise Blend offers a colorful assortment of trumpet blooms that flutter against vigorous heart-shaped foliage from midsummer to the first fall frost. Soak or nick the hard seed coat, then direct sow after the last frost near a trellis, fence, or arbor for the climbing vines to scale. The flowers open in early morning and may stay open on cloudy or cool days. Grow in full sun in average soil, avoiding overly rich ground that favors leaves over flowers. Note that the seeds are poisonous if eaten, so keep them away from children and pets.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; loves heat and dry soil.
directions: Sunshine Blend gazania is a heat- and drought-loving groundcover in warm colors from yellow to mauve, with lush low foliage and blooms held high on their stems. Start seed indoors 6 to 4 weeks before the last frost, covering lightly since darkness aids germination, then transplant after frost danger passes. Grow in full sun in sandy, well-drained soil where the daisy-like petals unfurl in sunshine and close under clouds. Water sparingly once established, as this tough plant tolerates drought and poor soil. Deadhead to keep the color coming through summer.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil and full sun.
directions: Sunspot delivers large sunflower heads on surprisingly dwarf 18-to-24-inch stalks, making it a favorite for kids and small gardens. Direct sow the seed about an inch deep after the last frost in warm soil and full sun. Thin seedlings so each plant has room to develop its outsized bloom. The short stature makes dramatic cut flowers and container plantings, and the heads later provide seeds for birds. Provide steady moisture while young and full sun throughout the season.

plant_note: Start indoors in darkness; transplant after frost.
directions: Salpiglossis, or painted tongue, produces velvety, trumpet-shaped flowers with striking metallic coloration and intricate veining in a range of jewel tones. Start seed indoors 8 to 6 weeks before the last frost, covering it fully since the seed needs darkness to germinate, and keep soil warm. Transplant after frost into rich, well-drained soil in full sun with shelter from strong wind. Pinch young plants to encourage branching and stake the somewhat lax stems. Deadhead regularly and enjoy the blooms as long-lasting cut flowers.

plant_note: Start indoors 6 weeks early; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Start Swan River daisy indoors about six weeks before the last frost for earliest bloom. The dainty, fragrant daisies in blue, mauve, and white cover mounding plants ideal for edging, containers, and hanging baskets. Provide full sun and well-drained soil, avoiding overly rich conditions that soften growth. Shear lightly after the first flush and feed lightly to keep the plant blooming through summer.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; alyssum tolerates light frost and prefers cool weather.
directions: Scatter seed on the soil surface and press gently — light aids germination; just barely cover if needed to prevent the seed from blowing away. Sweet alyssum may slow or stop blooming in peak summer heat; shear plants back by half and they will rebound strongly when temperatures cool in early fall. The honey-scented flowers attract beneficial insects throughout the season.

plant_note: Direct sow outdoors 4–8 weeks before last frost; seeds prefer cool soil (55–65°F) and young plants tolerate frost.
directions: Nick or soak seeds overnight before sowing 1 in deep in cool, enriched soil at the base of a trellis. Sweet peas perform best during cool weather and decline as summer heat arrives — sow as early as possible. Cut flowers frequently, as even a few fading blooms left on the plant will trigger seed set and halt blooming. Provide sturdy support from the start, as vines climb to 4–6 ft.

plant_note: Sow in spring or summer; often blooms the second year as a biennial.
directions: Sweet Rose Magic delights with flowers that open white, shift to soft pink, and mature into rich rose, creating a tricolor display on a single stem. Start seed indoors and barely cover it, or sow outdoors in late spring, transplanting into rich, well-drained soil. As a biennial it typically forms foliage the first year and blooms the second, though it may flower the first season from an early start. Grow in full sun to part shade and deadhead for repeat bloom. The clustered flowers are lightly clove-scented and excellent for cutting.

plant_note: Start indoors 6 weeks early for cut flowers.
directions: Start sweet scabious indoors about six weeks before the last frost, or direct sow once soil warms. The domed pincushion flowers in deep maroon, pink, and white are long-lasting cut flowers and pollinator magnets. Grow in full sun and fertile, well-drained, neutral to alkaline soil, staking tall stems if needed. Harvest or deadhead frequently to keep the plants producing new blooms all summer.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost; does not transplant well due to taproot.
directions: Sow seeds directly in a sunny, well-drained location in early spring; thin to 9–12 in apart as seedlings emerge. The fragrant, fringed, powder-puff flowers in shades of pink, purple, yellow, and white are excellent for cutting, with a pleasant, musky sweet scent. Plants prefer cool weather and will begin to decline as summer heat intensifies — make a second sowing in late summer for fall bloom in warm climates. Successive sowings every 3–4 weeks in spring extend the flowering season through early summer.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant can go out 2–4 weeks before last frost as plants are frost-tolerant.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in well-drained germination mix; germination is fast and reliable at 65–70°F. Plant in full sun in well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline soil; sweet William is technically a biennial that flowers in its second year, but modern annual varieties bloom the first year from an early indoor start. The domed, fragrant flower clusters in vivid shades of red, pink, white, and bicolor appear in late spring and early summer. Deadhead spent clusters promptly to extend the blooming period; plants self-sow readily to maintain a colony from year to year.

plant_note: Start indoors in cool, dark conditions; transplant in early spring or fall.
directions: This Swiss heirloom is the parent of most modern pansies, producing low, bushy plants covered in showy, large, velvety flowers. Start seed indoors in cool conditions and darkness, then grow seedlings on in bright light before transplanting in early spring or fall. Pansies thrive in cool weather and make a fine companion to spring bulbs. Grow in full sun to part shade in rich, moist soil and deadhead to extend bloom. The edible petals garnish soft cheeses and baked goods.

plant_note: Surface sow indoors; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Strawflowers live up to their name, with stiff, papery bracts that look like colored straw and never wilt, making them the classic everlasting cut flower. Surface sow the seed indoors since light aids germination, then transplant after frost into full sun and well-drained soil. Swiss Giants produce extra-large double blooms in bright shades on long, sturdy stems. Cut flowers just before they fully open and hang them upside down to dry for lasting arrangements. Deadhead spent blooms to keep plants flowering, and enjoy the pollinators they attract.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost in spring; warm soil (above 60°F) promotes best germination.
directions: Sow seeds in full sun after last frost in average to poor, well-drained soil; switchgrass is extremely adaptable and tolerates clay, sand, wet or dry conditions, and even brief flooding — a true workhorse native grass. The airy, branching panicles of tiny seeds in late summer create a misty, luminous effect, and many cultivars develop spectacular red, burgundy, or orange fall color. Cut back to 4–6 in in late winter; divide every 3–4 years in spring when the clump center dies out. Named cultivars like 'Shenandoah' and 'Prairie Fire' offer predictably strong red fall color.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; seed needs darkness to germinate.
directions: Bachelor's button, also known as cornflower, is a drought-tolerant, low-maintenance annual with flowers great for cutting or drying and edible petals for garnishing salads. Direct sow in early spring, covering the seed since it needs darkness to germinate, and thin seedlings once up. Grow in full sun in average, well-drained soil. This tall blend self-sows readily, naturalizing for another crop of flowers the following year. Deadhead or harvest often to prolong the bloom period and support pollinators.

plant_note: Surface sow indoors; snapdragons tolerate cool weather when hardened off.
directions: This tall blend of snapdragons adds classic charm to gardens and cut-flower arrangements, and its blooms are a bee magnet as bumblebees climb right into the mouths. Surface sow the tiny seed indoors since light aids germination, and grow seedlings on before transplanting into rich, well-drained soil. Snapdragons prefer cool weather, so set out hardened plants a bit before the last frost and provide late-afternoon shade in hot regions. Pinch young plants to encourage branching and stake the tall spikes if needed. The edible flowers make a pretty garnish, and deadheading prolongs the display.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost after cold-stratifying for 1–2 weeks; often blooms from seed the first year.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds in moist towels for 1–2 weeks at 35–40°F, then sow indoors at 65–75°F; germination remains erratic over 14–28 days. Plant in full sun in poor to average, well-drained soil; this South American native is drought-tolerant and naturalizes readily once established. The wiry, branching stems carry clusters of small purple flowers from midsummer through frost at an unusual height that allows it to weave transparently through other plants. Grown as an annual north of zone 7, it self-sows prolifically even in cold climates and seedlings replace killed parent plants.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; thrives in heat.
directions: Direct sow tassel flower after the last frost in warm, well-drained soil. The small brush-like scarlet and orange flowers rise on wiry stems above tidy rosettes, adding fine texture to arrangements. It thrives in heat and full sun and blooms freely all summer with little care. Deadhead to prolong flowering and allow occasional self-sowing for future plants.

plant_note: Plant in fall; includes paperwhites for forcing.
directions: Plant tazetta daffodil bulbs in fall six inches deep in well-drained soil, or force paperwhite types indoors in pebbles and water. These clustered, intensely fragrant narcissi are well suited to mild-winter gardens and bloom early. Give them full sun and let the foliage die back naturally outdoors. Forced bulbs are generally discarded after flowering, while garden bulbs naturalize in suitable zones.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil and full sun.
directions: Teddy Bear produces fluffy, bright-yellow puffball blooms 3 to 5 inches across on a compact, multi-branching plant 24 to 36 inches tall. Direct sow the seed about an inch deep after the last frost in warm soil and full sun. Thin seedlings to give each plant room to branch and form its many pompom flowers. It works beautifully as a short hedge and makes a fabulous, long-lasting cut flower. Water steadily while young and enjoy the double blooms all summer.

plant_note: Plant tubers outdoors after the last frost when the soil is warm.
directions: Terracotta produces romantic terracotta-bronze blooms with wavy petals on long stems, making it an outstanding cut flower and a strong tuber producer. Plant tubers 4-6 inches deep in well-drained, fertile soil once frost has passed, laying them horizontally with the eye up. Provide full sun and stake tall plants to keep the long cutting stems upright. Cut flowers frequently and deadhead to encourage continuous bloom into fall. Dig and store the tubers before hard frost in cold climates.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; seed germinates in darkness.
directions: The Bride stands out among bachelor's buttons for its unusual snowy-white, frilly blooms and captivating honey fragrance. Direct sow in early spring, covering the seed since darkness aids germination, and thin the seedlings once established. Grow in full sun in average, well-drained soil where this drought-tolerant annual needs little care. Frequent cutting or deadheading keeps the plants producing their pollinator-friendly flowers, which draw bees and butterflies. Leave a few flowers to set seed and it will often self-sow.

plant_note: Plant the potted or bare-root shrub in spring after frost danger passes.
directions: The Champion Sunblush is an easy-care flowering rose shrub with brilliant, sun-kissed petals suited to both beginner and experienced gardeners. Set the plant in full sun in rich, well-drained soil, digging a hole wide enough for the roots and watering in well. Provide consistent moisture at the base to keep foliage dry, and mulch to conserve water and moderate soil temperature. Prune in late winter to shape the shrub and remove dead or crossing canes, and feed in spring as growth begins. Hardy in zones 5 to 9, it rewards steady care with repeat blooms through the season.

plant_note: Plant nursery stock in spring after the ground thaws; can also plant in early fall.
directions: The Rising Sun redbud is a small deciduous tree prized for new growth that emerges in fiery apricot, orange, and gold before aging to lime and green. Site it in rich, well-drained soil where it receives morning sun and some afternoon shade in hot climates. Rosy-pink pea-like flowers cover the bare branches in early spring before the leaves unfurl. Water deeply during the first two years to establish the root system, then supplement only in dry spells. Prune lightly after flowering to shape the canopy.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring or summer; biennial that blooms the second year.
directions: The Watchman appears nearly black from a distance but reveals a rich purplish-burgundy cast up close on towering flower spikes. Direct sow the seed in spring or summer, barely covering it, since this biennial usually forms a rosette the first year and blooms the second. Grow in full sun in rich, well-drained soil and stake the tall spikes in windy locations. The dramatic towers make an excellent backdrop for pink, red, yellow, or white flowers and attract bees and butterflies. Let some flowers set seed and it will readily self-sow for future years.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall; space 5-6 ft apart for a solid privacy screen.
directions: Thuja Green Giant is a fast-growing evergreen conifer widely used as a living privacy hedge, adding several feet of dense, rich green foliage each year. Plant in full sun to partial shade in moist, well-drained soil, spacing plants 5 to 6 feet apart for a continuous screen. Water regularly the first season to establish deep roots; once mature it tolerates heat, humidity, and brief drought. Its naturally pyramidal habit rarely needs pruning, though it takes shearing well if a formal shape is desired. It resists deer browsing and most pests.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in a sunny, well-drained spot.
directions: Thuja Virginian is a compact arborvitae hybrid that offers the dense evergreen screening of Green Giant in a more manageable size for smaller yards. Plant in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil and water consistently through the first growing season to establish. Its tight, upright pyramidal form makes an excellent privacy hedge or specimen without heavy pruning. Mature plants tolerate heat and humidity and stand up to deer better than many evergreens. Mulch the root zone to conserve moisture and moderate soil temperature.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil and full sun.
directions: Thumbelina is a charming dwarf zinnia and 1963 All-America Selections winner, producing abundant cheerful blooms in a wide range of colors on compact plants. Direct sow after the last frost in warm soil and full sun, thinning seedlings for good airflow. Its small stature suits containers, edging, and the front of borders. Deadhead spent blooms to keep flowers coming through summer and into fall. Water at the base to reduce foliar disease, and enjoy the pollinators the flowers attract.

plant_note: Plant after the last frost in spring in a hot, sunny location.
directions: Thunderstruck Coral Boom is a fast-growing crape myrtle that produces large panicles of vivid coral-pink blooms from early summer into fall. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil; more sun means more flowers and better mildew resistance. Its multi-season interest includes attractive exfoliating bark in winter and colorful fall foliage. Prune in late winter to remove crossing branches and shape the canopy, avoiding heavy topping. Water regularly while establishing, after which it becomes notably heat- and drought-tolerant.

plant_note: Plant after the last frost in a warm, sunny spot.
directions: Thunderstruck Rumblin' Red is a crape myrtle celebrated for its fiery red summer blooms and strong year-round interest. Site it in full sun in well-drained soil, where abundant light maximizes flowering and helps prevent powdery mildew. The dark-tinged foliage and smooth exfoliating bark add appeal beyond the long bloom season. Prune lightly in late winter to maintain a tree-like structure rather than topping it hard. Once established it is quite tolerant of heat and dry conditions, needing supplemental water only in extended droughts.

plant_note: Sow in fall or early spring; a reliable wildflower for meadows.
directions: Scatter tidy tips seed on cool, moist soil in fall or early spring and rake in lightly. This California native produces cheerful yellow daisies tipped in white that attract native bees and butterflies. It thrives on lean soil with minimal water once established, making it ideal for wildflower meadows and drought-tolerant plantings. Allow some flowers to set seed for reliable self-sowing in future seasons.

plant_note: Plant bulbs after all frost danger passes in spring, once soil has warmed above 60°F.
directions: Plant bulbs 3–4 in deep in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; each exotic three-petaled flower lasts only one day but new buds open in succession over weeks. The vivid blooms come in yellow, orange, red, pink, and white, typically with a heavily spotted or blotched center cup. Plant in groups of a dozen or more for a showy display from midsummer into fall. In zones colder than 8, dig bulbs after first frost, dry thoroughly, and store in paper bags in a cool, frost-free location.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; propagates from stem bulbils.
directions: Plant tiger lily bulbs in fall six to eight inches deep in rich, moist, well-drained soil in full sun to light shade. The recurved orange flowers heavily spotted with black nod in mid to late summer atop tall sturdy stems. This vigorous heirloom produces small dark bulbils in the leaf axils that can be planted to grow new bulbs. It is a known carrier of viruses, so plant it well away from other prized lilies.

plant_note: Plant in spring or early fall in a spot with morning sun and afternoon shade.
directions: Tilt-A-Swirl is a bigleaf hydrangea whose mophead blooms shift through pink, red, and green as the season progresses, with flower color influenced by soil pH. Plant in rich, moist, well-drained soil where it gets morning sun and shade during the hottest part of the day. Keep the soil consistently moist, especially in summer, as this species wilts quickly when dry. It blooms on old and new wood, so avoid heavy pruning; simply remove spent flowers and dead stems in late winter. Amend soil with aluminum sulfate for bluer tones or lime for pinker ones.

plant_note: Surface sow in early spring; seed needs light to germinate.
directions: Tiny Tim is a compact, honey-scented sweet alyssum that stays covered in tiny flowers all season with no need to shear back. Surface sow the seed in early spring since light aids germination, pressing it into the soil without covering. Grow in full sun to part shade in average, well-drained soil where it forms a fragrant, spreading groundcover. It withstands heat and drought while drawing beneficial insects and pollinators. Use it to edge beds, tuck between pavers, or spill from containers and hanging baskets.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil; ideal for containers or small beds.
directions: Tiny Tower is a very slow-growing dwarf Alberta spruce that keeps a naturally dense, narrow pyramidal shape without shearing, making it perfect for small gardens, entryways, and containers. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and provide good air circulation to discourage spider mites, its main pest. Water regularly the first two seasons and mulch to keep roots cool and moist. In hot climates give it some afternoon shade to prevent needle scorch. Its compact evergreen form also serves as a living holiday tree.

plant_note: Plant divisions in early spring as growth begins; they establish quickly and bloom in their first season from divisions.
directions: Plant in humus-rich, consistently moist, well-drained shade; toad lily is an elegant late-season bloomer that produces exotic-looking, orchid-like flowers in white, purple, or lilac with dark spots from late summer through fall. The arching stems with clasping leaves create a graceful, architectural presence throughout the season. Consistent moisture is essential — plants decline in dry soil; mulch heavily to maintain soil moisture and temperature. Divide every 3–4 years in early spring to manage the slowly spreading clumps.

plant_note: Start indoors; transplant after frost into part shade.
directions: Tom Thumb Blend is a compact, shade-loving balsam standing 12 to 16 inches tall with bushy plants that come alive with violet, white, and rose double flowers. Start seed indoors and barely cover it, keeping soil warm, then transplant after frost into part shade and rich, moist soil. Bumblebees and hummingbirds are drawn to the array of blooms tucked along the stems. Keep the soil consistently moist, as balsam wilts quickly when dry. Its tidy size makes a charming centerpiece for shady beds and containers.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil; thrives in heat.
directions: Torch is a genuine heat lover, setting the dog days of summer ablaze with sizzling 3-inch orange flowers on a large, multi-branching plant for the back of the border. Direct sow after the last frost in warm soil and full sun, barely covering the seed. It tolerates infertile soil, drought, and neglect, so avoid overwatering and rich ground. The tall stems benefit from spacing for airflow and may need staking in exposed sites. Its nectar-rich blooms are irresistible to butterflies and other pollinators.

plant_note: Plant in spring in moist soil; tolerates wet sites well.
directions: The Tri-Color Dappled Willow is a fast-growing deciduous shrub whose new spring growth emerges in variegated green, white, and soft pink, with coral-red stems adding winter interest. Plant in full sun to partial shade in moist, fertile soil; it thrives in damp spots where many shrubs struggle. Prune hard in late winter to encourage a fresh flush of the brightest variegated foliage. Water generously, as willows are thirsty plants that dislike drying out. Grow it as a rounded shrub in a mixed border or shear it into a formal shape.

plant_note: Plant in spring in moist, fertile soil; tolerates wet ground.
directions: This is the standard, tree-form version of the tri-color dappled willow, grafted atop a single trunk to create a lollipop-shaped canopy of green, white, and pink variegated leaves. Plant in full sun to light shade in consistently moist, fertile soil where it can show off its cascading foliage. Prune the head in late winter to keep it dense and to renew the most colorful new growth. Keep the soil evenly moist, as willows demand regular water and tolerate wet feet. Remove any suckers that sprout below the graft to preserve the tree form.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall 6-8 inches deep before ground freezes.
directions: Plant Triumph tulip bulbs in fall about six to eight inches deep and four to six inches apart in well-drained soil. This mid-season class offers sturdy stems and classic egg-shaped blooms in a vast color range, excellent for beds and cutting. Provide full sun and allow the foliage to yellow naturally after bloom to recharge the bulb. In warm climates pre-chill bulbs for ten to fourteen weeks before planting, or treat them as annuals.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; in zone 8–11 it acts as a short-lived perennial and blooms from spring through hard frost.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in warm starting mix and transplant after frost danger has passed; tropical milkweed establishes quickly and blooms in its first season unlike most native milkweeds. It is an important monarch host plant and nectar source throughout the Gulf Coast growing season, but in zone 9–11 where it does not freeze back, cut plants to 6 in in fall and winter to disrupt the overwinter buildup of Ophryocystis elektroscirrha (OE), a protozoan parasite that accumulates on non-dormant milkweed. Deadhead spent flower clusters to encourage continuous blooming, or allow seedpods to dry and open for easy self-sowing. The red-and-yellow bicolor flowers distinguish it from native milkweeds at a glance.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring; never let dry out.
directions: Plant trumpet lily bulbs in fall or early spring about eight inches deep in deep, fertile, well-drained soil. The tall stems carry many large, powerfully fragrant trumpet-shaped flowers in midsummer and often need staking. Give them full sun with cool, shaded roots and never allow the bulbs to dry out completely. Leave stems standing until they yellow so the bulbs can store energy for the following year.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall when soil temperatures drop below 55°F, about 4–8 weeks before hard frost.
directions: Plant bulbs pointed end up at a depth of 3 times their height, typically 6–8 in deep, in well-drained soil — standing water will cause rot. In warmer zones (7–8), pre-chill bulbs for 6–8 weeks in the refrigerator before planting to simulate the cold period they need. Most hybrid tulips perform best in year 1; replanting fresh bulbs annually is common practice in mild climates. Allow leaves to die back naturally after bloom to recharge the bulb.

plant_note: Plant in spring in deep, moist, well-drained soil with room to grow large.
directions: The tulip poplar is a fast-growing native shade tree that reaches towering heights, with distinctive four-lobed leaves and cup-shaped, greenish-yellow tulip-like flowers in late spring. Give it a large, open site in full sun with deep, moist, well-drained soil, as its extensive root system needs room. Water regularly for the first few years to establish; mature trees are fairly self-sufficient. Its rapid growth makes it excellent for quick shade, though the soft wood benefits from a sheltered location. Foliage turns clear golden-yellow in autumn.

plant_note: Plant container stock after last frost; an extremely tough Texas native that thrives in heat, part shade, and dry soils where few flowering shrubs will grow.
directions: Plant in well-drained to average soil in partial to full sun; Turk's cap is one of the most shade-tolerant flowering natives for the Gulf South and performs well under the canopy of live oaks. The distinctive red flowers never fully open, forming a twisted turban shape, and are a top hummingbird nectar source from summer through fall. Small red fruits that follow are edible and mildly sweet — birds devour them quickly, and new plants sprout readily from seeds they drop. Prune back to 12–18 in in late winter; in zone 7–8 the top dies back but the roots are reliably hardy to about 10°F.

plant_note: Plant after the last frost in a sunny, well-drained spot.
directions: Tuscarora is a vigorous crape myrtle that bears long-lasting panicles of hot coral-pink flowers from midsummer into fall. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil to maximize bloom and reduce the chance of powdery mildew. Its smooth, mottled cinnamon bark and orange-red fall color extend the interest well past the flowering season. Prune only lightly in late winter to preserve its graceful vase-shaped structure rather than cutting it back hard. Established plants tolerate heat and drought, needing extra water mainly during long dry stretches.

plant_note: Start indoors well before last frost; slow to germinate.
directions: Tweedia, or blue milkweed, offers rare true-blue, aquamarine star-shaped flowers with lavender buds and older blooms adding extra flair. Start the slow-germinating seed indoors 8 to 6 weeks before the last frost in warm soil, and transplant out after frost danger passes. Grow in full sun in well-drained soil where the sweetly scented flowers draw pollinators. Unlike common milkweeds it stays compact, making it well suited to containers and cut-flower use. Handle the stems with care, as the milky sap can irritate skin.

plant_note: Plant tubers after all danger of frost when soil has warmed.
directions: Valley Porcupine is a spiky, pink-and-white cactus-type dahlia that is both an excellent cut flower and a reliable tuber producer, making it a great choice for beginners. Plant tubers horizontally about 4 to 6 inches deep after the soil warms in spring, with the eye facing up. Site in full sun in rich, well-drained soil and stake tall plants to support the heavy blooms. Pinch the growing tip when plants are about a foot tall to encourage bushier growth and more flowers. In cold zones, lift and store the tubers indoors over winter after the first frost blackens the foliage.

plant_note: Plant tubers after the last frost when soil is warm.
directions: Valley Rust Bucket is a smaller red-orange dahlia whose warm autumn tones blend beautifully with bronze and copper companions in the cutting garden. Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun and rich, well-drained soil once frost danger has passed. Stake the plants and pinch the central shoot early to promote branching and a fuller flush of blooms. Deadhead spent flowers regularly to keep the plant producing through the season. Lift the tubers before hard freezes in colder regions and store them in a cool, dry place for replanting.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil and full sun.
directions: Vanilla Ice brings a creamy, light-yellow color to the garden on multi-branched plants about 5 feet tall bearing sweet 3-to-5-inch flowers. Direct sow the seed about an inch deep after the last frost in warm soil and full sun. Thin seedlings so the branching plants have room to develop their many blooms. Practically care-free, these sunflowers provide months of color and lovely cut flowers, and are striking in a moonlit garden. Water steadily while young and enjoy the pollinators and birds they attract.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in well-drained soil; excellent drought tolerance once established.
directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun; speedwell is notably drought-tolerant and performs poorly in soggy or heavy clay conditions. The upright spikes of blue, pink, or white flowers bloom from late spring into summer and are excellent for cutting. Deadhead by removing spent spikes back to a lateral bud or leaf to extend the flowering period and prevent excessive self-seeding. Divide every 3–4 years in early spring to maintain vigor and an attractive mounding habit.

plant_note: Sow in spring or summer; biennial that blooms the following spring.
directions: Victoria Blue is a darling, compact forget-me-not with petite, star-shaped blue flowers perfect for the front of a shade garden or alongside spring bulbs. Direct sow the seed in spring or summer, covering it lightly since darkness aids germination. As a biennial or short-lived perennial it often blooms the spring after sowing and then self-sows to return. Grow in part shade in moist, rich soil where it heralds spring with its cheerful color. Keep the soil evenly moist and let plants naturalize for a lasting display.

plant_note: Sow in spring or summer; biennial that blooms the following spring.
directions: Victoria Pink is a compact forget-me-not with petite, star-shaped pink flowers that attract pollinators and pair beautifully with spring bulbs. Direct sow the seed in spring or summer, covering it lightly since darkness helps germination. As a biennial or short-lived perennial it typically blooms the spring after sowing and self-sows to return year after year. Grow in part shade in moist, rich soil, keeping it evenly watered. Let plants naturalize at the front of shady beds for a soft, spreading carpet of color.

plant_note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; transplant several weeks before last frost as plants tolerate mild freezes.
directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep in a cool, moist starting mix; darkness during germination is beneficial. Violas are more petite and heat-tolerant than pansies, often self-sowing prolifically in the garden. Deadhead to extend flowering, or allow seed to set for naturalized self-seeding the following year. They shine in early spring containers and border edges.

plant_note: Direct sow around the last frost in full sun.
directions: Violet Queen is a clary-type annual salvia grown for its showy, long-lasting violet bracts rather than its tiny true flowers, with aromatic foliage and blooms. Direct sow around the last frost in full sun and average, well-drained soil, barely covering the seed. It blooms non-stop through summer; simply remove spent spikes to encourage continual color. Drought-tolerant once established, it mixes well with bright annuals in beds and borders. The colorful bracts also dry nicely and draw hummingbirds, butterflies, and other pollinators.

plant_note: Sow fresh seed in fall directly in a shaded, moist site; or plant divisions/transplants in early spring.
directions: Plant in humus-rich, consistently moist, shaded to partly shaded woodland soil; Virginia bluebells are a spring ephemeral that goes dormant by early summer, disappearing completely underground. The clusters of pink buds open to sky-blue, nodding tubular flowers in early to mid-spring and are among the most beautiful of native wildflowers. Mark the location of clumps so they are not accidentally disturbed; interplant with hostas, ferns, or other perennials that will fill the gap when bluebells go dormant. Divide crowded clumps or transplant in early fall when dormant.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring for late spring color.
directions: Direct sow viscaria thinly in early spring where plants are to bloom. The dainty rose, blue, and white five-petaled flowers cover slender stems and suit cottage gardens and cutting beds. It appreciates full sun and well-drained soil and blooms quickly from seed. Thin seedlings for good air movement and deadhead to prolong the flowering period.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in a shaded, moist location.
directions: Vision In Pink is a shade-loving perennial astilbe that sends up soft pink, feathery flower plumes above mounds of deep green, finely cut foliage in summer. Plant in partial to full shade in humus-rich, consistently moist soil; it will not tolerate dry conditions. Divide clumps every few years in spring to maintain vigor and increase your planting. Cut back spent plumes or leave them for winter interest, and mulch to keep roots cool and moist. It is an excellent choice for woodland gardens, shaded borders, and near water features.

plant_note: Plant corms after last frost in spring for summer bloom; in mild-winter climates (zones 9–10) plant in fall.
directions: Plant corms 3–4 in deep in fertile, well-drained soil; watsonia closely resembles gladiolus and thrives in similar conditions. The tall, sword-like foliage produces elegant spikes of tubular flowers in shades of pink, red, coral, white, and bicolor throughout summer. In frost-free climates corms multiply into large colonies and can become invasive — divide every few years to manage spread. In colder zones lift corms after frost kills the foliage, cure, and store in a cool, dry, frost-free location.

plant_note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost due to slow growth; transplant after frost danger has fully passed.
directions: Sow dust-fine seed on the surface of moist, sterile starting mix under bright light — do not cover, as seed needs light to germinate. Start early as wax begonias are slow growers before transplant size. These self-cleaning plants rarely need deadheading, blooming continuously from spring through frost. Bronze-leaved cultivars tolerate more direct sun than green-leaved types.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil; stake to control form.
directions: The Weeping Norway Spruce is a striking evergreen with dramatic cascading branches clothed in rich, dark green needles, ideal as a living sculpture or accent. Its ultimate size and shape depend on how it is staked, so train a central leader upright for a tall specimen or let it sprawl as a ground-hugging form. Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained soil and water regularly during establishment. It is hardy and cold-tolerant, needing little pruning beyond removing damaged wood. Give it space to let the weeping branches drape naturally.

plant_note: Plant in spring in moist ground, away from pipes and foundations.
directions: The Weeping Willow is a graceful, fast-growing shade tree with long, sweeping branches that trail nearly to the ground and sway in the slightest breeze. Plant in full sun in moist to wet soil, ideally near a pond or stream where its thirsty roots have ample water. Keep it well away from septic lines, drains, and foundations, as the aggressive roots seek out moisture. Water deeply and regularly, especially in dry spells and during establishment. Prune in late winter to remove crossing or damaged branches and to raise the canopy as it matures.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil in full sun.
directions: The Weeping Yoshino is a flowering cherry with gracefully cascading branches that in spring become a fountain of delicate, blush-white blossoms before the leaves emerge. Plant in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil and provide good air circulation to limit fungal issues. Water regularly during establishment and mulch the root zone, keeping mulch away from the trunk. Prune only lightly right after flowering to maintain the weeping shape, as cherries are prone to disease through large cuts. It makes a spectacular focal point in a lawn or beside a patio.

plant_note: Start indoors; transplant in spring into well-drained soil.
directions: White gaura brings airy elegance with long, delicate stems and butterfly-like white blooms that dance in the breeze above the foliage. Start seed indoors 8 to 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant into full sun and lean, well-drained soil. Its deep taproot makes it drought-tolerant once established, so avoid soggy ground and heavy feeding. Cut back spent flowering stems to encourage a fresh flush of bloom through summer and fall. Hardy in zones 5 to 9, it adds graceful height and motion to beds and borders.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; cool temperatures aid germination.
directions: White Lace produces pure-white, lacy flowers in stunning 3-to-5-inch clusters on strong stems and a compact, bushy habit that suits gardens large and small. Direct sow in early spring or fall, as the seed benefits from cool temperatures and darkness to germinate. Grow in full sun to part shade in average, well-drained soil. This long-lasting cut flower is striking massed or intermingled with colorful blooms. Deadhead to prolong flowering, and let some seed drop to encourage self-sowing.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring; biennial that may flower the second year.
directions: White Swan clary sage dazzles with luminous white bracts rising in graceful tiers along each stem for a clean, sculptural summer look. Direct sow in spring in full sun and well-drained soil, barely covering the seed. Though the true flowers are tiny, the papery bracts hold their color beautifully through heat. This drought-tolerant biennial often forms foliage the first year and blooms the second, then may self-sow. Cut the long stems for fresh or dried arrangements and enjoy the pollinators the plant attracts.

plant_note: Cold-stratify seed for best germination; transplant in spring.
directions: White Swan is a graceful white-flowered coneflower selected from the native purple echinacea, with blooms that attract butterflies and seed heads that feed birds. Cold-stratify the seed for a few weeks, then start indoors and transplant into full sun and well-drained soil. This hardy perennial is drought-tolerant once established and needs little care. Leave the cone-like seed heads standing for winter interest and to support birds. Divide clumps every few years to keep them vigorous, and enjoy the pollinator activity all summer.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification; alternatively cold-stratify indoors and spring-sow after last frost.
directions: Sow in lean to average, well-drained soil in full sun; wild quinine is a sturdy native prairie plant that tolerates heat, drought, and clay once established. The flat-topped clusters of small white button flowers appear in midsummer and persist for many weeks, drawing in a wide diversity of native bees, wasps, and beetles. The button-like, persistent seed heads provide architectural interest into fall and winter. A reliable, long-lived plant that requires virtually no maintenance once established in a sunny meadow or native plant garden.

plant_note: Plant in spring in a sunny, well-drained spot or a container that can move indoors in cold zones.
directions: Wilma Goldcrest is a lemon cypress with glowing golden-green foliage that releases a fresh citrus scent when brushed, and a narrow, upright form perfect for tight spaces. Grow it in full sun to keep the foliage brightest, in fast-draining soil that never stays waterlogged. In containers it stays around 3 to 5 feet, while in the ground in mild climates it can reach 10 feet. In zones colder than 7, grow it in a pot and bring it indoors near a bright window over winter. Water when the top inch of soil dries, and avoid soggy roots.

plant_note: Plant the potted or bare-root climber in spring after hard frosts.
directions: Winner's Circle is a vigorous climbing rose with bright, cherry-red double blooms and a strong, upright habit that flowers spring through fall. Plant the potted or bare-root rose in spring in full sun and rich, well-drained soil near a sturdy trellis, arbor, or fence to support the canes. Tie in the long canes as they grow and train them horizontally to encourage more blooms. Water at the base to keep foliage dry, mulch to conserve moisture, and prune in late winter to shape and remove old wood. Hardy in zones 4 to 10, it rewards care with repeat flushes of color.

plant_note: Plant tubers in fall as soon as available — they dry out quickly; soak in water for 24 hours before planting.
directions: Soak the small, hard tubers in water for 24 hours before planting to rehydrate them; plant 2 in deep under deciduous trees or shrubs as soon as received in late summer or early fall. Bright yellow cup-shaped flowers surrounded by a green ruff appear in late winter, often alongside snowdrops, before any leaves emerge on overhead trees. Winter aconite self-seeds freely and slowly forms large, cheerful carpets if left undisturbed. Best planted where they can stay permanently; they strongly dislike being moved.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed.
directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist mix without covering; they need light to germinate at 70–75°F. Torenia is one of the best flowering annuals for shaded and part-shaded spots in hot, humid climates where impatiens are lost to downy mildew. The bicolored, snapdragon-like flowers in purple, pink, white, or yellow bloom profusely without deadheading throughout summer. Keep soil consistently moist, as plants wilt quickly when dry but recover readily; fertilize monthly with a balanced liquid feed.

plant_note: Plant rhizomes in fall in humus-rich shade.
directions: Plant wood anemone rhizomes in fall just below the surface in cool, humus-rich woodland soil in partial to full shade. The starry white spring flowers carpet the ground before deciduous trees leaf out, then the plant goes dormant by summer. It spreads slowly by creeping rhizomes to form a lovely ephemeral groundcover. Keep the soil moist during growth and mark the location, as the foliage disappears entirely in summer.

plant_note: Plant tubers after the last frost once soil has warmed.
directions: Wyns New Pastel is a tall, vigorous dahlia bearing big pink-and-yellow blended blooms all season on a well-branched, hedge-like plant. Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun and rich, well-drained soil after the danger of frost has passed. Because of its height, provide sturdy staking early and pinch the central shoot to encourage branching. Cut flowers freely and deadhead to keep new buds forming through summer and fall. In cold climates, dig and store the tubers over winter, replanting them the following spring.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in full sun; blooms quickly from seed.
directions: Xanthos is a breakthrough soft-yellow cosmos, an award winner that is early to flower on well-branched, shorter plants ideal for containers and the front of the border. Direct sow after the last frost in full sun and average, well-drained soil, barely covering the seed. It thrives in lean soil, so avoid overfeeding, which produces foliage at the expense of blooms. Deadhead or cut flowers often to keep the plant producing through summer. Drought-tolerant once established, it draws bees, butterflies, and other pollinators.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost; prefers to be direct sown as transplants disturb its taproot.
directions: Direct sow after last frost in full sun with lean, well-drained soil; xeranthemum is one of the easiest everlastings to grow and tolerates poor, dry conditions well. The papery, daisy-like flowers in shades of lavender, pink, purple, and white are naturally self-preserving and air-dry on the stem. Harvest stems for drying when flowers are about two-thirds open; hang upside down in a warm, airy location for 2–3 weeks. Successive sowings 2–3 weeks apart can extend the harvest season into early fall.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun and well-drained soil.
directions: The Yoshino cherry is the classic flowering cherry famed for its spring canopy of fragrant, blush-white blossoms that open before the leaves emerge. Plant in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil, giving it plenty of room for its broad, spreading crown. Water regularly while young and mulch the root zone, keeping the mulch off the trunk. Prune sparingly and only just after bloom, since cherries are susceptible to disease through large wounds. It provides light shade in summer and golden-bronze foliage in fall, making it a beloved landscape specimen.

plant_note: Plant in fall or spring in partial shade with acidic, well-drained soil.
directions: Yuletide is a sasanqua camellia that blooms in late fall and early winter, opening bright single red flowers with showy golden stamens against glossy evergreen foliage. Plant in partial shade in acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil; sheltering it from harsh afternoon sun and drying winds protects the blooms. Keep the soil consistently moist but never soggy, and mulch to conserve moisture and maintain acidity. Prune lightly right after flowering to shape the shrub. Its winter color makes it a standout for foundation plantings, hedges, and holiday-season interest.

plant_note: Sow outdoors around the last spring frost; tolerates light frost and can also be sown in late summer for fall bloom.
directions: Direct sow seeds about a quarter inch deep once the soil is workable in spring, or start indoors a few weeks before the last frost. This cultivar produces bronzy-orange petals that fade to soft pink over a maroon-backed, yellow-centered flower. Deadhead regularly to keep the edible blooms coming and prevent early fade in summer heat. Petals are edible and make a colorful addition to salads or as a garnish. Calendula self-sows readily and thrives in cool weather.

plant_note: Sow after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed; zinnias resent cold, wet ground.
directions: Direct sow after the last frost about a quarter inch deep, or start indoors a few weeks early for an earlier show. This scabiosa-flowered blend yields 2 to 2.5 inch double, semi-double, and occasional single blooms in soft pastel shades where no two flowers are alike. Pinch young plants once to encourage branching and more stems for cutting. Cut or deadhead frequently to keep the plants productive all season. Provide good air circulation to reduce powdery mildew.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost when soil temperature is above 65°F; also easy to start indoors 4–6 weeks early.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep directly in the garden once frost risk is past and soil is warm. Pinch seedlings at 6 in tall to promote branching and a fuller plant with more stems for cutting. Cut flowers frequently to the next set of leaves to stimulate continuous bloom through summer. Avoid overhead watering to reduce powdery mildew; water at the base.

plant_note: Sow directly after the last frost; successive sowings every couple of weeks extend the bloom season.
directions: Direct sow about one inch deep after the last frost once soil has warmed. This branching, pollenless-style florist sunflower blooms roughly two months from sowing, bearing golden-yellow 4 to 6 inch flowers on tall, sturdy stems ideal for bouquets. Space plants closer for single-stem cut flowers or wider to encourage branching and more blooms. Its long vase life makes it a favorite for the backyard florist. Stake in windy sites to keep the tall stems upright.

plant_note: Start indoors about 6 weeks before the last frost, then transplant out once frost danger has passed.
directions: Start seeds indoors about six weeks before the last frost and transplant after the weather warms, or direct sow in warm regions. Also called Monarch of the Veldt, this heat- and drought-tolerant South African annual produces 3 to 4 inch daisy-like flowers above silver-tinged foliage from midsummer to fall. Grow in full sun and well-drained soil, and avoid overwatering as it prefers drier conditions. The blooms attract bees, birds, and butterflies. Flowers close at night and on cloudy days.

plant_note: Plant grafted trees in spring; abiu is cold-sensitive and requires a frost-free year-round climate.
directions: Plant in deep, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun; abiu is closely related to sapodilla and canistel and shares their preference for warm, humid conditions with regular moisture. Trees are self-fertile and heavy bearers once established; grafted selections begin fruiting in 2–4 years versus 7+ for seedlings. The smooth golden-yellow skin of ripe fruit conceals a translucent, gelatinous, caramel-flavored flesh — eat by cutting in half and scooping; avoid getting the sticky latex near the lips if the fruit is slightly underripe. Harvest when the skin has turned fully golden-yellow and the fruit yields gently to thumb pressure, typically in late summer through fall.

plant_note: Plant container-grown or grafted stock in spring after all frost danger; in zone 9 site against a south-facing wall for extra warmth.
directions: Acerola produces cherry-like fruits with one of the highest natural concentrations of vitamin C of any food plant — up to 65 times that of an orange. The shrubby tree is self-fertile and can bear multiple times per year in truly frost-free climates; it is very sensitive to cold below 30°F and root systems are damaged below 28°F. Plant in well-drained, slightly acid to neutral soil and fertilize lightly with a balanced fertilizer three times per year. Fruit ripens very quickly from green to red and is extremely perishable — harvest when fully red and process (freeze, juice) within 24–48 hours for maximum vitamin C retention.

plant_note: Plant in the warm, wet tropics; strictly frost-free.
directions: The African mango, or bush mango, is a large tropical tree grown for its mango-like fruit and especially its oil-rich seed kernel, called dika nut. It demands a hot, humid, frost-free climate with abundant moisture and deep, fertile soil. Plant in full sun with plenty of room, as mature trees become tall and broad. Trees are slow to bear from seed, so grafted stock fruits sooner; harvest the fallen fruit and dry the prized kernels.

plant_note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter before bud break; almonds bloom very early and need a frost-free spring.
directions: Almonds bloom earlier than almost any other tree fruit and are severely damaged by late spring frosts, which is why commercial production is limited to Mediterranean-type climates. Most varieties require a cross-pollinator — plant at least two compatible cultivars (Non-Pareil with Carmel or Price, for example). Trees thrive in well-drained, alkaline to neutral soils and are drought-tolerant once established. Harvest when the hulls split open and the shells dry and harden, typically in late summer; spread nuts to dry for two weeks before storing.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container-grown trees in early spring in rich, moist, well-drained, acidic soil.
directions: American beech is monoecious and wind-pollinated, but nut production is highly variable and irregular — large mast crops occur every 2–5 years, driven by weather patterns; at least two trees (ideally a grove) dramatically improves mast yield in any given year. The triangular beechnuts are sweet and nutritious, high in fat and protein, and valued by both wildlife and people; they are best eaten raw or lightly roasted and are traditionally used for oil extraction. Beech is extremely sensitive to soil compaction and grade changes around the root zone — construction damage is the primary killer of mature trees in urban settings. Plant in deep, moist, well-drained, acidic soil away from construction zones and plan for very long-term production: significant nut crops typically begin 10+ years after planting.

plant_note: Plant in early spring; persimmons have a deep taproot so handle with care and disturb roots as little as possible.
directions: American persimmon is dioecious — male and female flowers occur on separate trees — so plant at least one of each for reliable fruiting, though some populations produce fruit without pollination. It is exceptionally cold-hardy and tolerant of poor soils, making it one of the easiest native fruit trees. Fruit is intensely astringent until fully ripe and soft, usually after the first hard freeze; pick after the flesh has turned translucent orange and jelly-soft. Little pruning is needed beyond removing dead or crossing wood.

plant_note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter to early spring while still dormant, before buds break.
directions: Plant bare-root stock in well-drained soil, spreading roots carefully; stake dwarf trees for the first two seasons. Most varieties require a second cultivar nearby for cross-pollination — check chill-hour requirements to match your climate (600–1200 hours for standard types, under 400 for low-chill selections). Prune annually in late dormancy to an open-center or central-leader form to improve light penetration and air flow. Harvest when fruit separates with a gentle upward twist and seeds have turned brown, 2–5 years after planting.

plant_note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter, as early as the soil can be worked, to allow root establishment before bloom.
directions: Most apricot varieties are self-fertile, which simplifies home-orchard planting, but two trees will increase yields. The key limiting factor in temperate climates is the tree's early bloom time — a hard frost after bud break will eliminate the crop, so choose a sheltered north-facing or slightly elevated site to delay bloom, or select late-blooming cultivars. Prune in early summer rather than winter to reduce risk of cytospora canker infection. Harvest when the fruit is fully orange, fragrant, and gives slightly to the touch.

plant_note: Set out dormant bare-root trees in early spring.
directions: An aprium is an apricot-dominant hybrid with the sweetness of apricot and a hint of plum, often bearing a faint fuzz. Plant in full sun on light, well-drained soil in a frost-protected spot, since apricot-type blooms open very early. Many apriums are self-fruitful but crop more heavily with an apricot pollinator nearby. Prune in dry summer weather to limit disease and thin the fruit for good size.

plant_note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter to early spring; in mild-winter zones plant in fall.
directions: Asian pears ripen on the tree — unlike European pears they do not need post-harvest ripening — so taste-test for sweetness before picking. Most varieties are self-unfruitful and yield best with a second Asian pear or even a compatible European variety nearby. Chill requirements are moderate (450–700 hours), making many selections suitable for zones 5–9. Annual thinning of fruit clusters to one per spur prevents limb breakage and encourages larger, better-flavored fruit.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost danger has passed; fall planting is fine in zones 8–10.
directions: Asian persimmons come in astringent types (such as Hachiya, best eaten when jelly-soft) and non-astringent types (such as Fuyu, eaten firm like an apple). Most cultivars are self-fertile and produce well as single trees. Trees need very little pruning — maintain a central leader and remove inward-growing branches to open the canopy. In cooler parts of zone 7, choose hardier varieties and site against a south-facing wall; in zones 9–10, low-chill cultivars perform best.

plant_note: Grown from a grafted or potted tree; move outdoors after frost.
directions: The Australian finger lime bears elongated fruit packed with juicy, caviar-like vesicles that burst with tart citrus flavor, making it a chef's favorite. Native to rugged coastal Australia, this compact, thorny citrus is tough and adapts well to container culture. Grow from a potted tree in full sun with rich, well-drained soil, and protect it from frost by moving it indoors in cold climates. Feed with a citrus fertilizer during the growing season and water steadily without letting roots sit wet.

plant_note: Plant in spring when all frost risk is past and soil is warm; avoid planting in summer heat.
directions: Avocados bear Type A flowers (open as female in the morning) and Type B flowers (open as female in the afternoon); planting one of each type near each other ensures pollen is available throughout the day and greatly increases fruit set. Grafted trees begin fruiting in 3–5 years; seedlings may take 8–10 years. Avocados are sensitive to root rot in heavy or poorly drained soils — plant on a mound or raised bed and irrigate deeply but infrequently. Fruit does not ripen on the tree; pick when full-sized and let ripen at room temperature for 3–8 days.

plant_note: Plant in spring after cold weather passes; bael tolerates light frost once established but does best in warm subtropical to tropical climates.
directions: Bael is a spiny citrus relative (family Rutaceae) highly revered in South Asian horticulture and medicine that tolerates drought, alkaline soils, and brief frosts to about 25°F once the trunk is mature — among the hardiest of the true tropical fruit trees. Trees are self-fertile and pollinated by insects; a single specimen fruits reliably. The hard-shelled, woody fruit takes 9–12 months to ripen on the tree; harvest when the skin turns from green to yellowish-green and the fruit sounds hollow when tapped. The sticky, aromatic flesh is scooped out and eaten fresh with sugar, blended into a popular cooling drink (bael sherbet), or dried; dried slices brew into a medicinal tisane with mild anti-diarrheal properties.

plant_note: Plant in spring in a frost-free site; bilimbi is more cold-sensitive than its relative starfruit.
directions: Bilimbi is a cauliflorous tree — clusters of small red flowers and the resulting green, cucumber-shaped fruits emerge directly from the trunk and main branches — creating a striking ornamental effect. It is self-fertile and prolific, producing nearly year-round in warm climates with adequate moisture. The fruit is extremely acidic and rarely eaten raw; it is prized as a souring agent in Southeast Asian and Caribbean cuisines, used as a marinade tenderizer, and made into preserves, chutneys, and cooling drinks. Harvest the pale green to yellow-green fruits while still firm for culinary use; fully ripe fruits become soft and ferment quickly on the tree.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost danger passes in a warm, sheltered position.
directions: The black mulberry is a slow-growing, long-lived tree bearing intensely flavored dark fruit far superior to that of white or red mulberries. Plant in full sun on fertile, well-drained soil in a spot sheltered from cold winds. It is self-fertile, so one tree crops well, though the staining fruit is best sited away from paving. Prune only lightly in winter, as mulberries bleed heavily from large cuts.

plant_note: Plant in spring after all frost risk has passed; young trees are cold-sensitive and need protection until the trunk is at least 2 inches in diameter.
directions: Black sapote is a relative of the Asian persimmon; when ripe its flesh turns chocolate-brown and has a rich, pudding-like flavor often described as chocolate mousse. Trees are dioecious or polygamous — plant at least two seedlings or a grafted female with a male nearby for reliable fruiting. They prefer well-drained, fertile soils with a pH near 6.5 and are sensitive to frost, waterlogging, and salt winds. Harvest when the skin turns from bright olive-green to olive-yellow-green and the fruit gives to very gentle pressure; leave at room temperature 5–7 days until the inside is completely dark and custardy.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container stock in early spring; site carefully as the tree grows very large and produces juglone.
directions: Black walnut is a native North American species grown primarily for its richly flavored nuts and valuable timber. It produces high concentrations of juglone in its roots, leaves, and hulls, which is toxic to many garden plants including tomatoes, peppers, apples, and blueberries; maintain a 50–80 foot buffer from sensitive plants. Trees are monoecious and partially self-fertile but cross well with a second tree. Hulls stain skin and clothing an indelible dark brown; wear gloves when husking. Nuts are ready when hulls are yellow-green to black and dent when pressed; cure for 2–3 weeks after husking.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost risk has passed; blood oranges require cool winters for anthocyanin color development — they color best in climates with cold nights during ripening.
directions: Blood orange cultivars (Moro, Tarocco, Sanguinello) develop their signature ruby-red flesh and juice through the accumulation of anthocyanins, a pigment triggered by cool nights during fruit development — temperatures between 40 and 50°F for several weeks from late autumn through midwinter produce the deepest coloring. In frost-free tropical climates the flesh remains orange with little anthocyanin. Trees are self-fertile, medium-sized, and managed identically to other sweet oranges: plant in well-drained soil, fertilize with a citrus blend three times per year, and irrigate deeply but infrequently. Harvest from late winter through early spring when the flesh has developed full color and the fruit is sweet.

plant_note: Plant the dormant or potted dwarf tree in spring; grown from a grafted tree, not seed.
directions: Bonanza is a genetic dwarf peach that fits easily on a patio yet produces full-size, sweet, rosy fruit. Its naturally compact habit and showy pink spring blossoms make it as ornamental as it is productive, ideal for containers and small yards. Plant a grafted tree in full sun with rich, well-drained soil, and provide a large pot or a spot with room for the roots. Thin the young fruit for better size, prune in late winter to shape, and water consistently while fruit is developing.

plant_note: Plant the dwarf ornamental peach in spring; grown from a grafted tree.
directions: Bonfire is a compact ornamental peach grown chiefly for its striking deep-crimson foliage and pink spring flowers, though it can set small edible fruit. Its fiery color and dwarf size make it a dramatic focal point for patios and small gardens. Plant a grafted tree in full sun with well-drained soil in a large container or bed. Prune after flowering to maintain its tidy form, water regularly during the growing season, and expect the best leaf color in full sun.

plant_note: Plant root-shoot suckers or grafted trees in spring; breadfruit requires a fully frost-free year-round climate.
directions: Propagate from root shoots or cuttings rather than seed, as most cultivated varieties are seedless and parthenocarpic — no pollination is required for fruit set. Trees prefer deep, fertile, well-drained soil and consistent moisture; they tolerate brief drought once mature but perform best with regular irrigation. A single mature tree can produce 200–800 fruits per year; thin clusters to 2–3 fruit per stem to improve size. Harvest when the skin transitions from bright green to yellowish-green with slight latex seeping from surface cracks, indicating starch has developed but the flesh has not yet become overripe and sticky.

plant_note: Grown from a grafted or air-layered tree; move outdoors after frost.
directions: The Brewster lychee is a classic favorite bearing sweet, juicy fruit encased in a glossy red shell over fragrant white flesh. This subtropical evergreen prefers hot, humid summers and needs a brief cool period to trigger flowering. Grow a grafted or air-layered tree in full sun with acidic, well-drained soil rich in organic matter, and shelter it fully from frost. Keep the root zone consistently moist, mulch heavily, and protect young trees from wind while they establish.

plant_note: Plant in spring in a frost-free or nearly frost-free location.
directions: The bronze loquat is grown both for its striking coppery new foliage and small, tart edible fruit. Plant in full sun on fertile, well-drained soil in a warm, sheltered position, as the flowers and young growth are frost-sensitive. Water regularly while establishing, then only during dry spells once mature. Prune after fruiting to shape the canopy and remove any frost-damaged tips.

plant_note: Plant after the last hard frost; in colder zones grow in a container that can be moved to shelter.
directions: This cold-hardy fig tolerates temperatures near 10 degrees F, making it a good choice for cooler regions where most figs struggle. Plant in full sun with rich, well-drained soil and provide consistent moisture while fruit is developing for deep burgundy fruit with a pink center. In borderline zones, mulch heavily around the base or grow in a pot to overwinter in a protected spot. Prune in late winter to keep the tree at a manageable height and encourage a strong branching structure.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container trees in early spring; pair with another Japanese or American plum for cross-pollination.
directions: This cold-hardy hybrid plum produces juicy reddish-purple fruit with a distinctive sweet, spicy flavor often compared to bubblegum. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil, and include a compatible pollinizer nearby since Toka also serves as an excellent pollinator for other plums. Water consistently during fruit set and thin heavy fruit clusters to improve size and prevent limb breakage. Prune in late winter to an open-center form for good light penetration and air flow.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost risk passes; Buddha's hand is very cold-sensitive and ideal for container culture.
directions: Buddha's hand is a citron variety bearing distinctive finger-like lobes that contain only aromatic peel with no juice or pulp; it is valued entirely for its intensely fragrant zest used in baking, cocktails, and as a natural air freshener. Trees are self-fertile and small enough to grow in 15–20 gallon containers, making them suitable for patios and sunrooms in colder climates; bring indoors before temperatures fall below 32°F. Fertilize with a citrus-specific fertilizer three times per year and maintain consistent moisture to prevent fruit drop. Harvest when the fingers have turned fully yellow and the fragrance is intense; fruit can be stored at room temperature for 1–2 weeks.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container stock in early spring; choose disease-resistant hybrid selections due to butternut canker pressure on the native species.
directions: Butternut, or white walnut, produces rich, oily nuts with a milder, sweeter flavor than black walnut and is native to eastern North America. The species is severely threatened by Sirococcus clavigignenti-juglandacearum (butternut canker), so seek out canker-resistant cultivars or hybrids (butternut x heartnut crosses, or selections from university breeding programs) rather than straight-species seedlings. Like other walnuts, butternut is monoecious and partially self-fertile but benefits from a second tree nearby; it also produces juglone that can inhibit nearby sensitive plants. Harvest when the sticky, hairy green hulls begin to soften and turn yellow-brown in early to mid-fall; hull promptly and dry nuts for 2–3 weeks before cracking or storing.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost risk passes; calamondin is excellent for container culture in zones 7–8, where it can be brought indoors in winter.
directions: Calamondin is a hybrid between a kumquat and a mandarin orange, producing prolific small, sour-sweet fruits that are eaten whole like kumquats or used as a lime substitute in Southeast Asian cooking. The tree is self-fertile, nearly thornless, highly ornamental with fragrant white flowers, and among the easiest citrus to grow in containers — it tolerates indoor conditions better than most citrus. It tolerates brief dips to about 20°F when established. Fruit ripens from late autumn through spring; harvest when fully orange for best flavor, though the rind remains sweet while the flesh is tart even at the green-orange stage.

plant_note: Grow outdoors only in frost-free zones; elsewhere keep in a container that overwinters indoors.
directions: This ornamental citrus, a kumquat-mandarin cross, bears a heavy crop of small, round, tart fruit prized for marmalades and drinks. Grow in full sun in well-drained, slightly acidic soil, and feed regularly with a citrus fertilizer during the growing season. In non-tropical climates keep it potted so it can move to a bright indoor spot before frost. Fragrant white blossoms and glossy evergreen leaves make it as decorative as it is productive.

plant_note: Plant outdoors in warm zones; grow in a movable container in colder regions.
directions: This compact mandarin produces sweet, juicy, easy-peeling fruit and stays small enough for patios or sunny indoor rooms. Plant in full sun in fast-draining soil and water deeply but allow the top inch to dry between waterings. Feed with a citrus-specific fertilizer through the growing season and protect from frost, moving containers indoors when temperatures drop. Light pruning after harvest keeps the canopy open and the tree productive.

plant_note: Plant grafted trees in spring after all frost danger has passed; canistel is limited to truly frost-free zones in the continental U.S.
directions: Canistel is an evergreen tropical tree that produces smooth, orange-yellow fruit with dense, dry, egg-yolk-colored flesh — hence the common name egg fruit. Grafted trees are strongly preferred over seedlings; seedlings may take 5–7 years to fruit and show variable quality, while grafted plants can begin bearing in 2–3 years. The tree is drought-tolerant once established and tolerates a range of well-drained soils but is damaged by temperatures below 32°F even briefly. Harvest when the skin turns fully golden-yellow and yields firmly to pressure; ripen indoors at room temperature for 3–5 days until completely soft and sweet.

plant_note: Grown from a grafted tree; move outdoors after frost.
directions: The Cara Cara is a sweet, low-acid navel orange distinguished by its rosy-pink flesh and berry-like flavor. This adaptable citrus performs well in the ground in mild regions or in a large container that can be moved indoors where winters are cold. Grow a grafted tree in full sun with well-drained soil and feed it a citrus-specific fertilizer through the growing season. Water deeply but allow the top of the soil to dry between waterings, and protect it from frost.

plant_note: Plant grafted trees in spring; young trees need protection from cold and wind for the first two seasons.
directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; carambola is sensitive to flooding and cold below 27°F even briefly, limiting outdoor cultivation to frost-free zones. The tree blooms multiple times per year and produces fruits along the trunk and main branches — a trait called cauliflory — so hang fruit-protection bags in commercial settings to prevent blemishes. Harvest when the ribs have turned fully yellow and the fruit has slight give; overripe fruit browns quickly at the tips. Fertilize with a balanced citrus-type fertilizer 3–4 times per year to sustain heavy bearing.

plant_note: Plant container-grown stock in spring after frost; carob is well-suited to dry Mediterranean-climate zones 9–11 and tolerates alkaline, rocky soils.
directions: Carob is a slow-growing evergreen legume tree that fixes atmospheric nitrogen and thrives in dry, rocky, alkaline soils where few other fruits survive; it is exceptionally drought-tolerant once established. Trees are dioecious — plant one male for every 8–10 female trees, or choose hermaphroditic cultivars such as Santa Fe and Clifford that produce fruit without a separate male. The long, dark-brown seed pods ripen in late summer and fall and are left on the tree to dry and sweeten; they are harvested when fully brown, dry, and the seeds rattle inside. Carob pods are sweet and chocolatey and used ground as a cocoa substitute or in stock feed.

plant_note: Plant grafted trees in spring for reliable fruiting.
directions: Che, sometimes called the Chinese mulberry, produces round, red, fig-like fruit with a mild, melon-melon flavor. It is drought-tolerant and adaptable once established, growing well in full sun on well-drained soil. Seedling trees can be thorny and slow to bear, so plant a grafted, female cultivar for earlier crops; a male tree nearby can improve set. Prune to keep it manageable and net the sweet fruit against birds as it ripens in autumn.

plant_note: Plant grafted trees in spring after frost danger; in coastal California (zone 10a) fall planting before the rainy season is ideal.
directions: Cherimoya thrives in California's coastal valleys where marine influence moderates temperature; it is less suited to the interior heat of zones 9–10a than to the mild, humid coast. Its flowers are protogynous — the stigma is receptive before the pollen is shed — so hand-pollinate by collecting pollen from partially open flowers in the afternoon and transferring it to newly opened receptive flowers in the morning for reliable fruit set. Trees are partially deciduous and shed leaves during dry or cool periods; withhold irrigation slightly during this dormancy to mimic the seasonally dry upland habitat. Harvest when the green skin begins to turn pale or slightly yellowish and the fruit gives under light thumb pressure; ripen at room temperature 3–5 days until soft and fragrant.

plant_note: Plant bare-root trees in early spring; chestnuts strongly prefer acidic, well-drained soil.
directions: Chestnuts require cross-pollination between at least two different cultivars or seedlings — plant them within 50 feet of each other for reliable nut set. American chestnut was devastated by blight, so choose blight-resistant Chinese, European, or hybrid selections (such as Dunstan Chestnuts). Trees strongly prefer acidic soils (pH 4.5–6.5) and perform poorly in alkaline or wet conditions. Harvest in fall when burs open and nuts fall; collect daily as nuts on the ground deteriorate quickly. Fresh chestnuts are perishable — refrigerate promptly and use or freeze within a month.

plant_note: Plant after the last frost; in cold zones the tops may die back and regrow from the roots.
directions: One of the hardiest figs available, Chicago Hardy can be grown down to Zone 5, where the tops may freeze back but resprout from the roots to fruit on new wood. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and mulch heavily over winter in colder regions to protect the crown. Water steadily while fruit develops for the best sweetness. In the coldest zones, grow it in a container that can overwinter in a garage or basement.

plant_note: Plant container-grown trees in early spring in moist, well-drained, acidic soil; prefers Pacific Northwest conditions.
directions: Chinkapin (Castanopsis chrysophylla, also called golden chinquapin) is a large Pacific Coast evergreen related to chestnuts and oaks, bearing small, intensely sweet nuts in spiny burs — often considered the finest-flavored nut native to the Pacific Northwest. Unlike American chestnut it is not susceptible to chestnut blight, making it a valuable blight-free nut tree for western gardeners. Trees are monoecious but require cross-pollination between at least two seedlings or distinct cultivars for reliable nut production; wind and insects both carry pollen. Burs take two years to mature and split open in autumn — collect nuts promptly before squirrels and jays claim the entire crop, and consume or freeze within a month as the small nuts dry out quickly.

plant_note: Plant in spring in frost-free areas; very cold-sensitive.
directions: Citron is an ancient citrus grown mainly for its thick, fragrant rind, which is candied or used in preserves. It is among the most frost-tender citrus, so grow it in full sun in the warmest possible spot or in a container to move under cover. Provide rich, well-drained soil and steady moisture, feeding regularly during growth. Prune to remove the plant's stout thorns and dead wood, and harvest the large fruit when fully colored.
