plant_note: Plant in spring in frost-free citrus zones or grow in a container.
directions: Clementines are sweet, seedless, easy-peeling mandarins that ripen in early winter. Grow in full sun on well-drained soil, sheltered from frost, or in a large pot to overwinter under glass in cool climates. Some cultivars fruit more reliably without a cross-pollinator, which keeps them seedless. Feed with citrus fertilizer during the growing season and harvest as soon as the fruit colors, since it holds only briefly on the tree.

plant_note: Plant outdoors in warm zones; grow in a movable container elsewhere.
directions: Clementine produces sweet, seedless, easy-peeling mandarins on a tidy, manageable tree suitable for gardens or large containers. Plant in full sun in fast-draining, slightly acidic soil and water deeply, letting the surface dry between waterings to avoid root rot. Feed with a citrus fertilizer through spring and summer for strong fruit set. Protect from frost by moving potted trees indoors to a bright spot when cold weather arrives.

plant_note: Plant outdoors in warm zones; grow in a container that can move indoors in cooler regions.
directions: This semi-dwarf grapefruit-pomelo hybrid produces juicy, sweet-tart, low-acid fruit on a compact tree ideal for patios or small yards. Grow in full sun in fast-draining, slightly acidic soil and water deeply while letting the surface dry between waterings. Feed with a citrus fertilizer during the growing season for good fruit development. Move container trees to a bright indoor location before frost, as grapefruit is cold-sensitive.

plant_note: Plant in spring in a frost-free site; tolerates salt, flooding, alkaline soils, and sandy coastal conditions.
directions: Cocoplum is an extremely adaptable native Florida and Caribbean shrub-tree that tolerates salt spray, periodic flooding, alkaline soils, and full sun — one of the toughest edible landscape plants for coastal south Florida. Plants are self-fertile and fruit reliably as hedges or specimens; the white, pink, or purple oval drupes ripen sporadically through the warm months. The sweet, mildly flavored flesh is eaten fresh or made into jams and jellies; the large oily seed is edible and has a coconut-like flavor when roasted. Harvest when fruit has reached its full color (cream, pink, or deep purple depending on ecotype) and is soft to the touch.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost in a hot, sunny site with sharp drainage.
directions: This cold-tolerant pomegranate bears vibrant red-fruited crops and handsome orange-red flowers on a large, multi-stemmed shrub or small tree. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil; it thrives in heat and tolerates drought once established. Water deeply but infrequently, easing off as fruit ripens to prevent splitting. In the colder end of its range, site it against a warm south wall and mulch the base for winter protection.

plant_note: Plant grafted or container-grown trees in spring after all frost risk has passed; in frost-free zones fall planting avoids summer heat stress.
directions: Guava is self-fertile and productive as a single tree; grafted selections of known cultivars (Ruby Supreme, Thai White) are superior to seedlings for flavor and bearing consistency. Trees tolerate a wide range of soils but are sensitive to standing water and prolonged frost — temperatures below 28°F damage foliage and below 24°F kill established trunks. Prune annually to 10–12 ft to simplify harvest and improve light penetration; guava bears on new growth so heading-back encourages productivity. Harvest when the skin softens, the color shifts from green to yellow or pink, and the fruit is intensely fragrant.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container trees in early spring in a sunny, well-drained site.
directions: Contender is a disease-resistant, cold-hardy, self-pollinating peach that produces abundant medium-to-large juicy freestone fruit across many climates. Plant in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil and water consistently during fruit development. Thin young fruit to about 6 inches apart to improve size and prevent limb breakage. Prune annually in late winter to an open-center shape for light penetration, and apply dormant sprays as needed to manage common peach diseases.

plant_note: Plant in spring in mild-winter regions.
directions: Coolidge is a self-fertile pineapple guava selection, useful where only one plant can be grown. Set it in full sun on well-drained soil and water consistently through the fruiting season for the best crop. It tolerates coastal conditions and light frost once established, and can be grown as a shrub or clipped hedge. Fruit is ripe when it drops naturally, so gather it daily from the ground beneath the plant.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container stock in late winter to early spring before bud break; fall planting also works in zones 5–7.
directions: Cornelian cherry is one of the earliest-blooming woody plants — small yellow flowers appear in late winter before the leaves — and the small, olive-shaped drupes ripen in late summer to a rich ruby red. Plant at least two seedlings or cultivars for best fruit set, as it is partially self-fertile but cross-pollination greatly improves yields. The tree is extremely cold-hardy and tolerates drought, alkaline soils, and urban pollution once established. Harvest when fruit is fully dark red and slightly soft; it is too tart to eat out of hand but makes outstanding preserves, syrups, and fruit leather.

plant_note: Plant dormant trees in early spring or fall in mild areas.
directions: Crabapples bear small, tart fruit excellent for jelly and serve as reliable pollinizers for dessert apples. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, choosing disease-resistant selections to limit scab and rust. They are extremely cold-hardy and adaptable, tolerating a wide range of soils. Prune in late winter to maintain an open canopy, and harvest the high-pectin fruit after the first firm color develops.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container trees in early spring with a compatible pear nearby for pollination.
directions: D'Anjou is a vigorous, productive European pear yielding sweet, juicy fruit excellent for fresh eating, baking, and canning. Plant in full sun in deep, well-drained soil and include a different pear variety nearby for cross-pollination. Water consistently during fruit development and prune in late winter to a central-leader form. Harvest the fruit while still firm and ripen it off the tree in cool storage for the best texture.

plant_note: Plant while dormant in early spring; tolerates cooler, wetter sites than dessert plums.
directions: The damson is a hardy, self-fertile culinary plum whose tart, astringent fruit shines in jams, gins, and preserves. It thrives even on heavier soils and in exposed positions where dessert plums struggle. Plant in full sun to partial shade and keep young trees weed-free and mulched. Little pruning is needed beyond removing dead or crossing wood in summer; expect reliable heavy crops after three to four years.

plant_note: Plant offshoots or container stock in spring when the soil is warm; dates require the specific combination of desert heat and dry air to ripen properly.
directions: Dates are dioecious — you need a male tree to pollinate female trees, with one male sufficient for up to 50 females; commercial growers hand-pollinate by shaking male flower strands over female inflorescences in spring. Ripe dates require extremely hot, dry summers (110°F daytime heat preferred) with very low humidity during the ripening period — this limits quality fruit production in the continental U.S. almost entirely to the Coachella Valley and low desert Arizona. Offshoots from the base of female palms are the preferred planting material as they preserve the sex and fruit type of the parent; seed-grown palms yield 50% males and highly variable fruit. Harvest in fall when fruit reaches the desired stage: khalal (crisp, astringent), rutab (partly soft, sweet), or tamr (fully dried).

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost danger passes; overwinter indoors in cold climates.
directions: Dragon fruit is a climbing, white-fleshed cactus that produces dramatic sculptural stems and sweet fruit following its large night-blooming flowers. Grow it in fast-draining cactus soil and provide a sturdy trellis or post for the heavy vining stems to climb. Water only when the soil dries out, since the plant stores moisture and is prone to rot if kept wet. It needs warmth above 40 degrees, so grow it in a container to move indoors where frost occurs. Hand-pollinate the evening flowers for reliable fruit set.

plant_note: Plant grafted trees in spring in a humid, frost-free tropical climate; durian requires consistent warmth above 50°F.
directions: Durian demands deep, fertile, well-drained soil, high humidity, and temperatures between 75°F and 95°F year-round; it is the most climate-specific of the major tropical fruits and can only be grown outdoors in the most tropical parts of the continental United States (extreme south Florida). Flowers open at night and are primarily bat-pollinated, so maintain nearby native habitat and avoid pesticide applications at dusk; hand-pollination improves set in enclosed settings. Fruit develops over 3–5 months and the spiny husk is potentially dangerous — trees should be netted and fruit harvested from the ground after it falls naturally. The ripe flesh has a rich custard flavor with a pungent sulfurous aroma that intensifies rapidly after harvest; consume or freeze within 24–48 hours.

plant_note: Plant after frost danger; grow in a container to overwinter indoors in cold climates.
directions: Dwarf Cavendish is a compact banana cultivar that fruits at a manageable height, making it well suited to containers and small spaces. Grow it in full sun in rich, well-drained soil, feeding heavily through the warm season since bananas are gross feeders. Keep the soil consistently moist and shelter the large leaves from strong wind that can shred them. Where temperatures drop below the mid-50s, move the plant indoors to a bright, warm spot for winter. Each stem fruits once, after which it is cut down and replaced by new pups from the base.

plant_note: Grown from a rooted cutting; a climbing cactus needing sturdy support.
directions: Edgar's Baby is a compact dragon fruit, a climbing epiphytic cactus that rewards growers with sweet, vividly colored fruit on a manageable plant. It produces large, fragrant night-blooming flowers followed by fruit and thrives with warmth and strong support to climb. Grow from a rooted cutting in full sun and fast-draining, sandy soil, watering only when the mix dries since the cactus rots in soggy conditions. Provide a trellis or post, feed lightly, and shelter it from any frost.

plant_note: Plant while dormant in early spring in full sun.
directions: Elberta, sometimes called Early Elberta, is an early-fruiting freestone peach with juicy, flavorful yellow flesh and good disease resistance. Plant it in full sun in deep, well-drained soil, avoiding frost pockets that can damage early blossoms. This variety is self-fruitful, so a single tree will set a crop without a pollinator. Prune annually in late winter to an open-center shape to boost light penetration and fruit quality, and thin young fruit to improve size. Water deeply and consistently while fruit is developing.

plant_note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter to early spring; in mild-winter zones fall planting works well.
directions: English walnuts are monoecious — male (catkins) and female flowers occur on the same tree — and most cultivars are partially self-fertile, though a second tree improves yields. The tree produces juglone, a chemical that inhibits growth of many plants beneath its canopy; avoid planting near vegetables, berries, or sensitive ornamentals. Grafted Carpathian-strain trees are more cold-hardy and begin fruiting in 4–7 years; seedling trees may take 10+ years. Harvest in fall when hulls begin to crack and turn dark; remove husks promptly and cure nuts for 2–3 weeks before storing.

plant_note: Grown from a grafted tree; move outdoors after frost.
directions: The Eureka is California's go-to commercial lemon, a strong grower that produces tart, juicy fruit nearly year-round. It bears reliably and adapts well to container culture, making it a practical choice for both mild-climate yards and patios that overwinter indoors. Grow a grafted tree in full sun with well-drained soil and feed with a citrus fertilizer during active growth. Water deeply and let the surface dry between waterings, and shield the tree from frost.

plant_note: Plant container or bare-root figs after the last frost date when soil has warmed; in mild climates, fall planting works well.
directions: Common figs (the type grown in most gardens) are parthenocarpic and self-fertile — no pollinator or fig wasp is needed. In zones 6–7 protect the trunk and lower branches over winter by wrapping with burlap or mounding mulch; established roots often resprout even after top-kill. Figs bear two crops on some cultivars: a small breba crop on old wood in early summer and a main crop on new growth in late summer. Harvest when the neck droops, the skin splits slightly, and the fruit is soft and fragrant; a drop of syrup at the eye signals perfect ripeness.

plant_note: Plant after frost danger; grow in a pot to overwinter indoors in cold zones.
directions: Fignomenal is a true dwarf fig reaching only 2 to 3 feet, making it ideal for containers and small patios while still producing full-sized sweet fruit. Grow it in full sun in well-drained soil and water consistently, easing off as fruit ripens to concentrate flavor. Its compact size makes it easy to move indoors to a cool bright space for winter in colder regions. Prune lightly in late winter to shape and remove weak wood. Feed during the growing season for steady production of its self-fertile crop.

plant_note: Plant grafted trees in spring; finger lime tolerates brief frosts to about 22–25°F once established.
directions: Finger lime is an Australian rainforest citrus producing cylindrical finger-length fruits filled with spherical pulp vesicles that burst on the palate like caviar — highly prized in fine dining as a garnish. Grafted trees are strongly preferred over seedlings, which are slow to fruit and thorny; grafted plants begin fruiting in 2–3 years and are more manageable in size. The tree is self-fertile and does not require a pollinator; it performs well in containers and espalier forms. Fruit ripens in late autumn through winter; harvest when the skin (green, yellow, red, or black depending on variety) is fully colored and the fruit releases easily with gentle pressure — avoid overripening, as the vesicles lose their pop.

plant_note: Plant while dormant in early spring on a sturdy trellis in full sun.
directions: Flame Red Seedless is a vigorous grape producing sweet, juicy, ruby-red seedless berries in large clusters. Plant it in full sun in well-drained soil and provide a strong trellis or arbor to support the heavy vining growth. Train a permanent trunk and cordons the first years, then prune hard every dormant season to a few productive spurs or canes, as grapes fruit on the current year's growth. Water steadily while fruit swells but reduce moisture as harvest approaches for sweeter grapes. Good air circulation helps prevent mildew.

plant_note: Plant while dormant in early spring in full sun.
directions: Flavortop is a fast-growing nectarine producing large, juicy, well-balanced freestone fruit that ripens in mid to late summer. Plant it in full sun in deep, well-drained soil, avoiding low frost pockets that harm early bloom. It is self-fruitful, so one tree will bear without a partner. Prune yearly in late winter to an open-center form for light and airflow, and thin developing fruit to boost size and prevent limb breakage. Provide steady water while fruit develops, and manage peach leaf curl with dormant sprays if needed.

plant_note: Plant in spring; a dwarf, contorted form of the hardy orange.
directions: Flying Dragon is a dwarf, contorted selection of trifoliate orange with curved thorns and twisting stems, valued both ornamentally and as a size-controlling rootstock. It bears the same small, aromatic fruit and shares the species' extreme cold hardiness. Plant in full sun on well-drained soil, where its sculptural winter silhouette is a feature. Prune minimally to preserve the natural twisting form, and use the tart autumn fruit for preserves.

plant_note: Grown from a grafted or potted tree; plant after frost.
directions: Frantoio is a renowned Italian olive prized for producing high-quality oil and rich, dark fruit that ripens mid-season. This evergreen brings old-world Mediterranean character to the garden and tolerates heat and drought once established. Grow a young tree in full sun with lean, well-drained soil, watering sparingly since olives dislike wet roots. Prune to an open framework for light and air, and in marginal climates grow it in a container to shelter from hard freezes.

plant_note: Plant while dormant in early spring in full sun.
directions: Frost is a hardy peach bred for cooler climates, offering sweet, juicy fruit and notable resistance to peach leaf curl. Plant it in full sun in deep, well-drained soil, and it tolerates spring frosts better than many varieties. It is self-fruitful, producing a crop from a single tree. Prune annually in late winter to an open-center shape and thin the young fruit to improve size. Water deeply during fruit development for the best harvest.

plant_note: Plant while dormant in early spring in full sun.
directions: Fuji is a Japanese-bred apple beloved for its exceptionally sweet flavor and crisp, dense texture that stores well. Plant it in full sun in well-drained soil, and provide a compatible pollinating apple nearby since Fuji is not self-fertile. Prune each dormant season to a central-leader or open form for light and air, and thin fruit clusters to improve size. Water consistently during establishment and dry spells. Fuji ripens late in the season, so allow ample time on the tree for full sweetness.

plant_note: Plant after frost danger; grow in a container to move indoors in cold climates.
directions: Fukushu is a compact kumquat producing small oval citrus with an edible sweet rind and tart flesh, well suited to container growing. Grow it in full sun in rich, well-drained soil and water when the top inch dries, avoiding both drought stress and soggy roots. In climates with frost, keep it potted so it can move to a bright indoor spot for winter. Feed regularly with a citrus fertilizer during active growth. It is self-fertile, so a single tree will set fruit.

plant_note: Plant in spring in full sun with well-drained soil.
directions: Fuyu is a non-astringent persimmon whose sweet, firm fruit can be eaten crisp like an apple while still firm. Plant it in full sun in well-drained soil; it is self-fruitful and needs no pollinator. Water regularly during establishment and while fruit develops, then it tolerates some drought. Prune in dormancy to build a strong framework, since heavy fruit can break branches. The fruit ripens in fall and holds on the tree well after the leaves drop, creating an ornamental display.

plant_note: Plant tuber divisions or started plants after last frost; provide a strong trellis — vines reach 30 feet.
directions: Gac is a vigorous, tuberous-rooted perennial vine in the cucumber family that regrows each year from a starchy tuber in zones 9–11 and can be grown as an annual elsewhere; it requires a sturdy trellis or fence. Plants are dioecious, so grow at least one male and several female plants together; bees transfer pollen between the separate male and female flowers. The football-sized, brilliant orange-red fruit is among the richest known sources of lycopene and beta-carotene; the red aril surrounding the seeds is the edible portion, traditionally mixed into Vietnamese sticky rice (xôi gấc). Harvest when the skin is fully orange-red and the surface texture softens from spiny to smooth; fruit is mature approximately 4–5 months after planting.

plant_note: Plant while dormant in early spring in full sun.
directions: Gala is a widely grown apple with a sweet, mild flavor and firm, crisp flesh that suits fresh eating and cooking alike. Plant it in full sun in well-drained soil and include a compatible pollinating apple nearby, as Gala benefits from cross-pollination. Prune every dormant season for structure and light, and thin fruit clusters to improve size and prevent limb breakage. Water steadily during establishment and dry periods. Gala ripens in late summer to early fall, earlier than many other apples.

plant_note: Plant the dormant tree in early spring after the ground is workable; a self-fertile genetic dwarf, it fruits in a container or small bed.
directions: This genetic dwarf peach tops out around 4-6 feet, making it ideal for large containers or tight yards while still yielding full-size, sweet yellow freestone fruit. Site it in full sun with well-drained soil and give it consistent moisture through the growing season. Thin the young fruit to about 6 inches apart so remaining peaches size up well. Prune lightly in late winter to keep an open, vase-shaped canopy, and expect a harvest beginning two to three years after planting.

plant_note: Set out after all danger of frost; grow in a pot to overwinter indoors in cold climates.
directions: Introduced from Genoa, Italy, this productive lemon bears juicy, tangy fruit much like Eureka. Give it at least 8 hours of sun, fast-draining slightly acidic soil, and a dedicated citrus fertilizer during the growing season. In zones colder than 9 grow it in a container and move it indoors near a bright window before frost. Water deeply but let the top inch of soil dry between waterings to avoid root rot.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container-grown trees in early spring; site male trees downwind of living areas to avoid the malodorous female fruit.
directions: Ginkgo is dioecious — only female trees produce the edible nut, but a male is needed nearby for pollination; purchase named female cultivars (Tit, Megi, Oishi) or graft known female wood to avoid planting only males, which cannot be sexed until first flower at 20+ years. The outer fleshy seed coat (sarcotesta) smells powerfully of rancid butter when ripe and contains urushiol-related compounds — wear gloves when handling and process outdoors; the inner cream-colored nut inside the hard shell is the edible portion. Harvest when the fleshy coating has turned yellow-orange and fallen from the tree in autumn; clean immediately, dry the shells, and roast or steam before eating. Do not eat ginkgo nuts raw or in excessive quantities — they contain 4-O-methylpyridoxine, which is toxic in large amounts.

plant_note: Plant dormant in early spring; this compact columnar tree needs a second apple variety nearby for good pollination.
directions: This columnar Fruit Snacks apple grows tall and narrow, producing sweet snack-size fruit in as little as 2 feet of width, perfect for patios and small gardens. Plant in full sun with well-drained soil and provide a compatible apple pollinator blooming at the same time. Water regularly the first two seasons while roots establish. Its naturally slim habit needs minimal pruning beyond removing dead or crossing wood in late winter.

plant_note: Plant dormant in early spring; provide a compatible apple variety nearby for cross-pollination.
directions: The iconic tart green Granny Smith is prized for baking and long storage as well as fresh eating. It needs a long, warm season to fully ripen and a second apple variety nearby for pollination. Plant in full sun with deep, well-drained soil and water consistently while the tree establishes. Prune in late winter to maintain an open canopy that ripens fruit evenly and improves air circulation.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost risk has passed; requires the longest, hottest growing season of any common citrus.
directions: Grapefruit is self-fertile and produces best in the warmest citrus climates — the prolonged heat of south Texas and southern California delivers better sugar development than coastal or inland zones 9. Trees are large and vigorous; allow 20–25 feet between them and avoid overcrowding with other trees. Ruby Red and Flame are popular pink-fleshed varieties with good cold tolerance for zone 9. Fruit takes 12–18 months from bloom to full ripeness; taste-test from late autumn onward — the peel may remain slightly greenish even when the juice is sweet.

plant_note: Set out dormant bare-root trees in early spring before bud break.
directions: Greengage is an old European dessert plum prized for its honeyed, amber-green fruit. Give it a warm, sheltered site with fertile, moisture-retentive but well-drained soil to ripen its sugars fully. Many greengage selections benefit from a compatible European plum pollinator planted within about 50 feet. Prune only in summer to reduce the risk of silver leaf disease, and thin crowded fruit for best size and sweetness.

plant_note: Plant dormant in early spring; this self-fertile variety needs no second tree to set fruit.
directions: Hardired is a cold-tolerant, self-fertile nectarine known for smooth-skinned, freestone fruit and good resistance to bacterial spot and brown rot. Plant in full sun with well-drained soil and thin the fruit to about 6 inches apart for larger nectarines. Prune each late winter to an open-center vase shape to maximize light and airflow. Consistent watering during fruit development keeps the flesh juicy and sweet.

plant_note: Plant after frost danger passes; grow in a container to move indoors in zones colder than 9b.
directions: The world's most popular avocado, Hass produces rich, buttery fruit with pebbly skin that darkens as it ripens. Grow in full sun with fast-draining soil, as avocados are extremely sensitive to soggy roots. Water deeply but infrequently and protect from frost, growing in a large pot indoors where winters are cold. It is partly self-fertile, though a second avocado nearby improves fruit set.

plant_note: Plant bare-root shrubs/trees in late winter to early spring; hazelnuts establish readily and tolerate a range of soils.
directions: Hazelnuts require cross-pollination and are wind-pollinated, so plant at least two different cultivars within 20 feet of each other; pollen is shed in late winter before leaves emerge. American hazelnut (C. americana) is hardier and more disease-resistant than European types; many gardeners grow hybrid selections for both traits. Eastern filbert blight (Anisogramma anomala) is a serious fungal disease in the eastern United States — choose blight-resistant cultivars such as Jefferson or York for eastern plantings. Harvest in late summer when nuts begin to fall from the husks on their own.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container stock in early spring; heartnut adapts to a wide range of soils and is one of the most productive walnuts for northern growers.
directions: Heartnut is a variety of Japanese walnut bearing distinctively heart-shaped nuts that crack open cleanly along a suture to release a whole kernel — unlike black walnut, which requires significant effort to extract meat. Trees are monoecious and partially self-fertile but yield better with a second tree; they begin bearing in 4–7 years from grafted stock. Heartnut is notably less allelopathic than black walnut, making it more garden-friendly as a specimen tree. Nuts fall in clusters in late summer to early fall; hull and dry for 2–3 weeks before cracking and storing.

plant_note: Plant dormant in early spring; requires a second apple variety nearby for pollination.
directions: Honeycrisp is beloved for its explosively crisp texture and balanced sweet-tart flavor, and it is notably cold-hardy. It needs a compatible pollinating apple variety and thrives in cooler northern climates. Plant in full sun with rich, well-drained soil and water steadily through the season. Thin fruit clusters and prune in late winter to promote large, well-colored apples and reduce disease pressure.

plant_note: Plant the pup after frost; grow in a large pot to shelter indoors in cold climates.
directions: Also called Blue Java, this banana yields creamy, vanilla-flavored fruit and tolerates more cold and wind than most bananas. Give it full sun, rich soil, and abundant water plus regular feeding to fuel its fast growth. The bluish fruit ripens to pale yellow and is best eaten fresh. In marginal climates grow it in a large container and overwinter the plant in a bright, frost-free spot.

plant_note: Plant grafted trees in spring in a frost-free setting with a distinct dry season to stimulate bloom.
directions: Ilama is a semi-deciduous Annona from Mexico and Central America that tolerates drier conditions than most of its relatives, preferring a pronounced dry season to trigger leaf drop and subsequent flowering. Like other Annonaceae, flowers are protogynous and benefit strongly from hand-pollination — collect pollen in the afternoon from half-closed male-stage flowers and dab onto receptive stigmas of newly opened female-stage flowers in the morning. Fruit comes in pink-fleshed and white-fleshed strains; the pink-fleshed type has a slightly acidic flavor while the white is sweeter and more custard-like. Harvest when the skin has slightly separated at the segment lines and the fruit gives to gentle pressure; shelf life at room temperature is only 1–2 days.

plant_note: Set out after frost; ideal as a container plant to overwinter indoors.
directions: A younger, bushier form of the classic Improved Meyer, this lemon-orange hybrid produces sweet-tart, thin-skinned fruit on a compact plant well suited to pots. Grow in full sun with fast-draining, slightly acidic soil and feed with citrus fertilizer during active growth. Let the top inch of soil dry between deep waterings to prevent root rot. Bring it indoors to a bright window before frost in cooler zones.

plant_note: Set out after frost; grow in a container to overwinter indoors in cold climates.
directions: Improved Meyer is a low-maintenance lemon-orange hybrid that bears masses of fragrant, sweetly tart fruit, and it is nearly thornless and disease-resistant. It fruits reliably even for beginners and works beautifully as a container specimen. Provide full sun, fast-draining acidic soil, and regular citrus feeding through the growing season. In cold-winter areas keep it potted and move it indoors near bright light before the first frost.

plant_note: Plant container-grown stock in spring after all frost risk passes; jaboticaba is extremely slow-growing and may take 8–15 years from seed to first fruit.
directions: Jaboticaba produces grape-like berries directly on its trunk and main branches (cauliflory) in dramatic clusters; the fruit ripens just 20–25 days after flowering and can bear several times per year in frost-free climates. It is self-fertile and requires well-drained, slightly acidic soil (pH 5.0–6.5); chlorosis is common in alkaline soils and can be corrected with chelated iron and sulfur. Trees are slow-growing, making them well-suited to container culture where they can be overwintered in zone 8 or moved under frost protection. Harvest berries when they are entirely deep purple-black, soft, and sweet-tart — they ferment within 3–4 days at room temperature, so refrigerate or process promptly.

plant_note: Plant grafted or container-grown trees in spring in frost-free zones; jackfruit is damaged below 35°F.
directions: Plant in well-drained, deep soil — jackfruit is intolerant of waterlogged roots — in full sun with adequate space, as trees can reach 40–50 feet and produce fruit directly on the trunk and main branches (cauliflory). Trees are monoecious, with separate male and female flowers on the same tree, so a single tree can fruit, though planting two improves yields. Fruit can weigh up to 100 lbs; support developing fruit with a rope sling tied to a branch when they are the size of a cantaloupe. Harvest when the skin changes from green to yellowish-brown and the fruit emits a strong sweet aroma; immature fruit is used as a vegetable and ripe fruit eaten fresh or processed.

plant_note: Plant after frost; this evergreen blooms in fall and ripens fruit in spring.
directions: The evergreen Japanese loquat offers handsome, bold foliage and clusters of sweet-tart, apricot-like fruit that ripen in spring. It flowers in autumn, so late frosts can damage the developing crop in colder zones. Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained soil and water regularly while young. Thin fruit clusters for larger loquats and prune lightly after harvest to shape the canopy.

plant_note: Plant container-grown palms in spring after frost; avoid disturbing the root ball during transplanting.
directions: Jelly palm is one of the hardiest fruiting palms, surviving temperatures down to 12–15°F, and is well-suited to zones 8–11 in well-drained soils ranging from sandy to clay. The tree is monoecious, with male and female flowers on the same large branched inflorescence, so a single specimen fruits reliably without a pollinator. It produces abundant clusters of small orange-yellow drupes in summer; the sweet-tart pulp is rich in pectin and classically used for jelly, wine, and preserves. Allow fruit to fall naturally or gently shake clusters when most fruit has colored; process quickly as the thin-skinned fruit ferments rapidly after harvest.

plant_note: Plant in spring after the last frost date; jujubes establish quickly in warm soil.
directions: Jujube is exceptionally drought-tolerant and thrives in hot, dry climates where other fruits struggle; it prefers well-drained, even sandy soils and performs poorly with excessive moisture. Most cultivars are self-fertile, though a second variety increases yields. Li and Lang are the most widely planted in North America; Li produces large, round, date-like fruit while Lang is more elongated. Fruit is eaten fresh when crisp and apple-flavored or dried to a sweet, wrinkled date; harvest when skin turns from yellow-green to mahogany-red.

plant_note: Plant in spring after all frost risk has passed; key lime is the most cold-sensitive common citrus and is suitable only for zones 10–11 in the ground.
directions: Key lime is a small, thorny tree distinct from the larger Persian lime (Citrus latifolia) — its fruit is smaller, more aromatic, more acidic, and nearly seedless at full yellow ripeness. It is self-fertile and bears prolifically in truly tropical conditions; the tree is damaged by any frost and requires zone 10–11 for outdoor culture, though it adapts well to large containers for zone 9 growers who can move it inside. Harvest fruit while still light green to yellow-green, before it turns fully yellow; yellow fruit is riper and less acid but also more perishable. Apply citrus fertilizer three times per year and ensure excellent drainage to prevent root rot.

plant_note: Grown from a grafted tree; move outdoors after frost.
directions: Key lime, also called Mexican lime, produces small, intensely aromatic and juicy fruit beloved for pie, drinks, and marinades. This tender, somewhat thorny citrus stays compact and grows readily in a container that can shelter indoors in cold weather. Grow a grafted tree in full sun with excellent drainage and feed it a citrus fertilizer during the growing season. Water deeply, letting the top of the soil dry between waterings, and protect it from any frost.

plant_note: Set out after frost; well-suited to containers moved indoors for winter.
directions: A younger, compact form of the Key lime, this semi-tropical evergreen produces small, juicy green limes nearly year-round, famous for Key lime pie. Grow in full sun with fast-draining acidic soil and feed with citrus fertilizer during the growing season. Water deeply, letting the surface dry between soakings to avoid soggy roots. Its small size makes it perfect for patio pots that can be sheltered indoors in cold weather.

plant_note: Plant dormant in early spring; partly self-fertile but a second pear improves yields.
directions: Kieffer is a tough, adaptable pear valued for its heavy crops of crisp, gritty fruit that is excellent for canning, cooking, and preserves. It shows strong resistance to fire blight and tolerates a wide range of climates. Plant in full sun with well-drained soil and water regularly while establishing. Prune in late winter to build a strong scaffold and allow light into the canopy for even ripening.

plant_note: Plant in early spring before bud break; Korean pine is a cold-hardy alternative to Stone pine for producing large, commercially sized pine nuts in the northern U.S.
directions: Korean pine is a large, ornamental five-needle pine that produces the large, wingless pine nuts commonly sold in Asian markets — seeds are bigger than those of Italian stone pine and have a rich, buttery flavor. Trees are monoecious and bear cones that ripen over two growing seasons; significant cone production typically begins at 15–25 years from seed. Cones are harvested in early autumn when the scales begin to separate; spread cones on tarps to dry and then beat or tumble to dislodge seeds. The outer seed coat must be cracked to access the edible kernel, which is high in oil and should be refrigerated or frozen to prevent rancidity.

plant_note: Grown from a grafted or potted tree; plant after frost.
directions: Koroneiki is a productive Greek olive celebrated for generous harvests and a compact, easygoing habit that suits both orchards and patios. Its small fruit yields a robust, high-quality oil, and the silvery evergreen foliage lends year-round Mediterranean charm. Grow a young tree in full sun with sharp drainage and minimal water once established. Prune to keep light reaching the interior, and where winters are cold, grow it in a pot to move to shelter during freezes.

plant_note: Plant in spring; kumquats are the hardiest citrus relative and tolerate brief dips to 18°F.
directions: Kumquats are the hardiest of the citrus family and the only ones whose entire fruit — sweet peel and tangy flesh — is eaten whole. They are self-fertile and well-suited to container growing in zones 7–8, where they can be overwintered indoors. Unlike other citrus, kumquats go dormant more readily in winter, which improves their cold hardiness. Fruit ripens from late autumn through winter; harvest when fully orange and sweet-peel is prominent.

plant_note: Plant dormant in early spring; self-fertile, so no second cherry is needed.
directions: Lapins is a self-fertile sweet cherry producing large, firm, dark-red fruit without needing a pollinator, ideal for smaller gardens. Plant in full sun with deep, well-drained soil and water steadily as fruit develops. Prune in late winter or after harvest to keep an open canopy and reduce disease. Netting the tree near ripening helps protect the crop from birds.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost risk has passed; in frost-free zones plant any time but avoid midsummer planting.
directions: Lemons are self-fertile and can bloom and fruit year-round in frost-free climates; Eureka and Lisbon are the commercial standards, while Meyer lemon is hardier (damaged below 22°F) and better suited to containers in cooler climates. Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic soil and fertilize with citrus-specific fertilizer three times per year. In zones 8–9, grow in large containers and bring indoors when temperatures drop below 28°F. Fruit is ready when fully yellow, slightly soft, and the rind is thin — do not wait for it to fall off the tree.

plant_note: Plant in spring after the soil has warmed; tolerates heat and drought.
directions: Li is a large-fruited jujube cultivar producing sweet, apple-crisp fruit that can be eaten fresh or dried like dates. It thrives in hot summers and tolerates drought, alkaline soils, and neglect once established. Plant in full sun with excellent drainage; a second cultivar such as Lang nearby improves fruit set. Prune in late winter to remove suckers and thin the canopy, and harvest when the fruit turns fully brown.

plant_note: Plant in spring after all frost risk has passed; limes are the most cold-sensitive citrus.
directions: Key lime (C. aurantiifolia) is the most cold-sensitive common citrus and is reliable only in zones 10–11; Persian lime (C. latifolia) is somewhat hardier and suits zone 9 in sheltered spots. Both are self-fertile and produce almost continuously in warm climates with adequate water. Container culture in a large pot (25+ gallons) allows limes to be grown farther north and moved indoors during frost. Fruit is best harvested while still green and fully sized — if left to yellow on the tree it becomes dry and flavorless.

plant_note: Plant grafted trees in spring; limequat's kumquat parentage gives it excellent cold tolerance for a lime-flavored citrus.
directions: Limequat is a Key lime × kumquat hybrid combining the intense lime flavor of Citrus aurantiifolia with the cold hardiness of Fortunella, tolerating temperatures down to about 20°F — the hardiest of the lime-flavored citrus. Like kumquat, the entire fruit — sweet edible peel and sour juice pulp together — can be eaten whole or sliced into drinks and salads. Trees are self-fertile, compact, and well-suited to container culture in zones 7–8, where they can be moved indoors during hard freezes. The small oval to round fruits ripen from green to yellow in autumn through winter; harvest when fully yellow for the sweetest peel, or use while still green for the most intense lime flavor.

plant_note: Set out grafted trees after all danger of frost; grow in a large container where winters drop below 25 F.
directions: Lisbon is a vigorous, cold-tolerant lemon that fruits heavily and produces classic tart, juicy fruit almost year-round in warm regions. Plant in full sun in fast-draining soil and keep the graft union above the soil line. In zones colder than 9, grow in a pot that can be moved indoors or to a bright greenhouse for winter. Feed with a citrus-specific fertilizer during active growth and water deeply but allow the top inch of soil to dry between waterings. Thin fruit lightly on young trees so the branches are not overloaded.

plant_note: Plant after frost; this dwarf fig stays small enough for containers that can be sheltered over winter in colder zones.
directions: Little Miss Figgy is a naturally dwarf fig reaching only about 4 to 5 feet, making it ideal for patios and small gardens. It produces sweet, deep-purple figs on new growth, often with a light second crop later in the season. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and water consistently while fruit is sizing up. In zones below 7, grow it in a container and move it to an unheated garage or shed during hard freezes. Prune lightly in late winter to shape the plant and encourage fruiting wood.

plant_note: Plant grafted trees after all cold weather has passed; young trees are cold-sensitive and need protection until the trunk is well established.
directions: Plant grafted trees in deep, well-drained, slightly acidic soil (pH 5.5–6.5) in full sun; longan will not tolerate standing water or saturated roots. A brief winter cool-dry period — temperatures in the mid-40s°F for several weeks — is essential to trigger reliable flowering, making coastal south Florida and extreme south Texas the two viable zones in the continental United States. Fruit clusters ripen in summer and should be harvested by cutting whole panicles with scissors when the skin turns tan to light brown and the flesh is translucent and sweet. Prune annually after harvest to keep the canopy to a manageable height and promote new fruiting wood.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost risk has passed; in zones 9–11 fall planting is preferred.
directions: Loquat is an evergreen tree that flowers in fall and winter and ripens its fruit in late winter to early spring — one of the earliest fruiting trees of the season. Most varieties are self-fertile, though two trees increase crop size. Fruit clusters can be thinned at golf-ball size to produce fewer but larger fruit. Harvest when fruit is fully golden-yellow to orange, slightly soft, and sweet-tart; the thin skin is edible but seeds are large and should be discarded. Trees are cold-hardy to about 12°F but flowers and developing fruit are damaged below 28°F.

plant_note: Plant in spring after all frost risk has passed; in zones 10–11, fall planting after summer heat breaks is preferred.
directions: Plant a grafted or air-layered tree in deep, well-drained, slightly acidic soil (pH 5.0–6.5) in a site sheltered from salt spray and drying winter wind; lychee cannot tolerate waterlogged roots at any age. Trees require a cool-dry stress period of 4–6 weeks in late autumn — nights reliably below 59°F — to trigger flower bud development, so hold irrigation during that window rather than watering on a regular schedule. Fruit clusters develop rapidly after bloom and benefit from thinning to 3–5 fruits per panicle when marble-sized to improve final fruit size and reduce limb stress. Harvest clusters when the rough, bumpy skin has turned fully red to pinkish-red and the aroma is distinctly floral and sweet, typically 90–112 days after bloom.

plant_note: Plant grafted trees in spring after frost; macadamias grow best in frost-free zones with well-distributed rainfall.
directions: Macadamias are self-fertile but cross-pollination between two or more varieties meaningfully increases yields; grafted trees start bearing at 3–5 years, while seedlings can take 10–15 years. The tree prefers well-drained, slightly acid soil (pH 5.5–6.5) and is sensitive to root disturbance — plant without disturbing the root ball. Trees produce racemes of cream-white flowers that fall and create a slippery surface; harvesting is done by collecting nuts that have dropped naturally as the green husk splits and the nut falls. Shell and dry nuts for 3–4 weeks before cracking; the shells require a commercial or heavy-duty nut cracker as they are among the hardest of any food nut.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost; mandarins are among the hardiest citrus, tolerating brief dips to 24°F.
directions: Mandarins (including tangerines, clementines, and satsumas) are the most cold-hardy of the common sweet citrus, with satsumas surviving to about 15°F and thriving in zone 8. Most are self-fertile, though some varieties set more fruit with a compatible pollinator nearby. Fruit tends to ripen earlier in the season than oranges — from October through January depending on variety — and the loose peel and sweet-tart flavor make them popular for fresh eating. Avoid overwatering, as mandarins are somewhat drought-tolerant and susceptible to root rot in wet soils.

plant_note: Plant grafted trees in spring after all frost risk has passed; in zones 10b–11 the rainy-season break in early spring is the optimal planting window.
directions: Plant grafted trees of named cultivars (Kent, Tommy Atkins, Haden, Keitt) rather than seedlings; grafted trees fruit in 3–5 years and produce fruit true to type. Mangoes require a cool-dry period of 4–6 weeks during winter — nights below 60°F without frost — to trigger uniform flowering; excess rainfall during bloom causes anthracnose and poor fruit set. Prune after harvest each year by removing one-quarter to one-third of the outer canopy to keep the tree at a manageable height and promote fresh fruiting wood. Fruit is ripe when the shoulders near the stem fill out, color develops according to the cultivar, and the flesh yields slightly to pressure; the stem end is often the last to color.

plant_note: Plant in spring in a site with reliably warm temperatures year-round; mangosteen is extremely sensitive to cold and drought stress.
directions: Mangosteen is one of the slowest-growing and most demanding tropical fruit trees, requiring deep, rich, well-drained, acidic soil (pH 5.0–6.5), humidity above 80%, and temperatures that never fall below 40°F or rise above 100°F. It is apomictic — seed-grown trees are genetically identical to the mother, eliminating the need for pollinators, though fruit set is parthenocarpic in cultivation. Young trees require shade for the first several years; gradually transition to full sun as the canopy develops. Harvest when the thick purple rind has deepened to a dark maroon-purple and yields slightly to thumb pressure, typically 5–6 months after fruit set.

plant_note: Plant only where winters stay above freezing; young trees are very frost-sensitive and benefit from wind protection.
directions: Mauritius is a productive lychee bearing rough-skinned red fruit with sweet, fragrant, translucent flesh. It needs a frost-free subtropical or tropical climate with a brief cool, dry period to trigger flowering. Plant in full sun in deep, acidic, well-drained soil and protect young trees from wind and cold. Water generously during flowering and fruit development but ease off during the winter rest period. Mulch heavily to keep the shallow roots moist and feed with a balanced fertilizer through the growing season.

plant_note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter to early spring while dormant; fall planting suits zones 6–8.
directions: Medlar is a small, gnarled tree that tolerates a wide range of soils and needs minimal pruning beyond removing crossing branches; it is largely self-fertile and rarely needs a pollinator. Fruit is harvested in late autumn after the first light frost when the skin has turned brown and slightly wrinkled, then 'bleted' (stored at room temperature for 2–4 weeks) until the flesh softens to a custard-like consistency and loses its harsh astringency. The bletted fruit tastes of apple butter with wine overtones and is traditionally made into jelly or cheese. Few pests bother medlar, making it one of the easiest heritage fruits for a low-spray garden.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost risk passes; Meyer lemon is the hardiest true-fruiting lemon and can be grown in containers in zones 7–8 with winter protection.
directions: Meyer lemon is a natural hybrid between a lemon and either a mandarin or sweet orange, resulting in a thinner-skinned, less acidic, and more cold-tolerant fruit than standard lemon varieties; it tolerates brief dips to about 22°F once established. It is self-fertile, blooms nearly year-round in frost-free climates, and is exceptionally well-suited to container culture — a 20-gallon pot allows the plant to be moved indoors in zones 7–8. Plant in fast-draining citrus mix or well-amended garden soil and fertilize with citrus-specific fertilizer in late winter, late spring, and midsummer. Fruit is ripe when the skin turns deep golden-yellow and yields to gentle pressure; the rind is thin and zests well for culinary use.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost danger has passed and soil is warm, or start indoors in short-season areas.
directions: Direct sow this compact heirloom melon in warm soil, or start indoors a few weeks early in short-season climates. Introduced in 1948, it produces loads of small 4 inch fruits that are sweet and juicy right to the rind, on short vines well suited to small gardens and containers. Give plants full sun and steady moisture while fruits develop, then ease off watering as they ripen to concentrate the sugars. Harvest when the fruit slips easily from the vine and smells fragrant.

plant_note: Best planted dormant in early spring in a warm, sunny position.
directions: Mirabelles are small, round, golden plums famed in Lorraine for tarts and eau-de-vie. Plant in full sun on fertile, free-draining soil and provide shelter from late frosts that can damage the early blossom. Most are self-fertile, so a single tree will crop, though a European plum companion boosts yields. Thin the abundant fruit set and prune lightly in summer to keep the canopy open.

plant_note: Plant in spring; grows well in large containers that can be overwintered indoors in zones 9 and below.
directions: Plant in highly acidic soil (pH 4.5–5.5), which is critical — miracle fruit performs poorly above pH 6.0 and the leaves turn chlorotic in alkaline conditions. It requires consistently warm temperatures and suffers at any temperature below 40°F; container culture in large pots using acidic potting mix is the most practical approach outside zones 10–11. The small red berries contain miraculin, a glycoprotein that binds to taste receptors and makes sour foods taste sweet for 30–60 minutes after eating one fruit; harvest when berries are fully red and soft. Although individual plants are self-fertile, cross-pollination by insects improves berry set, so grow multiple plants together.

plant_note: Grown from a grafted or potted tree; plant after frost.
directions: The Mission olive, first cultivated in California, is a versatile favorite valued for both flavorful oil and classic table olives. It is a hardy, resilient evergreen that adapts to a range of conditions and delivers reliable harvests for home growers. Plant a young tree in full sun with well-drained soil and water sparingly once it establishes. Prune for an open canopy and good airflow, and in cold-winter regions grow it as a container specimen that can be sheltered from hard freezes.

plant_note: Plant dormant bare-root trees in early spring.
directions: Montmorency is the classic self-fertile tart pie cherry, so a single tree will crop heavily on its own. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, avoiding frost pockets that damage the early bloom. It is more compact and cold-hardy than sweet cherries, making it well suited to northern gardens. Prune to an open center in late winter and net the ripening fruit against birds.

plant_note: Sow seed or plant cuttings after last frost; moringa grows explosively in heat and can be coppiced to manage size in zone 9.
directions: Sow seeds 1 in deep directly into warm, well-drained soil — moringa dislikes transplanting due to its long taproot. It grows extraordinarily fast in hot, dry conditions and tolerates poor, sandy soils; avoid over-watering or heavy clay, which causes stem and root rot. In zone 9 the tree may freeze to the ground in hard winters but regrows vigorously from the root crown each spring. Harvest young leaves, flowers, and immature seed pods (drumsticks) regularly; coppicing the main stem at 3–4 ft encourages dense, bushy regrowth with more leaf area at reachable height.

plant_note: Plant in early spring; mulberries establish readily from container stock.
directions: Red mulberry (native to eastern North America) and white mulberry (Morus alba, from Asia) are both grown for fruit; white mulberry is more tolerant of heat and alkaline soils. Most mulberries are self-fertile or near-enough so that a single tree crops well. Trees require very little care once established — no regular spraying or thinning is needed. Fruit ripens over several weeks and drops when ripe; lay a tarp beneath the canopy for easy collection, as the juice stains pavement and clothing permanently.

plant_note: Plant in spring in a frost-free site; nance tolerates poor, well-drained soils exceptionally well.
directions: Nance is a tough, drought-tolerant shrub-tree native to tropical America that thrives on poor, acidic, sandy or rocky soils where other fruit trees struggle, and is among the most fire-tolerant tropical fruit plants. It is partially self-fertile, but fruit set improves significantly when multiple plants are grown together; bees are the primary pollinators. The small yellow drupes ripen over an extended season and have a distinctive pungent, waxy, cheese-like aroma that intensifies when processed; they are consumed fresh, frozen in syrup, fermented into a popular Central American drink called chicha, or cooked into preserves. Harvest when the fruit has fully yellowed and drops freely from the clusters, then refrigerate or process immediately.

plant_note: Plant in spring in a frost-free coastal or tropical setting; natal plum is exceptionally tolerant of salt spray and wind.
directions: Natal plum is a spiny, salt-tolerant, evergreen shrub from coastal South Africa widely used as a fruiting hedge or ornamental in zones 9–11; only the ripe fruit is edible — all other plant parts, including the milky latex, are toxic. Plants are self-fertile and bear fragrant white flowers year-round in frost-free climates, producing a steady supply of oval crimson fruits about the size of a large olive. The sweet, cranberry-like fruit is excellent eaten fresh, as jam, or in desserts; harvest when it has turned fully deep red and yields to light pressure. In zone 9 site against a south-facing wall or near structures for frost protection.

plant_note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter to early spring before bud break.
directions: Nectarines are essentially smooth-skinned peaches and share the same culture — self-fertile, open-center pruning, and annual heading-back of one-year-old wood. Because nectarines lack the peach's fuzzy skin barrier they are somewhat more susceptible to brown rot and bacterial spot in humid climates, so good air circulation is critical. Chill requirements range from 250 to 900 hours depending on cultivar; select accordingly. Fruit is ready when it gives slightly to thumb pressure and the background color has shifted from green to creamy yellow.

plant_note: Plant in spring after the last frost; in Mediterranean-climate zones fall planting is also suitable.
directions: Olives thrive in the classic Mediterranean climate — hot, dry summers and mild, wet winters — and are extremely drought-tolerant once established, but they require 200–400 chill hours to set fruit reliably. Most cultivars are self-fertile, but cross-pollination with a second variety improves yields significantly. Trees are long-lived and slow-growing; minimal pruning is needed beyond thinning for light penetration. For table olives, harvest when fruit is still green-to-straw colored; for oil, pick when about half the fruit has turned black.

plant_note: Plant after frost; grow in a container in zones colder than 8 so it can shelter indoors over winter.
directions: The olive is a slow-growing Mediterranean evergreen with silvery foliage, gnarled character, and small fruit ripening from green to black. It thrives in hot, dry conditions and needs a stretch of winter chill to set fruit well. Plant in full sun in fast-draining, even rocky soil and water deeply but infrequently once established. In zones below 8, grow it in a large pot and move it to a cool, bright indoor space during hard freezes. Prune in late winter to open the canopy and cure the fruit before eating, as fresh olives are bitter.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost risk passes; in frost-free zones, spring or early fall planting is ideal.
directions: Sweet oranges (Citrus sinensis) are self-fertile; navel oranges and many blood oranges are seedless because they are parthenocarpic. Most oranges need warm nights to develop sweetness and cannot be grown outdoors north of zone 9; the Hamlin and Navel types are slightly more cold-tolerant. Fruit can stay on the tree for weeks to months after ripening, extending harvest, but may re-green in warm weather without losing quality. Apply a balanced citrus fertilizer in late winter, late spring, and midsummer for best production.

plant_note: One of the more cold-tolerant citrus; grown from a grafted tree.
directions: The Owari satsuma is the early bird of citrus, ripening juicy, seedless, easy-peel mandarins in early winter. It is among the most cold-tolerant citrus, making it a good choice for growers in warm-temperate regions or in containers that overwinter indoors. Grow a grafted tree in full sun with well-drained soil and feed with a citrus fertilizer during the growing season. Water deeply but allow the surface to dry between waterings, and give some frost protection during hard cold snaps.

plant_note: Grown from a grafted tree; more frost-tolerant than most avocados.
directions: Pancho is a cold-hardy Mexican-type avocado that shrugs off chills that would damage tender varieties, extending avocado growing into cooler regions. It is low-maintenance and rewards patient growers with creamy, homegrown fruit. Plant a grafted tree in full sun with deep, fast-draining soil, since avocados are extremely sensitive to soggy roots and root rot. Water regularly but never let the tree stand in water, mulch well, and protect young trees during their first few winters.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8 weeks before last frost and transplant after all cold risk has passed; even a light frost kills papaya plants.
directions: Papaya is one of the fastest-bearing fruit plants — grafted or transplanted seedlings can fruit within 6–12 months of planting. It is highly sensitive to frost, waterlogged soil, and root disturbance; plant in well-raised, excellently drained beds and never let roots sit in water even briefly. Most commercial papaya is hermaphroditic (self-fertile), allowing a single tree to produce fruit; dioecious types require a male plant within 20 feet. Cut the growing tip back at 3–4 ft to encourage a compact, easily harvested form. Harvest when at least one-quarter of the skin has turned yellow; ripen fully at room temperature and refrigerate only after fully ripe.

plant_note: Plant container-grown pawpaws in late spring after frost; avoid bare-root stock as the taproot is easily damaged.
directions: Pawpaws require two genetically distinct seedlings or named cultivars for cross-pollination; hand-pollinating flowers with a small brush greatly improves fruit set. Young trees need shade for the first one to two seasons — plant under a light canopy or use a shade cloth — but mature trees prefer full sun to fruit well. The taproot makes transplanting difficult, so use container-grown stock and disturb roots as little as possible. Fruit ripens in late summer to fall and goes from firm to custardy-soft within days; refrigerate immediately after picking as shelf life is very short.

plant_note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter before bud swell; in zones 8–9 fall planting is also successful.
directions: Most peaches are self-fertile, so a single tree will produce fruit, but they need 600–1000 chill hours — choose low-chill varieties (below 400 hours) for zones 8–9. Train to an open-center (vase) shape to allow sunlight deep into the canopy, and prune out about one-third of last year's growth each dormant season because peaches only fruit on one-year-old wood. Thin fruit to 6–8 inches apart after June drop to size up remaining fruit and reduce limb breakage. Harvest when the green ground color fades to cream and the fruit yields to gentle pressure.

plant_note: Plant in frost-free conditions in spring; peach palm requires consistently warm, humid conditions.
directions: Peach palm is clump-forming, producing multiple spiny stems from the base; plant suckers or container-grown plants in rich, moist, well-drained soil in full sun with consistent irrigation. It is dioecious in the wild but many cultivated selections are monoecious and self-productive; planting in clusters of 3–5 stems improves pollination and yield. The starchy, oily orange-red drupes are rich in beta-carotene and must be boiled or roasted before eating as raw fruit contains irritating calcium oxalate crystals. Harvest bunches when fruit has turned fully orange to red and softens slightly; boil or cook within a few days as the starchy fruit does not store well raw.

plant_note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter to early spring while dormant; fall planting works in zones 7–9.
directions: European pears require 700–1200 chill hours and almost always need a second compatible cultivar for adequate pollination; Bosc and Anjou cross well with each other. Pears are prone to fire blight, so choose resistant varieties and prune out any infected wood well below the canker margin. Train to a central leader and avoid excess nitrogen, which promotes lush growth susceptible to blight. Pick pears while still firm and ripen indoors at room temperature — they go mealy if left on the tree too long.

plant_note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter to early spring; pecans have a deep taproot — handle carefully and plant immediately.
directions: Pecans are protandrous or protogynous depending on variety — pollen shed and receptive female flowers rarely overlap on the same tree — so always plant at least two cultivars of opposite types (Type I with Type II) to ensure cross-pollination. Northern varieties (Hark, Kanza, Lakota) are bred for shorter growing seasons and suit zones 5–7; southern varieties need 250+ frost-free days. Pecans are heavy zinc feeders; apply zinc foliar spray in spring if leaves show rosette symptoms. Harvest when shucks split open in autumn and nuts fall freely.

plant_note: Plant dormant, grafted trees in early spring; expect long establishment before cropping.
directions: Perry pears are tannic, high-acid cultivars grown to ferment into perry, the pear equivalent of cider. These are famously long-lived, slow-to-fruit trees that eventually reach great size, so give them ample space and patience. Plant at least two varieties for pollination in deep soil and full sun. Little pruning is required once a strong framework is established; harvest the astringent fruit slightly underripe and let it sweat before pressing.

plant_note: Plant after frost danger; grow in a container where winters drop below 30 F.
directions: Persian Bearss is a prolific, thornless lime producing juicy, seedless fruit perfect for cooking and drinks. It is one of the more cold-tolerant limes but still needs protection below freezing. Plant in full sun in fast-draining soil and keep the graft union above the soil line. Water deeply and let the top inch of soil dry between waterings, feeding with a citrus fertilizer during active growth. In zones colder than 9, grow in a large pot that can move to a bright indoor location for winter.

plant_note: Plant in spring; phalsa is coppiced annually in India but can be trained as a small tree in warmer zones.
directions: Phalsa is a fast-growing shrubby tree from South Asia that tolerates drought, heat, and a range of soils, fruiting reliably within 1–2 years of planting — one of the quickest-bearing subtropical fruits. In traditional cultivation it is heavily pruned (nearly to the ground) each year after fruiting, which promotes vigorous new fruiting wood; in zone 9 this also serves as winter frost management. The small, dark purple-red drupes ripen over several weeks in summer and have a refreshing sweet-sour flavor prized in Indian cooling drinks (phalsa sherbet) and jams. The fruit is extremely perishable — harvest when fully dark purple and soft and use within 24 hours; it does not store or ship well.

plant_note: Plant container-grown stock in spring after frost danger; established plants tolerate brief dips to 15°F once mature.
directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun; pineapple guava tolerates drought, coastal salt spray, and moderate alkalinity better than most subtropical fruits. Most selections set better crops with a second nearby cultivar for cross-pollination, though some are self-fruitful. The showy red-stamened flowers are edible and mild-flavored — harvest petals before they fall for salads. Fruits drop naturally when ripe in autumn; check the ground daily and collect before birds and insects take them, then ripen indoors for 3–5 days until they yield to gentle pressure.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost; pistachios need hot, dry summers and cold winters — a very specific climate.
directions: Pistachio is dioecious — you must plant at least one male tree (typically Peters variety) for every 8–10 female trees; pollen is wind-carried. The tree requires 700–1000 chill hours in winter and blisteringly hot, dry summers (100°F+ is ideal) to ripen nuts properly, making it poorly suited to humid or mild-summer climates. Trees are extremely drought-tolerant and actually prefer alkaline, well-drained soils. Nuts are ready when hulls turn from green to red-pink and slip off easily; harvest by shaking limbs and collect nuts off tarps laid below.

plant_note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter to early spring; fall planting suits zones 7 and warmer.
directions: European plums (Prunus domestica) are often self-fertile, but Japanese plums (P. salicina) generally need a compatible pollinator planted nearby within 50 feet. Prune European types to a central leader and Japanese types to an open center; remove crossing branches each dormant season. Thin fruit clusters to 3–4 inches apart after natural fruit drop to encourage larger size. Pick when fruit achieves full color and yields gently to pressure — overripe plums are soft and ferment quickly.

plant_note: Plant bare-root in early spring while dormant.
directions: The pluot is a complex plum-apricot hybrid skewed toward plum, prized for intensely sweet, richly flavored fruit. Plant in full sun and deep, well-drained soil, and provide a suitable Japanese plum or another pluot for cross-pollination. Train to an open vase form and thin fruit to 4-6 inches apart to prevent overbearing. In cold regions protect the early blooms from frost, as flowering can precede that of many plums.

plant_note: Plant in spring after the last frost; in zones 9–11 fall planting is also suitable.
directions: Pomegranates are self-fertile and thrive in hot, dry conditions with well-drained soil; they tolerate drought but produce sweeter, larger fruit with regular deep watering from bloom through harvest. In zone 7, mulch the root zone heavily and site against a south-facing wall to protect against winter kill. Allow plants to develop as a multi-stemmed shrub or train to a single trunk by removing suckers. Fruit is ready in fall when it turns deep red, sounds hollow when tapped, and the crown (calyx) is prominent.

plant_note: Plant grafted trees in spring after frost risk has passed; pomelo requires a long, hot growing season and performs best in zones 9b–11.
directions: Pomelo is the largest citrus fruit — often the size of a large cantaloupe — and the ancestor of the grapefruit. It is less bitter than grapefruit, with a sweeter, milder flavor, and a very thick pith that insulates the segments. Trees are self-fertile but cross-pollination with another variety increases fruit set. Pomelos require heat to develop sweetness and need a longer season than most citrus — fruit set in spring is ready from October through February of the following year. Apply citrus fertilizer three times annually and maintain consistent moisture during fruit development; drought stress during sizing causes dry, pithy fruit.

plant_note: Plant after frost; ideal as a container tree that overwinters indoors in cold climates.
directions: Ponderosa is a lemon-citron hybrid famous for enormous, grapefruit-sized, thick-skinned fruit with tart, seedy flesh. It is compact and free-fruiting, making it a rewarding container tree for patios and sunrooms. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and keep the graft union above the soil surface. Water deeply, letting the surface dry between waterings, and feed with a citrus fertilizer during the growing season. In zones colder than 9, bring the pot indoors to a bright spot before frost arrives.

plant_note: Plant the dormant or potted tree in spring; grown from a grafted tree.
directions: Prairie Fire is an ornamental crabapple that puts on a spectacular show of magenta spring blossoms followed by fiery fall foliage and small persistent fruit that feeds birds. Disease-resistant and adaptable, it makes a durable landscape tree for cold and temperate gardens. Plant a grafted tree in full sun with well-drained soil, watering regularly until established. Prune in late winter to shape and remove crossing branches, and enjoy the tiny fruit as wildlife forage or for jelly.

plant_note: Plant in spring on a sturdy trellis; this self-fertile variety fruits without a male vine.
directions: Prolific is a self-fertile, cold-hardy hardy kiwi bearing sweet, grape-size fruit with smooth green skin you can eat whole, no peeling required. Train the vigorous vine onto a strong trellis or arbor in full sun with rich, well-drained soil. Water consistently during the growing season and prune in winter to control its rampant growth and encourage fruiting spurs. Fruit typically arrives two to three years after planting.

plant_note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter to early spring before bud break.
directions: Quince is self-fertile and one of the easier tree fruits to grow, tolerating heavier and wetter soils than apples or pears. It needs 300–500 chill hours, which suits zones 5–9 broadly. The fruit is astringent and almost always cooked rather than eaten raw; it turns pink when heated with sugar and is prized for marmalade, paste (membrillo), and jelly. Harvest in late autumn when the fruit turns fully golden-yellow and fragrant, before the first hard freeze.

plant_note: Plant grafted trees in spring after all cold risk has passed; young trees are extremely frost-sensitive.
directions: Plant in deep, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil (pH 5.5–6.5) in a frost-free location; rambutan is damaged at any temperature below 40°F and is one of the most cold-sensitive common fruit trees. Most cultivars bear both male and hermaphrodite flowers on separate trees, so plant at least one male tree alongside female or hermaphrodite fruiting trees to ensure pollination. Trees require consistent rainfall or irrigation — roughly 60–150 inches of annual precipitation equivalent — especially during fruit development, with a short dry period to stimulate flowering. Harvest clusters when the leathery spines (soft spinterns) have turned fully red or yellow depending on variety and the flesh is translucent, sweet, and separates cleanly from the seed.

plant_note: Plant grafted trees in spring; Rangpur lime is hardier than true limes and withstands brief frosts to about 25°F.
directions: Rangpur lime is actually a mandarin-lime hybrid (not a true lime) prized for its mandarin orange appearance, intensely sour juice, and significantly greater cold-hardiness than Key or Persian limes, making it a practical choice for zone 8 citrus gardens and container culture. It is self-fertile, productive as a single tree, and bears fruit nearly year-round in warm climates; fruit ripens to a deep orange-red color similar to a tangerine. Use the juice as a direct substitute for regular lime juice in cocktails, marinades, and curries — it is slightly more complex and aromatic. Harvest when the fruit has turned fully orange-red and yields to gentle pressure; fruit can be held on the tree for several weeks after ripening without significant quality loss.

plant_note: Plant grafted or seedling trees in spring in a frost-free tropical site; biriba is among the faster-fruiting Annona relatives.
directions: Rollinia / biriba is an Annona relative from Amazonia that prefers deep, fertile, consistently moist soil and produces fruit faster than cherimoya or soursop under hot, humid conditions. Like other Annonaceae, the flowers exhibit protogynous dichogamy — the female stage opens first, closes, then reopens in the male stage — so hand-pollination at the correct time greatly increases fruit set: collect pollen from half-open flowers in the afternoon and apply to fully open receptive stigmas the following morning. The soft, custard-flavored fruit is covered in soft yellow spines and must be handled very gently — it bruises and ferments within hours of reaching peak ripeness. Harvest when the spines have softened and yellowed and the fruit gives noticeably to pressure; consume immediately or refrigerate for no more than 24 hours.

plant_note: Plant dormant trees in early spring once the soil is workable.
directions: Romeo and Juliet are a pair of compact, extremely cold-hardy bush cherries that produce sweet, robust fruit good for snacking and baking. Plant both varieties near each other in full sun with well-drained soil to improve cross-pollination and yields. Space plants 5-8 feet apart and water consistently while young to establish strong roots. Prune lightly in late winter to maintain an open, productive shape. These dwarf trees stay small enough for backyard gardens and fruit reliably even in harsh winter regions.

plant_note: Grown from a grafted or potted tree; move outdoors after frost.
directions: Ruby Supreme is a tropical guava that bears fragrant fruit with sweet, pink-red flesh rich in flavor and nutrition. This fast-growing evergreen fruits young and adapts well to containers, making it accessible even where winters are cold. Grow in full sun with rich, well-drained soil, and feed regularly during the warm season for the heaviest crops. Keep the soil evenly moist, prune to maintain a manageable size, and bring potted trees indoors before frost.

plant_note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; tolerates humid, warm climates well.
directions: The sand pear is a vigorous, disease-resistant Asian pear species valued for its gritty, juicy fruit and tolerance of hot, humid summers. Plant two compatible cultivars for cross-pollination in full sun and deep, well-drained soil. Train young trees to a central leader and thin fruit clusters to one per spur to improve size. Its strong fire-blight resistance makes it a dependable choice where European pears fail.

plant_note: Plant bare-root while dormant in early spring or in fall in mild climates.
directions: Plant this popular Japanese plum in deep, well-drained loam and full sun, setting the graft union a few inches above the soil line. Though partially self-fruitful, yields improve dramatically with a second Japanese plum nearby for cross-pollination. Prune to an open-center vase shape during the dormant season to admit light and air. Thin heavy fruit sets to 4-6 inches apart to size up the crop and prevent limb breakage.

plant_note: Plant in the humid tropics; intolerant of any frost.
directions: Santol is a fast-growing Southeast Asian tree bearing round, downy fruit with sweet-sour, cottony pulp surrounding large seeds. It requires a hot, humid, frost-free climate and deep, fertile, well-drained soil with steady moisture. Plant in full sun, allowing ample space for the large canopy to develop. Grafted trees fruit within a few years; harvest when the skin turns yellow and use the pulp fresh or in preserves and candies.

plant_note: Plant grafted trees in spring; sapodilla tolerates salt spray and wind, making it useful for coastal zones 10–11.
directions: Sapodilla is an exceptionally wind- and salt-tolerant tropical tree; it is one of the few fruiting trees that thrives in coastal south Florida and can tolerate brief periods of flooding. Trees are slow-growing and long-lived with extremely hard, durable wood. Grafted plants fruit in 4–8 years; seedlings may take a decade. The fruit is fully ripe when the skin loses its sandy, rough texture, the green coloring fades to light tan, and the flesh yields to slight pressure — unripe fruit contains astringent latex. Harvest by rubbing the skin; if the scurf (grainy coating) rubs off easily and flesh inside is reddish-brown and translucent, it is ready.

plant_note: Plant in spring; among the most cold-tolerant of the sweet citrus.
directions: The satsuma is a nearly seedless, cold-hardy mandarin that ripens early and peels with ease. It survives brief dips near the low twenties Fahrenheit once established, making it the best citrus for marginal areas.
