In zones 8–9, plants die to the ground after frost but reliably regrow from the roots; mulch heavily to protect the root zone through winter.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; do not transplant until nights are reliably above 55°F as cold soil stunts growth.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in warm starting mix at 75–85°F; germination is swift when soil is adequately warm. Transplant after night temperatures stay above 55°F and pinch growing tips immediately to promote bushy growth. Regular pinching and removal of flower spikes prolongs the leaf harvest and delays bitterness; harvest leaves in the morning after dew has dried for the most aromatic result. Fresh or dried leaves are used in adaptogenic teas, Ayurvedic preparations, and as a sacred plant in Hindu tradition.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; surface-sow as seeds are small and benefit from light for germination.
directions: Surface-sow onto moist mix 6–8 weeks before transplanting; horehound germinates readily in warm soil but can be slow and erratic. Plant in dry, well-drained, even rocky or sandy soil where most plants would struggle — fertile, moist conditions cause leggy, weak growth. Harvest stems and leaves before flowering for the most potent bitterness, bundling for drying or infusing fresh into candy and cough syrups. Shear plants back in late summer to keep growth compact and prevent excessive self-seeding.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost once soil is warm; can also be started indoors 4 weeks early for a longer season.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in well-drained soil after last frost; thin to 18–24 in apart as huacatay grows into a tall, upright plant. This Andean herb — essential in Peruvian cuisine for dishes like ocopa sauce and anticuchos marinades — has a complex flavor combining mint, tarragon, and citrus. Plants are day-length sensitive and flower in late summer to fall; harvest leaves and stem tips throughout the vegetative season before flowering commences. Grow in full sun with moderate fertility; the plant is drought-tolerant once established and spreads by self-seeding in warm climates.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; surface-sow or cover lightly with 1/8 in of fine mix.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep indoors in warm starting mix; germination is fast and reliable at 65–70°F. Plant in lean, well-drained, slightly alkaline soil — hyssop is drought-tolerant once established and thrives in conditions similar to lavender. Prune plants back by about one-third after flowering to keep them from becoming woody and to promote a compact, bushy habit. Harvest leafy stems and flowers in the morning just as the blossoms begin to open, drying them for use in expectorant teas, culinary herb blends, and potpourri.

plant_note: Set out only after nights stay reliably above 50 F.
directions: Genovese is the classic Italian basil, offering the traditional fragrance and flavor that make it the top choice for pesto and countless summer dishes. Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant after the soil is warm and settled weather has arrived, as cold stunts young plants. Give it full sun, rich soil, and even moisture for lush growth. Pinch out flower buds and harvest from the top to keep plants leafy, tender, and productive all season.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed as young plants are cold-sensitive.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in warm, moist starting mix and provide a trellis or support on which the vigorous twining vine can climb; jiaogulan grows 10–20 ft if given the right conditions. Plant in moderately fertile, well-drained soil with some afternoon shade in hot climates; consistent moisture promotes the most lush and harvestable leaf growth. In zones 7–8 the plant dies to the ground in winter but regrows from the root; in zone 9 and above it may be evergreen. Harvest the five-lobed leaflets throughout the growing season for fresh or dried tea; known as southern ginseng, the leaves contain saponins similar to those in Panax ginseng and are used in adaptogenic preparations.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; kalonji prefers cool spring conditions and dislikes heat.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep directly where they are to grow; kalonji has fine, thread-like foliage similar to its ornamental cousin love-in-a-mist and dislikes transplanting. Grow in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; the plant bolts quickly in summer heat, so time sowing for a cool-season harvest. Harvest the swollen seed pods when they begin to turn tan and paper-like, cutting whole stems into bags before the pods split and release the seeds. The intensely aromatic black seeds are used in South Asian cooking on naan breads and in spice blends, and have a long history of medicinal use; they are not to be confused with black cumin (Bunium persicum).

plant_note: Surface-sow; light aids germination.
directions: Surface-sow indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, pressing seed onto the mix without covering it. This East Asian herb has minty-anise leaves used in Korean cooking and herbal teas. Transplant to full sun in well-drained soil, where purple flower spikes draw abundant bees. Deadhead to prolong bloom, and allow a few seed heads to ripen for self-sowing. Harvest young leaves through summer and cut plants back in fall.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost after cold stratification; or sow fresh seed outdoors in autumn for natural stratification and spring germination.
directions: Cold-stratify seed in the refrigerator for 3–4 weeks before surface-sowing; the tiny seeds need cool temperatures and moderate moisture to germinate. Plant in humus-rich, well-drained but moisture-retentive soil; lady's mantle tolerates full sun in cooler climates but benefits from afternoon shade in hot regions. The pleated, velvety leaves catch dewdrops and rainwater, creating sparkling spheres of water — a notable ornamental feature. Harvest leaves and the chartreuse flower sprays in early summer for use in traditional women's tonics; the foliage and flowers also add texture to fresh-cut and dried flower arrangements.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after the last frost date.
directions: Surface-sow seeds and press lightly into moist mix without covering; they need light and warmth to sprout. Plant in well-drained soil in partial shade, particularly in hot climates, to maintain fresh-looking, non-scorched foliage. Cut plants back by half after the first flush of growth and again after any flowering to encourage compact, leafy regrowth. Harvest leaves before the plant flowers for the strongest lemon fragrance, picking in the morning after dew has dried.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant only after nights are reliably above 50°F.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in warm seed-starting mix at 70°F or above; germination is rapid once soil temperature is adequate. Transplant into rich, well-drained garden soil in full sun and pinch the growing tip early to encourage a bushy, multi-stemmed plant. Lemon basil is compact compared to sweet basil and has a distinct citrus fragrance from the presence of citral; it pairs well with fish, poultry, and Southeast Asian cuisines. Harvest by snipping stems just above a leaf pair once plants are well established, and pinch flower buds promptly to extend the harvest season.

plant_note: Direct sow or start indoors 4 weeks before last frost; lemon catnip self-seeds freely once established.
directions: Sow seeds on the surface of well-drained starting mix and press in lightly; fresh seeds germinate readily in 1–2 weeks. Lemon catnip has all the nepetalactone chemistry of common catnip (making it equally irresistible to cats) but its foliage carries a pleasant lemon-mint scent suitable for tea and aromatherapy. Grow in full sun in any well-drained soil; cut plants back by half after the first flush of flowers to encourage a second bloom and fresh aromatic foliage. Harvest stems before flowers fully open for the best flavor in herbal teas; dry quickly in a warm, airy spot and store in airtight containers.

plant_note: Root stem cuttings in late spring; named cultivars do not come true from seed, so vegetative propagation is necessary to preserve the lemon character.
directions: Take 3–4 in tip cuttings in late spring or early summer, remove lower leaves, and insert into gritty, free-draining mix; roots develop in 2–3 weeks. Plant in full sun in sharply drained, lean, slightly alkaline soil, as lemon thyme rots in wet or rich conditions just like common thyme. Shear lightly after flowering each season to prevent woodiness and maintain a tidy mound of fragrant foliage. Harvest leafy sprigs at any time; the lemon-thyme combination flavor is prized in fish dishes, salad dressings, and desserts, and is excellent fresh or dried.

plant_note: Transplant rooted cuttings outdoors after all frost danger has passed; bring containers indoors before the first autumn frost.
directions: Propagate from softwood cuttings taken in summer rather than from seed, which has poor viability; root cuttings in moist perlite under plastic until rooted. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil; in zones below 8 grow in containers that can be overwintered indoors in a cool, bright spot. Prune hard in spring when new growth emerges from what may look like dead wood — lemon verbena is often a slow riser after dormancy. Harvest leaves any time for intensely lemon-scented tea, dessert flavoring, and potpourri; fragrance peaks just before the plant begins to flower.

plant_note: Transplant divisions or rooted stalks after last frost when soil is reliably warm.
directions: Propagate by placing fresh grocery-store stalks (with roots intact) in water until roots develop, then transplant into rich, well-drained soil in full sun. Lemongrass needs heat and consistent moisture; water deeply and regularly during the growing season. In zones below 10, grow in large containers and bring indoors before first frost, cutting back to 6 in tall. Harvest outer stalks by cutting or twisting at the base when they reach pencil thickness; use the pale, tender base for cooking.

plant_note: Warmth-loving; transplant after soil warms.
directions: This exceptionally productive sweet basil produces huge, light green leaves nearly the size of your hand that are more sweet than spicy. The broad leaves make an excellent lettuce substitute in wraps and sandwiches or a bold layer in a caprese salad. Start seed indoors in warmth and move to a sunny bed only after frost danger has passed. Keep soil evenly moist, pinch off flower spikes, and harvest whole leaves to encourage continued production.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost after scarifying or soaking seed overnight; transplant carefully after frost danger passes to avoid disturbing roots.
directions: Scarify seed coat lightly with sandpaper or soak overnight before sowing 1/4 in deep in warm starting mix; germination improves significantly with this treatment. Plant in deep, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun — licorice develops an extensive, deep root system and resents being moved. Roots are ready to harvest in the third or fourth autumn when they are at least pencil-thick, digging carefully to extract as much of the lateral root run as possible. The dried root is used medicinally as a demulcent and anti-inflammatory for digestive and respiratory conditions; note that excessive consumption raises blood pressure.

plant_note: Chartreuse foliage; light-dependent seed.
directions: Surface-sow indoors 8 weeks before last frost, leaving seed uncovered since light aids germination. This golden-leaved anise hyssop offers licorice-scented foliage for teas and blue flower spikes for pollinators. Transplant to full sun in well-drained soil, spacing to allow good airflow. Deadhead spent blooms to extend flowering into fall and prevent excessive self-seeding. Harvest leaves and flowers fresh or dried and cut plants back after frost.

plant_note: Direct-sow into warm soil after frost.
directions: Direct-sow after the soil warms past 65F, scattering seed thinly and thinning to a hand's width apart. The bright citrus scent makes this basil superb in Southeast Asian dishes and cold drinks. Give it full sun and steady moisture in rich, free-draining soil for the best oil content. Pinch growing tips early to build a bushy plant and delay bolting. Harvest leaves in the morning when the fragrant oils are most concentrated.

plant_note: Set out after frost danger; grow in a pot where winters fall below 20 F so it can overwinter indoors.
directions: Little Ragu is a compact, upright form of culinary sweet bay with aromatic evergreen leaves used fresh or dried in soups and stews. It stays tidy at 6 to 8 feet and takes well to pruning or shaping in containers. Plant in full sun to light shade in well-drained soil and let the surface dry slightly between waterings, as it dislikes soggy roots. In cold-winter areas grow it in a pot and bring it into a cool, bright spot before hard frost. Harvest mature leaves any time and dry them for the strongest flavor.

plant_note: Direct sow in succession; cilantro dislikes transplanting and bolts in heat.
directions: Also known as Chinese parsley, this variety is selected for excellent flavor, extra leafiness, and slow bolting, giving you a longer cilantro harvest. Direct sow into cool soil since the taproot resents transplanting, and make successive sowings every few weeks for a steady supply. Provide full sun in cool weather or light afternoon shade as temperatures climb to delay flowering. Let some plants bolt and set seed to harvest coriander for the spice rack.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring, 2–4 weeks before last frost; fresh seed germinates best.
directions: Sow fresh seed 1/4 in deep in moist, fertile soil in early spring; stored seed loses viability quickly. Give lovage a permanent spot as it develops a large taproot that dislikes disturbance, and provide deep, moisture-retentive, fertile soil. Remove flower stalks as they appear to keep the plant producing leafy growth for longer. Harvest young outer leaves and stems throughout the season; the intense celery-like flavor means a little goes a long way.

plant_note: Direct sow where it is to grow; the long taproot resents transplanting.
directions: Mammoth dill lives up to its name, reaching at least three feet and sometimes towering to five, with feathery foliage prized for cooking and pickling. Its large flower umbels are magnets for beneficial pollinators, and the plants readily self-sow once seed matures. Direct sow into full sun since the taproot dislikes being moved, and sow in succession for a continuous supply of leaves. Provide staking or a sheltered spot for the tallest plants, and harvest foliage before flowering for the best flavor.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Surface-sow the tiny seeds without covering, pressing them gently into moist, well-drained seed mix and placing in a warm, bright spot. Transplant into well-drained, slightly alkaline garden soil after nights are reliably above 50°F. Pinch out growing tips regularly to keep plants bushy and delay flowering. Harvest stems just as the knotted flower buds form for the sweetest, most aromatic leaves.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; scarifying the seed coat lightly with sandpaper or soaking overnight in warm water speeds germination.
directions: Scarify or soak seed overnight before sowing 1/4 in deep; marshmallow appreciates consistent moisture, particularly during its establishment period. Plant in deep, moderately moist soil as the tap root grows substantial in mature plants. Harvest leaves and flowers in summer for use in soothing teas and poultices; the fleshy root is the most mucilaginous part and is best dug in the plant's second or third autumn. The dried root can be sliced, powdered, or made into a cold-water infusion to extract its slippery mucilage for throat-coat and digestive preparations.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost after 4–6 weeks of cold stratification; transplant to a moist site after frost danger passes.
directions: Cold-stratify seed in moist medium in the refrigerator for 4–6 weeks before surface-sowing; plant out in consistently moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial shade or full sun where soil never dries out, mimicking its native pond-margin habitat. Meadowsweet bears frothy, sweetly almond-scented flower plumes in midsummer that were historically used to flavor mead and wines. Harvest flowers and upper leaves just as the buds begin to open, drying quickly at low heat to preserve the salicylate compounds that give the herb its traditional analgesic and anti-inflammatory reputation. The plant is the original source from which salicylic acid — the basis of aspirin — was first isolated.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or nursery transplants after last frost; in zones below 8 overwinter indoors or take cuttings each fall.
directions: Root 4–6 in softwood cuttings in moist perlite; this shrubby herb from the Mexican highlands is unrelated to Mediterranean oregano but delivers a stronger, more citrusy-oregano flavor essential in chili, mole, and refried beans. Plant in sharply drained, lean soil in full sun; Mexican oregano is extremely drought-tolerant once established and actually develops more aromatic foliage under moderate stress. Prune plants lightly after each harvest to encourage fresh, leafy growth and prevent excessive woodiness. Harvest leaves and small branch tips at any time, drying them at low heat for the most authentic dried herb for Tex-Mex and Mexican cooking.

plant_note: Surface sow; seed needs light. Grown as an annual in cold climates.
directions: A member of the marigold family, Mexican tarragon produces anise-flavored leaves that make an easy substitute for French tarragon in warm, humid regions. It handles heat and drought far better than true tarragon and rewards growers with cheerful golden flowers late in the season. Surface-sow seed indoors, as it needs light to germinate, and transplant to full sun after frost. Because the leaves are potent, use them sparingly, and harvest regularly to keep the plant bushy.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; in zone 8–11 it overwinters and becomes a dependable perennial where French tarragon languishes in summer heat.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in warm starting mix or propagate by stem cuttings, which root readily in moist perlite. Mexican tarragon thrives in the same hot, humid summers that kill French tarragon and delivers a very similar anise-like flavor; harvest foliage throughout the season by pinching stem tips. It blooms in fall with small golden-yellow flowers that attract butterflies — you can allow blooming or deadhead to maintain leaf production. In zone 9–11 the plant is perennial; cut back hard in early spring to stimulate fresh growth.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost; seeds germinate readily in cool soil and plants can handle light frost.
directions: Direct sow 1/4 in deep in early spring; milk thistle germinates readily in cool, moist conditions and self-seeds vigorously once established. Plants become quite large and spiny — space generously and wear gloves when tending them. Monitor flower heads closely as seeds ripen quickly and can be scattered by wind; harvest heads when the fluffy pappus begins to appear but before seeds fully disperse. Dry seed heads on a tray, then thresh to separate the hard achenes, which are ground or extracted for their silymarin content in liver-supportive preparations.

plant_note: Direct sow; grown mainly for its abundant coriander seed.
directions: Moroccan coriander is a fast-growing, four-in-one herb, giving a quick flush of leaves, edible white flowers for beneficial insects, and, above all, large aromatic seeds. It is especially valued for its heavy yield of round coriander seed used in baking and spice blends. Direct sow into cool soil since the taproot resists transplanting, and let plants flower and set seed for harvest. Provide full sun and even moisture, then cut seed heads once they turn tan and dry them fully before storing.

plant_note: Slow to germinate; soaking seed overnight speeds sprouting.
directions: This curled parsley forms dense, ruffled rosettes of deep green that garnish plates beautifully and pack in vitamins A, B, and C. Cold-tolerant, it can be grown as an annual that carries on through fall and into mild winters. Parsley seed is famously slow to sprout, so soak it overnight and be patient, keeping the soil consistently moist. Grow in full sun to part shade with steady water and harvest outer stems first to keep the plant producing.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring as the seed benefits from natural cold stratification or a brief refrigerator stratification period of 2–4 weeks.
directions: Direct sow in early spring or late autumn and allow natural freeze-thaw cycles to stratify the seed over winter for strong spring germination. Motherwort thrives in partial shade and moderately fertile, well-drained soil, often naturalizing along hedgerows and woodland margins. Harvest the upper third of flowering stems just as the lower flowers begin to open, using aerial parts fresh or tinctured in alcohol. Wear gloves when harvesting as the prickly calyces are sharp; avoid use during pregnancy due to its uterine-stimulating properties.

plant_note: Divide established clumps in early spring or plant nursery divisions around the last frost date; cold-stratified seed can also be sown indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost.
directions: Divide clumps by prying them apart with two forks and replant in moderately moist, average soil in full sun to light shade; mountain mint naturalizes readily and spreads by rhizomes, though less aggressively than true mints. The leaves and silvery-white flower clusters are intensely menthol-scented — far more potent than peppermint — and are excellent as a native tea herb. Harvest leafy stems and the clusters of tiny white flowers in midsummer just as blooming begins for the most aromatic result. The prolific flowers are exceptional for native bees and other pollinators, making this a valuable dual-purpose plant.

plant_note: Divide established clumps in early spring or plant root divisions around the last frost date; mugwort also self-seeds freely if flower stalks are allowed to mature.
directions: Plant root divisions in any well-drained soil; mugwort is extremely vigorous and can become invasive via rhizomes, so consider installing a root barrier or growing in a contained bed. The deeply lobed, silver-backed leaves are strongly aromatic with a bitter, sage-like scent used in traditional European and East Asian medicine. Harvest leafy stems just before flowering in midsummer for use in moxibustion, digestive bitters, dream pillows, and traditional holiday flavoring of fatty meats. Cut plants back hard in late summer to control spread and prevent self-seeding.

plant_note: Cold-stratify seed for a few weeks before sowing to improve germination.
directions: Introduced in 1916 and named for Gertrude Jekyll's garden at Munstead Woods, this compact English lavender blooms earlier than most and stays neatly mounded. Its sweet flowers are among the best for culinary use as well as sachets and dried bouquets. Cold-stratify seed before sowing, then start indoors and transplant to full sun with sharp drainage. Keep water low once established, trim after flowering to maintain a tidy shape, and avoid rich or soggy soil.

plant_note: Transplant only after soil warms and nights stay above 50 F.
directions: Noga basil balances heavy yields with classic Genovese flavor, producing tall, uniform plants that grow fast and stand up to repeated cutting. Its large, glossy, dark green leaves perform well in the field, greenhouse, or a patio container. Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant only after the soil has warmed thoroughly. Provide full sun, steady moisture, and frequent harvests, pinching out flower buds to keep leaf production high.

plant_note: Citrus-scented; propagate vegetatively.
directions: Propagate from cuttings to preserve the distinct orange-balsam fragrance of this thyme. The narrow grey-green leaves lend a citrusy note to poultry, fish, and marinades. Set plants in lean, fast-draining soil in full sun and keep water to a minimum. Shear lightly after bloom to maintain a tidy mound and prevent the base from going woody. Harvest sprigs throughout the season and protect crowns with gravel mulch over winter.

plant_note: Best grown from cuttings; contain roots.
directions: Propagate from stem cuttings or divisions, since named mints do not come true from seed. This citrusy peppermint variety perfumes teas, desserts, and cocktails with orange-bergamot notes. Plant in moist, rich soil in partial shade and confine roots to a buried pot or bottomless container. Water frequently and shear plants back after flowering to renew fresh leafy growth. Divide clumps every two or three years to keep the patch vigorous.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant after danger of frost has passed.
directions: Sow seeds on the surface of well-drained mix, pressing in lightly without covering since seeds need light to germinate. Plant in lean, gritty soil with excellent drainage; rich soil dilutes the essential oils that give oregano its pungency. Cut stems back by one-third after flowering to keep plants compact and productive. Harvest just as buds begin to open, when aromatic oil concentration is highest.

plant_note: Plant rooted suckers or stem cuttings after last frost in a warm, sheltered position; requires consistently warm temperatures above 60°F.
directions: Propagate by removing rooted basal suckers from an established plant and potting into rich, moist, well-drained potting mix; cuttings without roots should be allowed to callous before planting. Pandan is a tropical Southeast Asian herb that rarely sets seed in cultivation and must be grown vegetatively; the long, strap-like leaves impart a distinctive vanilla-like, grassy fragrance used throughout Southeast Asian sweets, rice dishes, and drinks. Grow in bright indirect light or dappled shade with consistent moisture and high humidity; in temperate climates it makes an excellent houseplant or conservatory specimen. Harvest mature outer leaves by cutting at the base; knot or tie leaves before use in cooking to release their fragrance, or blend with water to make an extract.

plant_note: Direct sow at or just after last frost once soil has warmed to 65°F; papalo thrives in hot summers when cilantro fails.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep directly in the garden once soil is warm and all frost danger has passed; papalo does not transplant well due to its taproot. This Mexican culinary herb is used raw, added at the table rather than during cooking — heat destroys its unique flavor, which blends notes of cilantro, rue, and citrus. Grow in full sun in well-drained soil; it tolerates heat and moderate drought admirably, making it valuable as a summer substitute for cilantro. Harvest young leaves and stem tips regularly while the plant is in vegetative growth; use very sparingly as the flavor is more intense than cilantro.

plant_note: Direct sow or start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; parsley tolerates light spring frosts once established.
directions: Soak seeds overnight to soften the hull, then sow 1/4 in deep; germination is notoriously slow—up to four weeks. Thin to 6–9 in and keep soil consistently moist, as parsley bolts quickly under drought stress. Apply a light nitrogen feed at mid-season to sustain lush leaf production. Harvest outer stems at the base, leaving the inner crown intact to keep the plant productive.

plant_note: Sow early; soaking seed overnight speeds notoriously slow germination.
directions: Italian Giant is a robust flat-leaved parsley prized by cooks for its strong, fresh flavor and easy-to-chop leaves. Grown as an annual, it produces large, dark green leaves ideal for soups, stews, and garnishes. Sow seed directly or start indoors, keeping the soil consistently moist since germination is slow and can take up to three weeks. Give it full sun to part shade and harvest outer stems first so the plant keeps producing. It tolerates light frost and can be grown in containers on a sunny windowsill.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; surface-sow as seeds need light and keep soil consistently moist.
directions: Surface-sow seeds onto moist starting mix as they need light to germinate, keeping the medium evenly moist throughout germination. Pennyroyal spreads by creeping stems and works well as a fragrant ground cover in moist, partially shaded spots; it spreads less aggressively than other mints. The intensely peppermint-scented leaves are used fresh or dried as a traditional flea-repelling herb for pet bedding and as a strewing herb. Caution: pennyroyal oil is highly toxic and internal use carries serious risk — use the dried herb only in small quantities for tea and keep away from pregnant women and pets.

plant_note: Divide or transplant rooted runners in spring around the last frost date.
directions: Plant divisions or purchased starts in moist, rich soil and install a root barrier or grow in sunken containers, as peppermint spreads aggressively via underground stolons. Keep soil consistently moist and mulch to retain moisture during dry spells. Cut plants back to a few inches after the first flush of growth to encourage denser, more flavorful regrowth. Harvest stems before flower buds open, when menthol content is at its peak.

plant_note: Transplant rooted cuttings outdoors after all frost danger has passed; in zones 8–11 it overwinters as a perennial shrub.
directions: Propagate from 4–6 in softwood cuttings in late spring or early summer, which root readily in moist perlite within 2–3 weeks; plants started from cuttings establish more quickly and reliably than seed. Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun and water consistently during summer; pineapple sage is less drought-tolerant than culinary sage. In zones below 8, take cuttings before the first autumn frost and overwinter plants on a bright, frost-free windowsill. Harvest the pineapple-scented leaves throughout summer for fruit salads, drinks, and tea; the brilliant red flowers bloom in autumn and attract hummingbirds.

plant_note: Cold-moist stratification improves germination.
directions: Sow in fall or cold-stratify seed for four weeks before spring sowing to break dormancy. This drought-tough native milkweed has a deep taproot historically used for respiratory ailments. Plant in lean, sandy, well-drained soil in full sun and avoid moving it once established. The brilliant orange flowers are a magnet for butterflies and native bees. Water only during severe drought and harvest roots from mature plants in autumn.

plant_note: Warmth-loving; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Purple Petra offers a mild, sweet basil flavor without the peppery bite of some varieties, making it a favorite for those who prefer gentle herbal notes. Its deep purple foliage doubles as an ornamental accent and tints vinegars and oils a lovely pink. Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant after frost, giving plants full sun and rich, moist soil. Pinch tips regularly to encourage bushy growth and keep the vivid leaf color coming.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; red-veined sorrel tolerates light spring frosts and can also be sown in early fall.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in moist, well-drained soil and thin to 9–12 in apart; red-veined sorrel germinates readily in cool soil. The lance-shaped leaves with vivid crimson veins are ornamentally striking and have a mild lemony-tart flavor suitable for salads, garnishes, and light sautés. Remove flower stalks promptly to prevent self-seeding and to keep the plant producing tender new foliage throughout the season. Harvest outer leaves when they are young and vibrant; flavor and texture deteriorate in the largest, oldest leaves.

plant_note: Cover seed well, as calendula germinates best in darkness.
directions: Resina calendula was bred for exceptionally resin-rich petals, making it the go-to variety for herbalists preparing salves and infused oils. Its bright yellow, daisy-like blooms shine in the garden and are so frost tolerant they may keep flowering into the snow. Direct sow and cover the seed well, since darkness aids germination, in a sunny spot with average soil. Deadhead spent blooms to prolong flowering and harvest fully open flower heads on a dry morning for the best medicinal quality.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; surface-sow as seeds need light to germinate, then transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Surface-sow the tiny seeds onto moist, well-drained starting mix without covering; they require light and a temperature of 55–65°F for reliable germination. Plant in sandy or gritty, well-drained soil and avoid heavy clay or constantly moist conditions, which promote crown rot. Roman chamomile spreads by creeping stems and can be walked on lightly, making it a fragrant lawn substitute in cooler climates. Harvest flower heads when the petals are fully reflexed downward and the yellow disc is well developed, drying them quickly on screens for apple-scented tea.

plant_note: Root softwood cuttings in late spring or early summer; transplant rooted cuttings after last frost.
directions: Propagate from 4–6 in softwood cuttings stripped of lower leaves and set into gritty, free-draining medium; mist lightly and expect roots in 3–4 weeks. Plant in sharply drained, neutral to slightly alkaline soil and never let roots sit in standing water. In zones below 7, grow in containers and overwinter indoors in a bright, cool spot. Harvest stem tips freely; regular light trimming keeps the shrub compact and promotes fresh, aromatic growth.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after soil has warmed.
directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep indoors in a warm spot; rue germinates best at around 68°F. Plant in poor, well-drained, alkaline soil and avoid overhead watering to prevent fungal issues in the bluish-green foliage. Always wear gloves and long sleeves when handling rue, as the sap can cause severe phototoxic skin burns when exposed to sunlight. Harvest small sprigs of foliage before flowering for traditional uses as a strewing herb or insect deterrent; internal use requires professional guidance due to toxicity at high doses.

plant_note: Plant corms 6–8 weeks before the first autumn frost so they establish before winter; flowers appear in the first autumn after planting.
directions: Plant corms (not seeds) 3–4 in deep in sharply drained, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun during late summer to early autumn; good drainage is critical as corms rot quickly in waterlogged soil. Each corm produces lilac-purple flowers in autumn of the first year; within each flower are three vivid red stigmas — these are saffron. Harvest the flowers as soon as they open in the morning and carefully pluck the three red stigmas from each blossom, drying immediately on a warm screen or in a low oven. Corms multiply each year; lift and divide every 3–4 years to prevent overcrowding and maintain flowering vigor.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost has passed and soil is warm.
directions: Press seeds 1/8 in into well-drained seed mix and keep at 60–70°F; germination is steady but takes two to three weeks. Plant in sandy, well-drained soil in full sun; wet, poorly drained soil is the leading cause of sage death. Prune hard in early spring once new growth shows at the base to prevent the plant from becoming too woody. Harvest young leaves throughout the season, cutting stems back by no more than one-third at a time.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost; can also be sown in autumn for spring germination.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in well-drained, alkaline or neutral soil; salad burnet tolerates poor, thin soils and performs well on chalky or limestone ground. As an evergreen perennial, it provides fresh cucumber-flavored leaves through mild winters when little else is harvestable. Remove flower stems as they appear to keep the plant producing the most tender, flavorful leaves rather than diverting energy into seed production. Harvest the youngest leaves at the tips of each stem for the freshest cucumber taste in salads, cool drinks, and herb butters.

plant_note: Italian foraged green; self-sows.
directions: Direct-sow in early spring in well-drained soil, covering seed lightly and thinning young plants. This Italian wild green, also called bladder campion, has a mild sweet-grassy flavor for risottos and omelets. Grow in full sun with moderate water where it forms tender leafy rosettes. Harvest young shoots and leaves before flowering for the best texture and taste. Let a few plants flower to self-sow for a continuing patch.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; cold stratification for 2–3 weeks improves germination rates for this low-growing native plant.
directions: Surface-sow seeds onto moist soil in early spring; a 2–3 week cold stratification in the fridge before sowing can improve germination rates. Self-heal spreads by stolons and will naturalize into a dense mat in moist, partly shaded spots; it tolerates light foot traffic and can work as a lawn substitute. Harvest aerial parts — leaves, stems, and flowers — when plants are in full bloom throughout summer, using them in poultices, teas, and infused preparations traditionally associated with wound healing. The purple flower spikes are also attractive and pollinator-friendly, making self-heal a useful dual-purpose ground cover.

plant_note: Direct sow at or just after last frost; shiso needs warm soil and will self-seed freely.
directions: Refrigerate seeds for a few days before sowing to break dormancy, then surface-sow or cover with 1/8 in of soil after the last frost. Grow in rich, moist, well-drained soil in full sun; shiso tolerates partial shade but growth is less vigorous. Pinch flower spikes promptly to extend leaf production, or allow a few to go to seed for next year's volunteer plants. Harvest the large, ruffled leaves at any stage; young leaves are most tender and aromatic for fresh use.

plant_note: Variegated; propagate from cuttings.
directions: Grow this variegated thyme from cuttings or division to keep the silver-edged leaf pattern true. It offers standard culinary thyme flavor along with attractive frosty foliage. Plant in gritty, sharply drained soil in full sun and water sparingly once established. Trim after flowering to prevent woodiness and encourage fresh compact growth. Harvest sprigs any time and mulch the crown in cold-winter regions.

plant_note: A blend of basil types; all are warmth-loving and set out after frost.
directions: This blend gathers six basils, including Genovese and large-leaf types for pesto, Dark Opal for colorful vinegars, plus lime and lemon basils for oils, sauces, and sorbets. Because every type in the mix is a form of Ocimum basilicum, they share the same warm-weather culture and can be grown together for a diverse harvest. Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant after frost into full sun with rich, moist soil. Pinch flower buds and harvest often to keep the range of scents and flavors at their peak.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; cold-moist stratification for 4 weeks before sowing greatly improves germination of this native wildflower.
directions: Cold-stratify seed in moist peat in the refrigerator for 4 weeks before surface-sowing onto a warm starting mix; germination is slow and erratic. Plant in partial shade with consistently moist, humus-rich soil that mimics its woodland-edge native habitat. Harvest aerial parts — stems, leaves, and small flowers — in full bloom during summer, using them fresh or dried for tinctures and teas valued for their calming properties. Divide established clumps every few years in early spring to maintain vigor.

plant_note: Direct sow; bred to stay leafy longer before flowering.
directions: Direct sow seed where plants are to grow, as cilantro has a taproot and dislikes transplanting. This beginner-friendly variety is bred to stay leafy longer before bolting to seed, extending the harvest of flat, flavorful leaves. Plants reach about twelve to twenty-four inches and respond well to successive sowings every few weeks. Harvest outer leaves regularly to encourage fresh growth and delay flowering. If plants do bolt, let them go to seed to collect coriander.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; soapwort can also be started indoors or divided from established clumps in spring or autumn.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in well-drained, neutral to alkaline soil; soapwort is extremely adaptable and tolerates poor, dry soils once established, spreading by both seed and rhizome. The entire plant — especially the roots — contains saponins that create a soapy lather when bruised and agitated in water, making it historically valuable for washing delicate textiles and woolens. Harvest roots in autumn and foliage before flowering for use in gentle natural soap preparations; the lather cleans without the harshness of detergents. Deadhead spent flowers to limit self-seeding, or allow a few to naturalize for a low-maintenance informal planting of clove-scented pink blooms.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or nursery transplants after last frost; southernwood rarely sets viable seed and is almost always propagated vegetatively.
directions: Root 4–6 in semi-ripe cuttings in late summer in gritty, moist propagating mix; cuttings root readily without hormone treatment. Southernwood is a woody, feathery-leaved Artemisia with a sharp lemon-camphor fragrance historically used as a moth repellent and insect deterrent in drawers and linen presses. Plant in well-drained, lean soil in full sun; like most artemisias it suffers in wet or rich conditions and prefers slightly alkaline soil. Cut back hard to within 6 in of the ground in early spring to prevent excessive woodiness and stimulate a fresh flush of aromatic silver-green growth.

plant_note: Divide established clumps or transplant rooted runners in spring near the last frost date.
directions: Set divisions in fertile, moisture-retentive soil, planting at the same depth they grew previously, and contain the root run with a buried barrier to prevent unwanted spread. Water regularly and avoid prolonged drought, which causes leaves to toughen and lose flavor. Cut stems back by half after each main harvest to stimulate a fresh flush of tender shoots. Harvest stems once they reach 6–8 in tall, taking no more than one-third of the plant at once.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed when soil is warm.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in warm, moist seed-starting mix at 75°F; transplant after last frost into rich, well-drained soil with consistent moisture. Spilanthes is notable for spilanthol, a compound in the flowers and leaves that causes an intense, numbing tingle on the tongue — hence the common name toothache plant; it has uses in local anesthetic preparations and salivation-stimulating tinctures. The distinctive red and yellow, cone-shaped flower heads are harvested fresh or dried for tinctures and topical preparations; even a small piece of flower head produces a pronounced oral sensation. Grow in full sun with moderate moisture; deadhead regularly to encourage continuous flowering throughout the warm season.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; surface-sow as seeds need light, and a 2–3 week cold-moist stratification period can improve germination rates.
directions: Surface-sow seeds onto moist mix; cold stratification for 2–3 weeks in the refrigerator prior to sowing can improve germination, especially with older seed. Plants thrive in poor, well-drained soil and become weedy in fertile conditions — resist the urge to fertilize. Harvest flower buds and newly opened blossoms around the summer solstice, when hypericin content peaks; rub a bud between your fingers and look for a red-purple stain confirming good potency. Dry flowers quickly on screens to preserve the active compounds.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds shallowly, just pressing them into moist seed-starting mix, as light aids germination; note that germination rates for stevia seed can be inconsistent. Transplant into well-drained, fertile soil after temperatures are reliably warm, and water consistently—stevia is sensitive to both drought and waterlogging. Pinch growing tips early to encourage a bushy form with more harvestable leaf area. Harvest leaves before the plant flowers, when glycoside content (sweetness) is at its peak; dry or use fresh.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring as soon as soil is workable; surface-sow as seeds need light and cool temperatures to germinate.
directions: Surface-sow onto moist, nitrogen-rich soil in a dedicated bed with underground barriers if possible, as nettles spread aggressively by rhizome. Always wear thick gloves and long sleeves when handling plants, as the hollow stinging hairs inject formic acid on contact. Harvest young growing tips in early spring before plants reach knee height, using them in soups, teas, and nutritive infusions — cooking or drying neutralizes the sting completely. Cut plants back to the ground two or three times per season to encourage fresh new growth throughout the year.

plant_note: Direct sow at or just after last frost once soil has warmed to 60°F.
directions: Press seeds lightly into the soil surface without covering, as they need light for good germination; thin seedlings to 6–9 in once they show two true leaves. Plant in full sun with very well-drained soil; summer savory tolerates lean soil and wilts under excess moisture. Pinch tips regularly to encourage branching and prevent early bolting in warm weather. Harvest whole stems just as flower buds begin to show for the best peppery flavor.

plant_note: Surface sow the tiny seed, which needs light to germinate.
directions: Sweet Annie, also called sweet wormwood, is beloved by crafters for its lacy, fragrant foliage that fills dried bouquets and wreaths. It grows rapidly into a tall, airy plant, tolerates drought, and gives off a pleasant camphor-like scent that naturally repels moths. Surface-sow the fine seed indoors since it needs light to germinate, then transplant to full sun. Give the tall plants room to spread, cut stems for drying just as they reach full size, and expect self-sown seedlings the following year.

plant_note: Direct sow just before or at last frost; seeds are tiny and need light for germination — do not cover.
directions: Broadcast the dust-like seeds onto a weed-free seedbed and press in without covering; they require light to germinate and will not sprout if buried. Sweet Annie grows rapidly into a large, feathery-leaved annual with an intensely sweet, camphor-like fragrance widely used in wreath-making and dried floral arrangements. The plant is the natural source of artemisinin, a compound researched extensively for anti-malarial properties; harvest aerial parts when the plant begins to flower for the highest artemisinin content. Self-seeds prolifically in warm climates, so deadhead spent plants before seeds fully ripen if reseeding in the garden is not desired.

plant_note: Sow fresh seed in autumn 2–4 weeks before first frost; the seed requires cold stratification through winter and will not germinate without it.
directions: Sow fresh seed immediately — it loses viability rapidly and requires a sustained cold period to germinate; autumn sowing directly in the garden is the most reliable approach. Plant in deep, fertile, moist soil in partial to full shade, where it will naturalize into a handsome long-lived clump. All parts of the plant are edible and anise-scented: young leaves sweeten acidic fruits in cooking and reduce the need for sugar, green seeds are eaten as a snack, and roots are grated raw in salads. Cut flower stems before they set seed if you want to limit self-sowing; once established, plants are highly drought-tolerant and largely self-sufficient.

plant_note: Also called Mexican mint marigold.
directions: Sow seed indoors 6 weeks before last frost, barely covering it and keeping flats warm. This anise-scented marigold serves as a tarragon substitute and yields golden fall flowers for tea. Transplant to full sun in well-drained soil once the weather settles and nights warm. It tolerates heat and drought better than French tarragon, thriving where that herb fails. Pinch stems for bushier growth and harvest leaves through the season, drying flowers for winter tea.

plant_note: Grown as annual outside zone 9.
directions: Start seed indoors 6-8 weeks before the last frost and press it onto the surface without burying, since light triggers germination. This improved culinary strain offers sweeter, milder flavor than wild oregano relatives. Transplant to a sunny, well-drained bed once frost danger passes, spacing plants closely. Pinch tips regularly to promote branching and harvest just as flower buds form for peak aroma. Bring a potted plant indoors over winter in colder climates.

plant_note: Warmth-loving; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Thai basil is a cornerstone of Southeast Asian cooking, prized as the aromatic garnish served alongside Vietnamese pho and stirred into curries and stir-fries. Its anise-scented leaves hold their flavor and texture better through cooking than sweet basils, and its purple stems and flower spikes add ornamental appeal. Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant only after the soil warms. Grow in full sun with even moisture, pinching flower spikes to prolong tender leaf production.

plant_note: Divide established clumps in early spring or plant nursery divisions around the last frost date; germination from seed is very slow and division is far more reliable.
directions: Plant divisions in cool, humus-rich, moist, slightly acidic soil in full to partial shade; sweet woodruff thrives under deciduous trees where little else will grow, spreading into an attractive ground-covering mat. Water regularly until established, then the plant largely maintains itself in moist woodland conditions. Harvest stems and leaves as the plant begins to flower in late spring, when coumarin content peaks and the characteristic fresh-mown hay scent intensifies on drying. Dried sprigs are used to flavor the traditional German May wine (Maibowle) and for fragrant sachets in linen.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; in zones below 8 grow as an annual or overwinter rooted cuttings indoors.
directions: Surface-sow seeds on moist, gritty seed-starting mix and keep at 68–72°F; tiny seeds need light so do not cover. Syrian oregano is the primary botanical ingredient in the Middle Eastern spice blend called za'atar; its flavor is strongly oregano-thyme with a hint of marjoram. Grow in very well-drained, poor to moderate soil in full sun — it is native to rocky limestone slopes and rewards lean conditions with the most aromatic foliage. Harvest young stems and leaves throughout the growing season, drying them for use in the za'atar spice blend or using fresh in flatbreads, salads, and dips.

plant_note: Divide established clumps in early spring or plant nursery divisions around the last frost date; tansy self-seeds freely and can also be direct-sown.
directions: Plant divisions in any well-drained soil in full sun; tansy is a vigorous, drought-tolerant perennial that spreads by rhizome and can become invasive — site it where its spread can be managed or grow it in a contained bed. The finely cut, strongly aromatic foliage contains thujone, which is toxic in large doses, so tansy should not be consumed internally and should be handled with care. Harvest leafy stems and flat-topped clusters of button-like yellow flowers throughout summer for use as a traditional insect-repelling strewing herb, moth-deterring sachets, and companion-planting aromatic barriers. Deadhead promptly to reduce the copious self-seeding that can make tansy weedy in favorable conditions.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost danger passes in the garden, or grow in a container to move indoors in cold winters.
directions: Camellia sinensis is the evergreen shrub whose young leaves are processed into green, black, and oolong tea. Grow it in acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun, sheltered from harsh wind. Water regularly to keep the soil evenly moist but never waterlogged, and mulch to protect the shallow roots. In cold-winter areas, grow the plant in a container so it can be moved to protection. Harvest the tender top two leaves and bud from established plants, then dry or process them to make your own tea.

plant_note: Direct sow; the taproot resents transplanting.
directions: Tetra is a bushy, late-flowering dill bred for heavy leaf production, so it stays leafy longer before running to seed. Its heat tolerance makes it slow to bolt, letting you pick fresh, aromatic foliage through the warmer months. Direct sow in full sun where plants will grow, since the taproot dislikes being disturbed, and sow in succession for a steady supply. Harvest leaves before flowering for peak flavor, and leave a few plants to bloom for pollinators and seed.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after soil and night temperatures are reliably warm.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in warm starting mix and transplant after the last frost when soil has warmed; Thai basil withstands heat slightly better than sweet basil. The glossy, dark-green leaves with purple stems carry a spicy anise-clove flavor that holds up better during cooking than sweet basil, making it ideal for stir-fries, curries, and pho. Pinch out purple flower spikes as they emerge to maintain vigorous leaf production, though the flowers are edible and attractive to pollinators. Harvest stems above a leaf node; plants branch densely and remain productive throughout the warm season.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after last frost when soil is warm.
directions: Surface-sow seeds under a thin layer of vermiculite and maintain warmth; germination is slow and irregular. Grow in fast-draining, slightly alkaline soil and avoid overwatering, which quickly leads to crown rot. Shear plants lightly in early spring to remove winter-killed wood and maintain a tidy mound. Harvest sprigs freely once plants are established; thyme's flavor is most concentrated before flowers open.

plant_note: Frost-tender; needs warm season.
directions: Surface-sow seed indoors 6 weeks before last frost and keep warm and bright until sprouts appear. The gold-and-red buds contain spilanthol, which produces a numbing, tingling sensation when chewed. Transplant into rich, moist soil in full sun after frost, spacing plants for their sprawling habit. Deadhead spent buds to keep new ones coming and pinch stems to encourage bushiness. Harvest flower buds fresh for medicinal use or to add a buzzing zing to salads.

plant_note: Surface sow tiny seed, which needs light to germinate.
directions: True Greek oregano is the genuine culinary type prized by Italian and Greek cooks for its intense, pungent aroma and flavor. It elevates tomato sauces, marinated vegetables, meats, fish, and poultry, and grows compactly enough for containers. Surface-sow the fine seed indoors since it needs light, then transplant to full sun with lean, well-drained soil. Keep water on the low side to concentrate the essential oils, and shear plants after flowering to maintain dense, flavorful growth.

plant_note: First-year flowering perennial; start indoors for earliest bloom.
directions: True hyssop is an easy-care perennial that flowers in its first year and lights up the late-summer garden with spikes that draw bees, butterflies, and beneficial insects. This ancient herb has long been valued for culinary and medicinal use as well as its ornamental blue blooms. Start seed indoors and transplant to full sun with well-drained soil, where it tolerates dry conditions once established. Trim after flowering to keep plants tidy, and grow it near vegetables to attract pollinators.

plant_note: Surface sow; seed needs light and warmth to germinate.
directions: Known as the Queen of Herbs in India, tulsi or holy basil is treasured for its sacred status and prized for tea and wellness uses. This kapoor or temperate type is fast-growing with a distinctive bubble-gum fragrance and better cold tolerance than most basils. Surface-sow seed indoors in warmth since it needs light to germinate, then transplant to full sun after frost. Pinch flower spikes to prolong leaf harvest, though many growers let it bloom to attract pollinators and gather leaves and flowers for tea.

plant_note: Usually grown from a live plant or cutting; set out after frost.
directions: Tuscan Blue is a robust, upright rosemary that grows into an aromatic evergreen shrub prized for both cooking and Mediterranean-style landscaping. Its stiff, fragrant stems are ideal for skewers and roasting, while blue flowers draw pollinators. Because rosemary is slow and erratic from seed, it is best established from a live plant or cutting set out after frost. Give it full sun and fast-draining soil, water sparingly once established, and shelter or overwinter indoors in cold-winter zones.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; seed viability drops quickly so use fresh seed and surface-sow as light aids germination.
directions: Use the freshest seed available, as viability declines within a year; surface-sow and keep cool and moist for best germination. Plants grow very tall in their second year and may need staking in windy locations; keep soil consistently moist and add compost annually. Remove flower heads in the first year to direct energy into root development. Harvest roots in the second autumn after the tops die back, washing and slicing them for drying; the characteristic musky odor intensifies as the root dries.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after all danger of frost has passed and soil is warm.
directions: Surface-sow or press seeds 1/8 in into warm seed-starting mix; maintain 70–80°F for best germination. Vana tulsi (forest holy basil) is the most clove-like and pungent of the tulsi varieties, with green leaves and a more open, branching habit than Rama tulsi; it is revered in Ayurvedic practice and used widely in adaptogenic herbal teas. Pinch growing tips early to encourage branching, and remove flower spikes as they emerge to extend vegetative leaf production throughout the season. Harvest by cutting stem tips; dry leaves at low temperature to preserve the volatile eugenol-rich essential oils.

plant_note: Transplant rooted cuttings after frost in full sun to part shade; thrives in hot, humid conditions that make cilantro bolt.
directions: Propagate from stem cuttings, which root readily in water or moist potting mix within a week; seed is rarely available. Vietnamese coriander prefers consistently moist, rich soil and tolerates partial shade — qualities that let it thrive in spots too hot or dry for cilantro. The pointed, dark-green leaves with chevron markings taste of cilantro with a peppery bite and are used raw in Vietnamese pho, salads, and noodle dishes. In zone 9 it may freeze back but generally rebounds from the root crown; mulch heavily in winter to protect roots.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or propagate from cuttings; transplant after frost danger has passed.
directions: Surface-sow seeds or take 3 in softwood cuttings in late spring; cuttings are generally more reliable as germination can be slow. Wall germander is an ancient medicinal herb used historically as a bitter digestive tonic; it has glossy, dark-green, oak-like leaves and rosy-pink summer flowers that attract bees. Plant in lean, well-drained, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; it is extremely drought-tolerant once established and makes an excellent low edging or clipped knot-garden hedge. Trim after flowering to maintain a tidy, compact form; harvest small quantities of fresh foliage for occasional medicinal use.

plant_note: Plant divisions in early spring around the last frost date in consistently moist to wet soil at a pond margin or bog garden.
directions: Plant divisions in the shallow margins of a pond, bog garden, or in any spot with consistently wet to water-logged soil; water mint is the only mint species that tolerates standing water and thrives in conditions where other mints would rot. The strongly aromatic leaves carry a robust spearmint-with-floral character and are the natural parent of peppermint alongside spearmint. Harvest leafy stems throughout the growing season before flowering for the most intense aroma; use fresh or dried in teas and as a flavoring. Water mint spreads rapidly by underwater runners and may need periodic thinning in contained water features.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; mature plants tolerate brief light frost but establish best in warm, dry conditions.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in lean, fast-draining mix; refrigerate seeds for 2 weeks before sowing to improve germination rates. Plant in extremely well-drained, sandy or rocky soil in full sun — white sage is a California chaparral native that is highly drought-tolerant once established but will rot quickly in wet, heavy soil. Avoid supplemental irrigation once plants are established in their second season; overwatering is the most common cause of death. Harvest mature leaves and small branches by cutting, and dry in bundles in a warm, well-ventilated space for aromatic and ceremonial use.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; surface-sow as seeds are tiny and need light for good germination.
directions: Surface-sow seeds onto moist starting mix and keep at 65–70°F; wild bergamot germinates in 10–21 days and is more drought-tolerant than the cultivated red bee balm. Plant in well-drained, average to lean soil in full sun for the most aromatic foliage and best disease resistance. Harvest leaves and flowers throughout summer for oregano-scented teas with a mild thymol character, used traditionally by many Indigenous nations for cold and fever support. Divide established clumps every 2–3 years as the center tends to die out.

plant_note: Establish from rhizome pieces.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in rich, moist woodland soil where the twining vine can climb a trellis or shrub. This native climber has a long history in traditional herbalism for the compounds in its roots. Give it partial shade and a support structure, as stems can reach many feet in a season. Keep soil consistently moist and mulch the crown heavily each fall. Harvest the knobby rhizomes from established plants after two or more growing seasons.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after danger of frost has passed.
directions: Surface-sow seeds on barely moist seed-starting mix, pressing them in without covering; light aids germination. Plant out in sharply drained soil in full sun, spacing 12 in apart; winter savory is more drought-tolerant and woodier than its summer cousin. Trim lightly after flowering each year to prevent excessive woody growth. Harvest young shoot tips for the most intense, slightly resinous flavor; older stems are good for long-simmered dishes.

plant_note: Heat-tolerant tarragon alternative.
directions: Start seed indoors 6 weeks before last frost, covering lightly and keeping flats warm. This anise-flavored marigold thrives in heat and humidity where true tarragon struggles. Transplant into full sun in well-drained soil once nights stay warm, and pinch to encourage branching. Harvest leaves through the season for culinary use in place of French tarragon. In frost-free zones it behaves as a perennial, blooming golden flowers in fall.

plant_note: Plant nursery divisions or transplants in early spring around the last frost date; wintergreen establishes slowly and is best sourced as a division rather than grown from seed.
directions: Plant in acidic, humus-rich, moist but well-drained soil under deciduous or evergreen shade; wintergreen is a native North American woodland plant that thrives in conditions hostile to most herbs, particularly under conifers or in acidic woodland gardens. Established plants slowly spread into a glossy evergreen mat bearing small white flowers in summer and bright red berries through winter. Harvest the thick, aromatic leaves at any season for wintergreen tea; crush a leaf to confirm the characteristic methyl salicylate fragrance before using. Both berries and leaves are edible and safe in culinary quantities; the essential oil is very concentrated and should not be used internally.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost after a brief cold stratification; transplant to a partially shaded site after frost danger passes.
directions: Cold-stratify seed for 2–4 weeks in the refrigerator, then surface-sow onto moist, peat-based starting mix in a warm location; germination can be slow and erratic. Plant in humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade or dappled light, where wood betony colonizes the herbaceous layer of meadow and woodland edge. The square stems bear whorls of rosy-purple flowers in early to midsummer and are ornamental as well as useful. Harvest aerial parts — stems, leaves, and flowers — in full bloom, drying for use in traditional headache and digestive bitters teas, where the herb is valued for its mild nervine and astringent properties.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; surface-sow as seeds need light and cool temperatures to germinate well.
directions: Surface-sow seeds under bright light at around 65°F; germination is often irregular so sow more densely than needed and thin later. Plant in lean, sharply drained soil — wormwood is extremely drought-tolerant and declines in wet or rich ground. The silvery foliage contains allelopathic compounds that can inhibit neighboring plants, so site it away from vegetables and herbs you want to thrive. Harvest leafy stems before or just as flowering begins for maximum bitterness, drying upside-down in bundles for use as a moth-deterring sachets or for absinthe production where legal.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; surface-sow as seeds need light to germinate well.
directions: Surface-sow seeds onto moist starting mix and press gently; do not cover as light is needed for germination. Plant in well-drained, even poor soil — overly fertile ground produces floppy growth with reduced potency. Divide clumps every 2–3 years in spring to maintain vigor and prevent the center from dying out. Harvest flat-topped flower clusters when three-quarters of the flowers are open, using them fresh or dried for wound-healing poultices, teas, and dried flower arrangements.

plant_note: Needs sharp drainage to survive winter wet.
directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed and press in without covering, as light aids germination. Grow in lean, gritty soil in full sun where it forms a low aromatic mound prized in Middle Eastern spice blends. Water sparingly once established, since the plant resents soggy roots and rots in wet clay. Shear lightly after flowering to keep the habit compact and encourage fresh flavorful leaves. In cold zones, mulch the crown or overwinter a potted plant under cover.

plant_note: Direct sow after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: Sow two or three seeds 1 inch deep in warm soil once frost danger has passed, thinning to the strongest seedling per hill. These white-skinned fruits reach 12 to 15 pounds and hold their clean color well after harvest, so give vines plenty of room to sprawl. The strong, sturdy handle makes them ideal for carving and display. Intermediate powdery mildew resistance helps vines stay productive, but water at the base and space plants for airflow to further reduce disease pressure.

plant_note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before your last frost, keeping the mix at 70 to 85 degrees for quick germination. This 1950s heirloom is a determinate type, so most of its 6 to 8 ounce, low-acid fruits ripen over a concentrated window, making it excellent for canning. Transplant seedlings deeply once nights stay warm, and stake or cage the compact bushes to keep fruit off the ground. Harvest tomatoes when fully colored and slightly soft for the best fresh flavor.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil is thoroughly warm.
directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in compost-enriched hills after soil reaches 65°F, planting 3–4 seeds per hill and thinning to 2. Allow vines to spread freely or train onto a sturdy trellis. Leave fruits on the vine until the rind is hard and resists a thumbnail; the stem will also begin to dry and cork over. Cure harvested squash at room temperature for 1–2 weeks to harden the skin before storage.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–3 weeks after last frost when soil is warm; adzuki beans need a full warm season to produce dried beans.
directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in well-drained soil; adzuki beans are relatively drought-tolerant once established and prefer soil that is not overly rich in nitrogen. Plants produce clusters of small, dark red beans in slender pods; harvest fresh pods at 60–70 days for green shelling beans, or leave pods until they dry and turn tan for the classic small red dried bean. Watch pods closely at maturity as they shatter easily — harvest when most pods are tan but before they split open. The small, sweet beans are famous in East Asian cuisine, particularly for red bean paste in confections.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant once soil reaches 70°F and nights are reliably above 55°F.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix at 80°F and provide consistent warmth until transplanting. This West African species produces small, rounded fruits in white, green, yellow, or red and is more heat and drought tolerant than common eggplant; it is widely grown as a staple vegetable across sub-Saharan Africa. Harvest fruits while skin is still glossy and firm — they are best eaten cooked in stews, soups, and sauces where their slightly bitter flavor mellows and complements. Plants are perennial in frost-free climates and can be cut back hard after the main season to produce a second flush of fruiting growth.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; fresh seed is critical as viability drops sharply after 6 months — sow generously.
directions: Sow very fresh seed 1/4 inch deep thickly in cool, well-drained soil, as germination rates drop dramatically when seed is more than a few months old — plan to sow thickly and use thinnings. Agretti is an Italian coastal plant related to glasswort, and it thrives in sandy, saline, or alkaline soils where most vegetables struggle; it is very intolerant of waterlogged conditions. Harvest young shoot tips and the grass-like, succulent leaves when 6–8 inches tall by snipping with scissors 1–2 inches above the soil, allowing the plant to regrow once or twice before it goes to seed. The slender, fleshy, bright-green leaves have a mildly salty, slightly mineral flavor outstanding blanched with lemon or served alongside fish and seafood in the Italian spring tradition.

plant_note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; transplant after soil reaches 65°F and all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix at 80°F; Capsicum baccatum germinates more reliably when bottom heat is maintained consistently throughout the seedling stage. Transplant to the warmest, most sheltered garden bed once nights stay above 55°F, spacing plants 18–24 inches apart in well-drained, fertile soil. Fruits ripen from green through yellow-orange to a vibrant golden yellow and should be harvested when fully colored for the characteristic fruity, berry-like heat. In frost-free climates the plants are perennial; pot and overwinter indoors elsewhere to carry productive plants to a second year.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring once soil is workable; parsnips need a long season.
directions: Sow fresh seed 1/2 inch deep directly in loose, stone-free soil, since parsnips do not transplant well and seed is short-lived. Germination is slow, often two to four weeks, so keep the bed evenly moist and mark the row. This variety thickens early, maturing in as little as 95 days, producing tender white roots with a sweet, distinctive flavor. Flavor sweetens further after a fall frost, so leave roots in the ground and harvest as needed through cold weather.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–3 weeks after last frost once soil has warmed to at least 65°F; seeds are tiny and benefit from surface sowing.
directions: Scatter tiny seeds on the soil surface or press in no deeper than 1/8 inch, and keep moist until germination; thin to 6–12 inches once seedlings are established. Vegetable amaranth thrives in heat and is exceptionally drought-tolerant once established, making it a reliable summer green when spinach has bolted. Harvest young leaves and shoot tips continuously from 30 days onward; regular harvesting delays flowering and extends the harvest window. Leaves are best cooked briefly — sautéed, steamed, or simmered in soups — and are rich in iron and calcium.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches at least 65 degrees.
directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep in warm soil, planting in blocks of at least four short rows rather than a single long row to ensure good pollination. This bicolor sugar-enhanced hybrid produces 8-inch ears of tender, creamy kernels and offers early vigor plus strong disease resistance. Feed with nitrogen and water deeply during tasseling and ear fill. Harvest when silks brown and kernels release a milky juice when punctured, and refrigerate quickly to preserve sweetness.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring as soon as soil is workable, or again in late summer.
directions: Sow seed 1/2 inch deep in cool soil in early spring or late summer, thinning seedlings to give each plant room. This 1952 All-America Selections winner has thick, savoyed leaves and unusual tolerance to both heat and drought, extending the harvest window. It performs well in the ground or in containers, so it suits small gardens. Harvest outer leaves as needed, or cut the whole plant before it bolts in warm weather.

plant_note: Direct sow in mid to late summer for a fall harvest after frost sweetens roots.
directions: Sow seed 1/2 inch deep in loose, fertile soil about three months before your first fall frost, thinning to 6 inches apart. Roots grow 4 to 6 inches long with sweet, mellow yellow flesh that mashes like potatoes or adds body to soups and stews. The tender tops are also edible as sauteed greens. Rutabagas develop the best flavor in cool weather, so leave them in the ground until after a light frost before harvesting.

plant_note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost for spring, or midsummer for fall.
directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before transplanting, then set out sturdy seedlings in rich, evenly moist soil. This variety forms striking purple, domed heads that mature early and reliably in spring or fall and hold color best with steady moisture. Vigorous plants tolerate a range of climates and perform especially well in the Eastern US. Harvest heads while the curds are still tight, and enjoy the tender, flavorful florets steamed or roasted.

plant_note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost, keeping the mix warm for even germination. This indeterminate heirloom produces meaty, acorn-shaped red fruits of 8 to 12 ounces with few seeds, excellent for rich sauce and paste but also good for slicing. Provide a tall stake or trellis and prune suckers to manage the vigorous vines. Water consistently at the base to prevent cracking and blossom-end rot, and pick fruit as it colors fully.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant 1–2 weeks after frost danger passes when soil is warm.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm mix at 80°F and provide bright light once germinated to prevent leggy growth; transplant after frost danger has completely passed. Anaheim peppers are mildly hot (500–2,500 Scoville units), long and tapered, and the classic pepper for green chile and chiles rellenos. Harvest green at 75 days for mild flavor, or allow pods to ripen to red for a sweeter, slightly hotter pepper that can be dried and ground into pure chile powder. Consistent watering and a light fertilizer application when flowers begin to set helps prevent blossom drop in heat.

plant_note: Start indoors 6 to 8 weeks before last frost; transplant once nights stay warm.
directions: Start seed indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the last frost, keeping the mix at 75 to 90 degrees since peppers germinate slowly in cool soil. Transplant into warm, well-drained soil after nights are reliably above 55 degrees. The 3 to 6 inch green poblano is excellent stuffed for chiles rellenos, while the dried, reddish-brown ancho anchors sauces like mole poblano, with mild heat around 1,000 to 2,000 Scoville units. Harvest green for fresh use or leave fruit to ripen red for drying.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost danger passes and soil has warmed.
directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep in warm, fertile soil once frost danger has passed, spacing hills to give vines room to run. Prolific plants yield up to fifteen uniform fruits weighing 1.5 to 2 pounds each, sized perfectly for serving on the half-shell. When cooked, the flesh forks away in tender spaghetti-like strands. Cure harvested fruit in a warm, dry spot for a week or two to improve storage life, and keep on the vine until the rind hardens.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost danger passes and soil has warmed to at least 65 degrees.
directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep directly in warm soil, since beans resent transplanting and rot in cold ground. This vigorous variety produces 5 to 5.5 inch pods that stay tender and crisp even as the season heats up. Sturdy, upright plants hold pods off the soil for clean, easy picking and do well in containers. Harvest frequently while pods are young and snap crisply to keep plants producing over a long window.

plant_note: Direct sow around the last frost, or grow indoors year-round for baby greens.
directions: Sow seed 1/2 inch deep and thin lightly, or broadcast densely for a cut-and-come-again baby-greens crop.
