plant_note: Sow early in spring; safflower has a deep taproot that scavenges subsoil moisture, making it the most drought-resistant oilseed for dryland gardens.
directions: Plant seed 1 in deep at 6–10 in apart in rows 18–24 in wide; safflower needs a long growing season so early planting in cool soil is critical. The plant grows a large rosette in the vegetative phase, then bolts to form spiny, branching heads with brilliant orange or yellow florets. Harvest when the heads are dry and papery — cut and place in cloth bags to finish drying, then thresh by rubbing to release the seed. Safflower oil is prized for its high linoleic acid content and heat stability; the petals can also be used as a saffron substitute.

plant_note: Succession sow from early spring; heat tolerance extends the harvest window.
directions: This blend combines award-winning 'Green Salad Bowl' and 'Red Salad Bowl' loose-leaf lettuces for a colorful, flavorful, heat-tolerant mix. Sow seeds shallowly, barely covering them since light aids germination, and thin to 8-12 inches or grow densely for baby greens. Harvest young leaves in a few weeks or pick outer leaves for a continuous cut-and-come-again supply. Its heat tolerance and slow bolting extend the picking season into warm weather.

plant_note: Direct sow as early as 2–4 weeks before last frost; the long season requires early sowing and deep, loose soil.
directions: Sow seeds 1/2 inch deep in deeply dug, stone-free soil; salsify develops long taproots that fork and deform in rocky or compacted ground. Thin to 3–4 inches after germination and keep weed-free, as salsify's grass-like tops are easily overwhelmed early on. Roots are ready in autumn when they reach pencil thickness and finger length; flavor improves markedly after hard frost. Roots can overwinter in the ground and be harvested as needed through winter in mild climates, or dig all roots in late fall and store in damp sand.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: 'San Marzano' is the legendary Italian paste tomato, an indeterminate pole type bearing meaty, low-seed plum fruits ideal for sauce and canning. Start seeds indoors about 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply into warm, full-sun soil. Support the tall vines with sturdy stakes or cages and prune suckers to focus energy on fruit. Consistent watering helps prevent blossom-end rot; harvest when fully red for peak flavor.

plant_note: Start indoors early with warmth; transplant well after frost.
directions: 'Santaka' is a hot Japanese chile, just 2 inches long but delivering 40,000-50,000 Scoville units of spicy heat. Start seeds indoors 8 weeks before the last frost with warm soil for reliable germination, then transplant once nights stay warm. Grow in full sun with even moisture and light feeding; the compact plants do well in containers. Harvest peppers red for full heat, and dry them for use in stir-fries and soups.

plant_note: Direct sow once soil is warm.
directions: This colorful patty pan blend combines the heirlooms 'Early White Bush', 'Bennings Green Tint', and 'Yellow Bush' for a bowl-worthy mix of scalloped summer squash. Sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm soil and space the bushy plants 24-36 inches apart in full sun. Harvest fruits young at 2-4 inches across when tender and flavorful, picking frequently to keep the plants producing. Steady moisture and rich soil support a heavy summer crop.

plant_note: Direct sow after all danger of frost once the soil has warmed.
directions: Scarlet Emperor is a vigorous pole runner bean prized for its brilliant clusters of edible red flowers as well as its rich-flavored pods. Sow seeds 1 inch deep and 4-6 inches apart at the base of a tall trellis, fence, or pole after the soil warms in spring. Pick young pods for tender snap beans, or leave them to mature and shell for green or dried beans. Keep vines watered during flowering and harvest regularly to keep new pods forming. This variety doubles as an ornamental screen thanks to its showy blooms that attract hummingbirds.

plant_note: Direct sow in loose, stone-free soil; keep the surface moist until seedlings emerge.
directions: Sow seed shallowly directly in the garden and thin seedlings to about 2 inches apart once they reach a couple inches tall. This sweet, nearly coreless heirloom develops best in deep, loose, well-drained soil free of rocks that can fork the roots. Keep the seedbed evenly moist during the slow germination period, which can take up to three weeks. Harvest when roots reach about six to seven inches and full color for the tenderest, sweetest flavor. Successive sowings from spring through midsummer extend the harvest.

plant_note: Sow as early as 2–4 weeks before last frost; scorzonera can also be sown in late summer for next-year roots, as it is a biennial in most climates.
directions: Sow seeds 1/2 inch deep in deeply loosened, rich soil; like salsify, roots fork in compacted ground. Scorzonera produces a black-skinned root with creamy white interior and a more refined flavor than salsify, often described as mildly nutty and sweet. Roots can be harvested in the first autumn when pencil-thick, but leaving them in the ground through winter and harvesting the following spring produces larger, sweeter roots. When harvesting, dig carefully as the roots are brittle and ooze a milky latex; peel under water to prevent discoloration.

plant_note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; transplant 2 weeks after frost danger has passed once soil is thoroughly warm.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in moist starting mix, maintaining soil at 85°F for best germination results; this Capsicum chinense type is among the slower starters. Transplant into fertile, well-drained soil only after the garden is fully warm, and consider black plastic mulch to boost soil temperatures in cooler climates. The bonnet-shaped fruits ripen from green through yellow, orange, or red depending on the strain and carry a distinctive fruity aroma alongside intense heat. Use gloves when handling ripe fruits; they are essential in Caribbean jerk seasoning and hot sauces.

plant_note: Start indoors early; transplant only after nights stay reliably warm.
directions: Start seed indoors eight or more weeks before the last frost, as this Caribbean chile needs warm soil and a long season to ripen. Transplant into the warmest part of the garden after the soil and nights have settled into the 60s. The fiery, fruity fruits mature from green to bright yellow-orange or red, so wait for full color for maximum heat and flavor. Consistent watering and a steady feed produce the heaviest yields. Wear gloves when handling the exceptionally hot pods.

plant_note: Plant root cuttings (thongs) in early spring; plants establish slowly and should not be harvested until the second or third year.
directions: Plant root cuttings (thongs) 2–3 inches deep in deep, well-drained, sandy or gravelly soil with good lime content mimicking its native coastal habitat; sea kale is extremely drought and salt tolerant once established. The harvest is the blanched young shoots produced in early spring — cover crowns with a large pot or bucket to exclude light in late winter, and cut the pale, tender shoots when 6–8 inches tall. After harvesting the blanched shoots, remove the cover to allow the plant to photosynthesize and rebuild energy for the following year — do not blanch the same crown more than two consecutive years. The blanched shoots taste like a delicate cross between asparagus and cauliflower and have been a British garden delicacy since the 18th century.

plant_note: Start indoors and set out after all danger of frost has passed.
directions: Start seed indoors about six to eight weeks ahead of transplanting and provide bright light and warmth for sturdy seedlings. Move plants outdoors once the soil warms and frost danger has passed, spacing them about a foot and a half apart. These prolific plants set many slender, spicy peppers used green for salsas and pico de gallo. Pick fruits green for the classic sharp heat, or leave them to ripen red for a slightly sweeter kick. Steady moisture and full sun keep production high through the season.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm, moist starting mix and provide consistent warmth around 80°F for best germination results. Transplant into well-drained, fertile garden soil after the last frost, spacing plants to allow good airflow. Serranos are productive plants that benefit from light fertilization mid-season. Harvest small green fruits at 1–2 inches long for peak heat and crunch, or let them ripen to red for a slightly fruity flavor.

plant_note: Sow after last frost when soil is thoroughly warm; sesame demands heat for germination and will rot in cool, wet soil.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in rows 18–24 in apart after soil reaches at least 70°F; sesame seed is very small, so mixing with dry sand aids even distribution and prevents clumping. Thin to 6–12 in once plants are 3 in tall and keep well-weeded until the canopy closes. The upright plants produce attractive tubular flowers along the stem that develop into rectangular seed capsules; the key challenge is timing harvest before 'shattering' — when ripe capsules split and scatter seed spontaneously. Cut entire stalks when the bottom capsules begin to yellow, invert plants into large paper bags, and allow to dry for a week indoors; the capsules then split easily and seed falls into the bag when shaken.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost when soil is warm; provide a tall trellis.
directions: Direct sow seed after the last frost once the soil is warm, planting alongside a sturdy trellis or pole seven to nine feet tall. This All-America Selections winner bears clusters of tender four-to-six-inch pods well ahead of many standard pole beans. Pick pods young and often to keep the vines producing over a long season. Even, moderate watering during flowering and pod set gives the best yields. Avoid handling the plants when wet to reduce the spread of disease.

plant_note: Plant cloves 2–4 weeks before last frost in spring; in mild climates, an autumn planting produces larger bulbs.
directions: Separate shallot clusters into individual cloves and plant each with the tip just at or slightly above the soil surface, 1 in deep, in fertile, well-drained soil. Each clove multiplies into a cluster of 6–12 new bulbs by season's end, so generous spacing pays off. Harvest when most of the green tops have toppled and turned papery. Dry clusters on screens in good airflow for 2–3 weeks and store in a cool, airy spot; well-cured shallots keep for 6 months or more.

plant_note: Plant cloves in fall a few weeks before the ground freezes.
directions: Break bulbs into individual cloves and plant them pointed end up about two inches deep in fall, a few weeks before the ground freezes. This early-maturing Turban-type hardneck yields white bulbs with six to eight cloves and matures noticeably faster than most hardnecks. Mulch after planting to protect the cloves through winter and conserve moisture. Cut the curling scapes in early summer to direct energy into bulb sizing. Harvest when the lower leaves brown, then cure the bulbs in a dry, airy place.

plant_note: Sow as early as the ground is workable, typically 4–6 weeks before the last frost date.
directions: Sow seeds 1 in deep in rows 18 in apart and provide support for tall types. Consistent moisture from flowering through pod fill is critical; drought during this window sharply reduces yield. Harvest when pods are well rounded and the peas inside feel firm through the pod wall; sugar converts rapidly to starch after picking, so chill or eat immediately. Shell and freeze any surplus the same day for best sweetness.

plant_note: Heat-tolerant, so it can be sown into late spring and summer.
directions: Sow seed shallowly in loose, deep soil and keep the surface moist during the slow germination. Unlike many carrots, this heat-tolerant Kuroda type can be sown well into late spring and summer for fall harvests. Thin seedlings to about two inches so the tapered five-inch roots with broad shoulders can size up. Steady moisture prevents cracking and keeps the roots sweet and tender. Harvest before roots become oversized for the best texture.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Start seed indoors six to eight weeks before the last frost and transplant seedlings out after the soil warms. This popular Japanese frying pepper produces heavy sets of slender two-to-four-inch fruits with a mild, citrusy, slightly smoky flavor. Harvest peppers green and small for the best flavor when blistered in a hot pan or on the grill. Roughly one in ten pods surprises with mild heat, adding to the fun. Pick frequently to keep the highly productive plants setting new fruit.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes into warm soil.
directions: Sow 1/4 inch deep at 80°F in starting mix; shishito germinates somewhat faster than most peppers and grows into a productive, bushy plant. Harvest the thin-walled, wrinkled green pods at 2–4 inches long; roughly 1 in 10 pods will be surprisingly hot while the rest are mild, which is a characteristic of this variety rather than a flaw. Blistering or pan-roasting whole peppers (popped in a hot dry skillet until charred in spots) is the classic preparation. Production is heavy and consistent through summer; keep picking to prevent fruit from ripening fully to red, which slows new pod development.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost in spring; make a fall sowing 6–8 weeks before first frost for the best quality crop.
directions: Scatter seeds on prepared soil and cover lightly with 1/8 inch of soil; thin to 4–8 inches once seedlings are an inch tall and use thinnings in salads. Begin harvesting young shoot tips and leaves at 30 days by pinching the growing tip, which encourages the plant to branch and remain productive for several more weeks. The aromatic, slightly resinous leaves are a classic ingredient in Japanese hot pot and Chinese stir-fries, best harvested before the plant flowers. Flowers develop quickly in heat, so time the crop for cool weather or provide afternoon shade.

plant_note: Fall-plant cloves; softnecks also do well in milder winter regions.
directions: Separate bulbs into cloves and plant them pointed end up about two inches deep in fall. This Artichoke-type softneck forms twelve to twenty cloves per bulb with classic pearl-white papery skin sometimes streaked purple. Softnecks store especially well and are ideal for braiding, and they perform reliably in milder-winter regions. Mulch for winter protection and keep the bed weed-free and evenly moist through spring. Harvest when the lower leaves yellow, then cure in a dry, ventilated spot before storing.

plant_note: Fall-plant cloves; adaptable to cold, hot, humid, and coastal climates.
directions: Plant individual cloves pointed end up about two inches deep in the fall. This California-bred silverskin softneck yields ten to twelve cloves per bulb and is prized for consistent performance and high yields. It adapts well across cold, hot, humid, and coastal climates and offers excellent long-term storage. Mulch after planting, then keep the bed evenly moist through the spring growing period. Harvest when lower leaves brown and cure the bulbs in a dry, airy place for months of storage.

plant_note: Start indoors in early to midsummer for a fall harvest sweetened by frost.
directions: Start seed indoors in early to midsummer and transplant sturdy seedlings for a long-season fall crop. Brussels sprouts need a lengthy, cool finish, and a light frost actually sweetens the tight little sprouts. Space plants generously and stake tall stalks in windy sites to keep them upright. Remove yellowing lower leaves as sprouts form to improve air flow and size. Harvest from the bottom of the stalk upward once sprouts are firm and about an inch across.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost or start indoors 6 weeks early; established clumps can also be divided in spring.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in well-prepared, deeply cultivated soil; skirret produces a cluster of multiple sweet, carrot-like roots that need loose ground to develop well. Keep well-watered through summer as drought stress causes woody, fibrous roots with an unpleasant core. Harvest in autumn after frost, washing and separating the finger-sized roots; any woody central strings are removed before eating. Divide clumps in early spring to propagate, replanting the outer sections and discarding the old center.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring and fall; more heat tolerant than standard arugula.
directions: Direct sow seed shallowly in cool weather and keep the bed moist for quick, even germination. As the name suggests, this variety resists bolting longer than standard arugula and tolerates a bit more heat. Harvest young leaves for microgreens or baby greens, or let plants reach full size for peppery salad leaves. Cut outer leaves and let the center regrow for repeated harvests. Successive sowings every couple of weeks keep a steady supply of tender greens.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–3 weeks after last frost once soil reaches 75°F; a truly tropical vine requiring a long, hot summer.
directions: Nick the seed coat or soak seeds 24 hours before sowing 1/2 inch deep to improve germination rates in this heat-demanding tropical. Provide a tall, sturdy trellis or overhead structure — vines can exceed 12 feet and produce pendulous fruits that must hang freely to grow straight, reaching 1–3 feet in length. Harvest young fruits at 12–18 inches while skin is still soft and pale green-striped white; the tender young fruit is used like summer squash in South and Southeast Asian cooking. Overripe fruits develop an unpleasant bitter compound and turn orange-red, signaling they should be discarded rather than eaten.

plant_note: Direct sow 4–6 weeks before last frost; a fall planting 8 weeks before first frost is equally productive in many climates.
directions: Sow seed 1 in deep as soon as the ground can be worked and provide twiggy brush or mesh netting for vines to climb. Pre-soaking seeds overnight can improve germination in cold soil. Harvest when pods are plump, fully filled, and glossy; snap peas pass their peak quickly in warm weather. Powdery mildew is common late in the season — choose resistant varieties when available.

plant_note: Sow 4–6 weeks before the last spring frost or in early autumn; cool weather produces the crispest, sweetest pods.
directions: Press seeds 1 in deep into cool, well-drained soil and erect support netting before the vines need it. Snow peas are harvested when pods are flat, bright green, and the seeds inside are barely visible; even slight bulging means the pod is overripe for stir-fry use. Keep pods picked daily during peak production to sustain cropping. Avoid high-nitrogen fertilizers, which produce lush foliage at the expense of pods.

plant_note: Start indoors; good for short-season areas thanks to its early maturity.
directions: Start seed indoors and transplant sturdy seedlings into rich, evenly moist soil for a spring or fall crop. This dependable variety forms flavorful six-inch snow-white heads in about seventy to eighty days, making it well suited to short-season gardens. The leaves naturally curl around the head for self-blanching and protection from sun scald. Keep water and nutrients steady, since stress can trigger loose or off-color curds. Mature heads hold well in the garden, so they need not all be cut at once.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring; can also be started indoors for transplants.
directions: Direct sow seed thickly in shallow rows in early spring and keep the bed evenly moist. This specialty bunching onion forms uniform, sweet, tender stalks with only a small bulb at the base, prized for grilled cebollitas asadas. Vigorous and easy to grow, it can also be started indoors and transplanted in clumps. Hill a little soil around the stalks to lengthen and blanch the white bases. Harvest whenever stalks reach usable size, pulling entire plants as needed.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Start seed indoors six to eight weeks before the last frost and transplant into warm soil once frost danger passes. This F1 hybrid produces extra-large, dark green, thick-walled four-inch jalapenos with a slightly above-average heat around 6,500 Scoville units. The disease-resistant plants are high yielding and the fruit has an exceptional shelf life. Pick peppers green and firm for classic use, or let some ripen red for added sweetness. Steady water and full sun keep the heavy sets coming all season.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool weather; good bolt tolerance for spring and fall.
directions: Direct sow seed in cool soil in early spring or late summer and keep the bed consistently moist. This upright, compact spinach produces smooth to lightly savoyed, medium-green leaves that are excellent fresh or sauteed. High resistance to downy mildew and good bolting tolerance make it dependable across seasons. Harvest outer leaves as needed or cut whole plants at the base for a full crop. Successive sowings extend the harvest before summer heat triggers bolting.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil; compact vines suit containers.
directions: Direct sow seed after the last frost once the soil is thoroughly warm, or start indoors in peat pots for an early start. This compact variety carries full-sized slicing cucumbers on short two-to-three-foot vines, making it ideal for small beds and containers. Keep plants evenly watered, especially during flowering and fruiting, to avoid bitter or misshapen cucumbers. Harvest fruits young and often to keep the vines productive. A little support keeps fruit clean and straight even in tight spaces.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil temperature reaches 65°F.
directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm, compost-enriched hills, placing 3 seeds per hill and thinning to 1–2 plants. Give vines ample room to spread, or train onto a strong trellis and support heavy fruits with a sling. The rind turns from pale green to golden yellow when mature and will feel hard when pressed. Allow picked fruits to cure in a warm spot for 1–2 weeks before using or storing; they keep for several months in a cool, dry room.

plant_note: Start indoors early; transplant crowns to a permanent bed after frost.
directions: Start seed indoors early, as germination is slow and warmth-dependent, then transplant to a permanent, weed-free bed after frost. This vigorous, all-male hybrid produces eight-to-ten-inch spears and, being male-sterile, channels its energy into strong crown and root growth rather than berries. Patience pays off, since harvest begins in the third year but a healthy bed can produce for up to twelve years. Feed and mulch the bed annually and let ferns grow after harvest to build the crowns. Harvest spears for only a few weeks the first productive year, then longer as plants mature.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; needs a long, warm season and a sturdy trellis.
directions: Direct sow seed after the last frost in warm soil, or start indoors to gain time in short seasons. These vigorous vines need a long, warm season and benefit from a strong trellis that lets the swan-necked fruits hang and shape naturally. The emerald green shells speckled with light green make striking autumn displays. For crafts, let gourds mature fully on the vine, then cure them in a dry, airy place for weeks to months until the shells harden. Cured hard-shelled gourds last indefinitely and take paint and carving well.

plant_note: Sow 2–6 weeks before the first fall frost; spelt establishes before winter dormancy and resumes vigorous growth in early spring.
directions: Drill or broadcast seed at 80–100 lb per acre (approximately 2–3 oz per 100 sq ft) in fall to 1 in depth; spelt is taller than modern wheat and slightly more susceptible to lodging in rich soils, so avoid high-nitrogen amendments. It tolerates clay and compacted soils better than most small grains and has reasonable resistance to rust diseases. Harvest at hard dough stage in early summer by cutting, sheaving, and drying for a week before threshing; spelt retains a tough hull (spikelet) that requires a dedicated pearler or dehuller before milling. The resulting flour has a mild, nutty flavor and is widely used by those who tolerate ancient wheats better than modern varieties, though it does contain gluten.

plant_note: Direct sow 4–6 weeks before last frost in spring; also sow in fall 6–8 weeks before first frost for overwintering where winters are mild.
directions: Sow seed 1/2 in deep in fertile, slightly alkaline soil and keep evenly moist; germination is slow in warm soil above 70°F. Thin to 3–6 in and fertilize with nitrogen to encourage lush, dark-green growth. Begin harvesting outer leaves once plants have 6–8 leaves, or cut the whole plant about 1 in above the crown for regrowth. Bolting is triggered by long days and heat; choose slow-bolt varieties for extended spring harvests.

plant_note: Sow densely in cool weather; can also be grown indoors year-round.
directions: Sow seed densely in shallow rows or broad bands and keep the surface moist for even germination. Fast-growing baby spinach leaves add flavor and nutrients to salads, sandwiches, smoothies, and pesto. This crop can be grown indoors at any time of year, so move containers as needed for the best light and cool temperatures. Harvest leaves young, when just a few inches tall, by cutting above the growing point so plants regrow. Successive sowings keep tender baby leaves coming through the cool seasons.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil; needs room to vine.
directions: Direct sow seed after the last frost in warm, rich soil, planting in hills with plenty of room for the vines to run. This small pie pumpkin, a 1966 heirloom crossing Sugar Pie and Jack O'Lantern, averages six to seven inches tall with sweet, fine-textured yellow-orange flesh. Give plants steady water and consistent feeding for good fruit set and size. Harvest when the rind hardens and turns fully colored, leaving a length of stem attached. Cure the fruit in a warm spot to improve flavor and storage life.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil is warm.
directions: Plant seeds 1 inch deep in warm, loose, well-drained soil enriched with aged compost. Thin to one plant per hill and keep the bed consistently moist throughout the growing season. Monitor frequently for squash vine borers and squash bugs, which are the most serious pests. Harvest fruits at 6–8 inches long while the skin is still soft and pale yellow for the best flavor and texture.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in blocks for good pollination.
directions: Direct sow seed after the last frost in warm soil, planting in blocks of several short rows to ensure good pollination. Each compact four-foot stalk produces two or more ruby-red, strawberry-shaped ears two to three inches long. The ornamental foliage and ears look striking at the back of a border as well as in the vegetable patch. Let the ears dry fully on the stalk, then harvest and cure the kernels before popping. Keep this popcorn separated from other corn types to avoid cross-pollination.

plant_note: Compact icebox type suited to shorter seasons and small gardens.
directions: This compact icebox watermelon produces round 8 to 10 pound fruit with sweet red flesh on shorter vines. Start seed indoors or direct sow once soil is thoroughly warm, and use black mulch in cool regions. Water steadily during vine growth and fruit set, then ease off as melons ripen to intensify sweetness. Signs of ripeness include a yellowing ground spot and a dried tendril nearest the fruit. Because it matures relatively quickly, it succeeds even where summers are short.

plant_note: Direct sow as soon as soil can be worked in early spring.
directions: Direct sow seed as soon as the soil can be worked in early spring, since peas thrive in cool weather. Bred by the developer of the original snap pea, this improved variety offers stringless three-inch pods, shorter vines, and better disease resistance. The compact plants need only light support and produce sweet, crunchy edible pods. Keep the soil evenly moist during flowering and pod fill for the best flavor and yield. Pick pods often while young and plump to keep the plants producing.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; tall vines need a sturdy trellis.
directions: Direct sow seed in early spring as soon as the soil can be worked and set up a tall, sturdy trellis. This striking variety bears tender, fine-flavored purple edible pods and lovely purple flowers on six-to-seven-foot vines. Long hypertendrils act as extra supports, helping the vigorous plants cling to their trellis. Keep soil evenly moist during flowering and pod set for the best flavor. Harvest pods young and often while the vines climb, picking before they toughen.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil; give vines room to run.
directions: Direct sow seed after the last frost in warm, rich soil, planting in hills with ample space for the vines. This heirloom, a standard since the 1800s, produces small six-to-seven-inch fruits with sweet flesh ideal for pies, soups, muffins, and casseroles. The compact fruits also work for small carving jack-o'-lanterns. Provide steady water and feeding for good fruit set, and harvest when the rind is hard and fully colored. Cured fruit stores for months in a cool, dry place.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; tall vines benefit from a trellis.
directions: Direct sow seed as soon as the soil can be worked in early spring and provide a trellis for the tall vines. This All-America Selections winner produces an extended harvest of thick, crunchy three-inch edible pods bursting with sweet flavor. The vigorous plants have some heat tolerance but still perform best in cool weather. Keep soil evenly moist during flowering and pod fill for tender, sweet pods. Pick regularly while young to encourage continued production and enjoy them fresh off the vine.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Start seed indoors about six weeks before the last frost and transplant seedlings out after frost danger passes. This indeterminate favorite produces long clusters of plump, tangerine-colored cherry tomatoes that are exceptionally sweet and juicy. Provide a tall stake or cage, since the vigorous vines easily reach six feet or more. Keep watering even to reduce cracking and pick fruit at peak color, or a bit early to finish ripening indoors. These low-acid tomatoes are rarely found in stores and are a special garden treat.

plant_note: Direct-sow at or after last frost; oilseed sunflower varieties have smaller, black-hulled seeds with a higher oil percentage than confection types.
directions: Plant seed 1 in deep and 8–12 in apart in rows spaced 24–30 in; oilseed varieties like 'Peredovik' or 'Rostov' are grown more densely than confection types to reduce branching and produce a single large head per plant. Harvest when the back of the head turns yellow to brown and the seeds are hard — cut heads and hang upside-down in a covered, airy space to cure. Rub seeds out over a screen, then cold-press for oil or roast and use as a whole food. Birds are the main pest; protect ripening heads with netting or paper bags.

plant_note: Grow indoors in trays year-round; not dependent on outdoor season.
directions: Soak the black oil sunflower seed for several hours, then sow densely across a shallow tray of moist growing medium. Keep the tray covered and dark until the seedlings push up, then move to bright light to green up. These nutrient-dense greens are high in iron, calcium, protein, and healthy fats and can be grown indoors year-round. Harvest by snipping the stems just above the soil once the first true leaves begin to show, usually within about ten days. Keep the medium evenly moist and provide good airflow to prevent mold.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Start seed indoors about six weeks before the last frost and transplant seedlings out once frost danger passes. This determinate Roma sets a dependable mid-season crop of large, uniform paste tomatoes even in heat. The concentrated harvest is ideal for making sauce or paste, and the meaty fruits also chop well for salads and salsas. As a bush type it needs only light support and produces most of its fruit over a short window. In long-season areas a successive planting extends the harvest.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Start seed indoors six to eight weeks before the last frost and transplant into warm soil after frost danger passes. This 1941 Hungarian heirloom is the traditional pickling type, bearing long, bright yellow, thick-walled sweet peppers. Enjoy the fruits fresh as a snack, in salads, grilled, or in any recipe calling for sweet pepper. Left on the plant, the peppers ripen through orange to red and grow even sweeter. Keep plants evenly watered and pick regularly to encourage a heavy set.

plant_note: Direct sow at last frost date once soil temperature reaches at least 55°F; sow in blocks of at least 4 rows for good pollination.
directions: Sow seeds 1–2 inches deep in blocks rather than single rows to ensure adequate wind pollination; block width should be at least 4 rows. Side-dress with nitrogen fertilizer when plants reach knee-height and again at tasseling to support high yields. Silks turn brown and dry about 20 days after they appear, signaling readiness; peel back the husk and pierce a kernel to check for milky juice as the ripeness test. Eat or preserve immediately after harvest for maximum sweetness, especially with modern supersweet varieties.

plant_note: Start indoors or direct sow after frost in warm soil.
directions: Start seed indoors in peat pots a few weeks before the last frost, or direct sow once the soil is warm, giving vines plenty of room. This honeydew ripens to an unbelievably sweet, almost wine-like flavor far superior to store-bought melons. Each seven-by-eight-inch fruit weighs about seven to eight pounds with light green flesh and skin. Water consistently while vines grow and fruit sets, then ease off as melons ripen to concentrate sugars. Harvest when the blossom end softens and the rind lightens to creamy color.

plant_note: Set out slips 2–4 weeks after last frost when soil is reliably warm; sweet potato vines are extremely cold-sensitive.
directions: Plant rooted slips 4 in deep in well-drained, loose soil on raised ridges or mounded rows to warm quickly; slips are propagated by sprouting cured roots in a warm, humid space 6–8 weeks before planting out. Resist fertilizing heavily with nitrogen, which favors vines over tubers. Harvest before the first frost by carefully lifting roots with a broad fork; any nicks will rot in storage. Cure freshly dug sweet potatoes at 85–90°F with high humidity for 10 days before storing at 55–60°F.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Start seed indoors about six weeks before the last frost and set plants out once frost danger passes. This indeterminate heirloom-style cherry tomato is known for hearty, sweet flavor and an early harvest. It produces large numbers of three-quarter to one-inch fruits in grape-like clusters on vigorous vines that easily reach six feet. Provide sturdy support and keep watering even to reduce cracking of these low-acid tomatoes. Pick clusters as they ripen to keep new fruit setting all season.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost in spring; also sow in late summer for a fall crop.
directions: Each chard seed is actually a cluster of seeds, so thin aggressively to 6–12 in once seedlings establish; sow 1/2 in deep. Swiss chard tolerates more summer heat than spinach, making it a valuable warm-gap green as long as irrigation is consistent. Harvest outer stalks when 8–10 in long, cutting cleanly at the base without disturbing the crown. Plants will produce continuously until heavy frost; mulching extends the season into early winter in temperate climates.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed and soil has warmed to at least 65°F.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix and provide consistent heat of 80°F until seedlings emerge. Transplant to well-drained soil in the sunniest garden position, as Capsicum frutescens types thrive with maximum heat accumulation throughout the season. Distinctive erect fruits ripen from yellow-green through yellow and orange to red; harvest at the red stage for the highest capsaicin concentration. Plants are short-lived perennials in frost-free climates and can be overwintered in pots to produce a second heavy crop.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; compact vines suit small gardens.
directions: Direct sow seed after the last frost in warm soil, or start indoors in peat pots for an early crop. This bush-type acorn squash matures early on compact four-foot vines, making it great for small gardens, large containers, and short seasons. The fruits offer the rich, sweet flavor of baked winter squash prized on cool autumn days. Water steadily during vine growth and fruit set, then let the rinds harden fully before harvest. Cure and store the squash in a cool, dry place for use through fall and winter.

plant_note: Plant corms at or just after last frost once soil reaches 65°F; in zones 8–11 taro may stay in the ground year-round.
directions: Plant small corms or corm sections 2–3 inches deep in moist to wet, organically rich soil; taro grows naturally in waterlogged conditions and tolerates standing water at the margins of ponds and streams. Provide afternoon shade in the hottest climates to prevent leaf scorch, and keep the soil consistently moist throughout the growing season — drought stress greatly reduces corm size. Harvest when the large shield-shaped leaves begin to yellow and die back, typically 6–9 months after planting, carefully digging corms to avoid damage. The corms must be thoroughly cooked before eating to neutralize calcium oxalate crystals that cause intense mouth irritation in raw form; young leaves and stems are also edible when cooked.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil; grow on a trellis for straight fruit.
directions: Direct sow seed after the last frost in warm soil, or start indoors in peat pots to gain a head start. This Japanese-type cucumber bears slender, long, burpless fruits with thin, sweet, non-bitter skin and very few tiny seeds. Grow the highly productive plants on a trellis to save space and encourage straight, clean fruit. Keep watering even to prevent bitterness and misshapen cucumbers. Harvest frequently while fruits are still young and tender to keep the vines producing.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; excels as a fall crop sown 6–8 weeks before first frost, as light freezes sweeten the leaves.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep and thin to 6–12 inches for full rosettes; closer spacing produces baby greens for salad mix. Tatsoi forms a flat, ground-hugging rosette of dark spoon-shaped leaves and is remarkably cold-hardy, surviving temperatures into the low 20s°F under row cover. Harvest whole plants when 4–6 inches across or pick outer leaves progressively. Avoid planting in summer as heat causes immediate bolting.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil; no staking needed.
directions: Direct sow seed after the last frost once the soil is warm, planting in rows or blocks for easy picking. This French filet bean, or haricot vert, is at its best when harvested young and slender and picked frequently. The disease-resistant bush plants need no staking and grow well in containers. Keep the soil evenly moist during flowering and pod set for tender, straight pods. Pick every couple of days at petite size to keep the plants producing over a long window.

plant_note: Sow after last frost when soil is warm; teff tolerates both waterlogged and drought conditions better than most grain crops.
directions: Surface-broadcast seed — it is extremely small (poppy-seed sized) and must not be buried; mix seed with sand to achieve even distribution at roughly 2–3 lb per 1,000 sq ft, then press lightly with a roller or board for good soil contact. Teff tillers prolifically and forms a graceful, open canopy with hundreds of fine panicles per plant; harvest the entire plant at the base when the majority of seeds have turned brown but before they shatter in a breeze. Thresh by beating bundles over a smooth tarp and winnow carefully in a gentle airflow — the seed is light and easily blown away. Teff grain is naturally gluten-free and is the basis for Ethiopian injera flatbread.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil; grow on a trellis for long, straight fruit.
directions: Direct sow seed after the last frost in warm soil, or start indoors in peat pots for an early crop. This English-type heirloom from the 1800s bears long, thin-skinned, burpless fruits up to eighteen inches with very small, soft seeds. Originally bred for greenhouses, it now thrives outdoors and produces best when trained up a trellis. Keep watering even to keep fruit straight and free of bitterness. Harvest the long cucumbers while young and tender for the sweetest flavor.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; tolerant of cool soil conditions.
directions: Direct sow seed after the last frost, though this variety tolerates cooler soil better than most cucumbers. The thin-skinned, sweet, burpless, acid-free fruits are a delight harvested at eight inches or shorter. Pick early for pickling or let fruit grow to full size as a slicer, since it works well for both. Tendergreen resists powdery mildew, adding to its reliability in the garden. Keep watering even and harvest frequently to keep the vigorous vines productive.

plant_note: Direct sow spring through fall; tolerates both heat and cold well.
directions: Direct sow seed shallowly and keep the bed moist for quick germination. This versatile Japanese green, also called komatsuna, is milder than most mustards and very easy to grow. It quickly produces flat, smooth, dark, glossy green leaves and tolerates both heat and cold better than spinach. Harvest outer leaves as needed or cut whole plants young for tender greens. Successive sowings from spring through fall keep a steady supply for stir-fries and salads.

plant_note: Direct sow in loose, stone-free soil once the ground can be worked; make succession sowings.
directions: Sow seed shallowly, about a quarter inch deep, directly in deeply worked, loose soil so the long imperator-type roots can grow straight. Keep the seedbed consistently moist during the slow germination period and thin plants to a couple inches apart once seedlings are established. Harvest these long, slender, nearly coreless roots at around 8 to 9 inches for the sweetest flavor; the deep orange-red color holds up well to steaming, baking, and freezing.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–6 weeks after last frost once soil reaches 65°F; tepary bean is a drought-adapted desert crop that actually produces poorly in wet soils.
directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep directly into very well-drained, low-fertility, sandy or rocky desert-type soil after the soil is thoroughly warm; avoid overwatering, as excess moisture promotes disease and poor productivity in this drought-adapted species. Tepary bean is an ancient domesticate of the Sonoran Desert and O'odham peoples, capable of completing its life cycle on surprisingly little water. Both vining and bush types exist; most modern varieties are semi-vining and benefit from support. Harvest entire plants when the majority of pods have dried and rattled, thresh the seeds, and dry thoroughly before storage; the small seeds (white, brown, tan, or speckled) are high in protein and have a nutty, earthy flavor excellent in Southwest-style soups and stews.

plant_note: Start indoors in late winter and transplant out as soon as the soil is workable; a short-day type best for southern gardens.
directions: Start seed indoors in late winter and transplant seedlings out once the ground can be worked, spacing them four to five inches apart for large bulbs. This is a short-day variety, so grow it in southern regions where it forms bulbs as days lengthen only slightly; the sweet, mild bulbs can reach up to a pound. Keep the soil evenly moist and weed-free, then cure the harvested bulbs in a dry, airy spot before storing.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed and nights stay above 55°F.
directions: Sow 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix at 82–85°F; Thai chili needs more heat for germination than most C. annuum types. Plants are compact and extremely productive, bearing dozens of small (1–2 inch) upright fruits that ripen from green to bright red and carry intense heat of 50,000–100,000 Scoville units. Harvest red-ripe fruits for maximum heat and full flavor; the plants can be brought indoors as houseplants before frost and often survive multiple seasons. Excellent for drying — the small fruits air-dry on the plant or can be strung and hung in a warm spot.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after soil warms to at least 65°F and all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix at 80–85°F and maintain consistent heat throughout the seedling stage; Thai eggplant germinates and grows faster than larger-fruited types. Transplant to a warm, sheltered bed after last frost and mulch heavily to retain soil warmth. The round, golf ball-sized fruits ripen from white-striped green to fully white or yellow depending on the cultivar, and are harvested while still firm and slightly underripe for use in Thai green curry. Overripe fruits become bitter; check plants every 2–3 days and harvest promptly to keep plants productive.

plant_note: Start indoors with bottom heat; transplant only after nights stay reliably warm.
directions: Start seed indoors with warm bottom heat several weeks before the last frost, since these peppers need heat to germinate well. Transplant the compact, mounding 8-inch plants outdoors only after all danger of frost has passed and nights are warm. Each plant carries dozens of upright, slender 1.5 to 3 inch fruits that ripen from green to red at the same time for a very ornamental, very spicy display; they do well in containers.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost, or start indoors a couple weeks early.
directions: Sow seed directly in warm soil after the danger of frost has passed, or start indoors a couple weeks early and transplant carefully. This compact vine saves garden space while producing uniform 1 to 1.25 pound fruits with creamy-white rinds boldly striped in green and orange. Let fruits fully mature and cure the hard rinds before storing; the smooth, sweet flesh is as good for eating as the striking rinds are for autumn displays.

plant_note: Bolts in heat; best as a spring and fall crop.
directions: This tender Asian green forms loose, frilly, pale chartreuse leaves used raw in salads or lightly cooked. Direct sow shallowly and thin to about 8 inches, or harvest thinnings as baby greens. It grows very fast and prefers cool weather, bolting quickly once temperatures climb. Keep soil evenly moist to maintain the crisp, mild leaves and prevent bitterness. Sow in early spring and again in late summer for tender harvests bracketing the heat of summer.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring for summer and fall harvest; heat tolerant.
directions: Direct sow seed in spring for summer and fall harvests, thinning to about an inch apart. These Japanese scallions are long, slender, and mild, forming no true bulb; blanch the stems by hilling soil around the bases for 16 to 18 inch lengths of tender white shank. The heat-tolerant plants grow well in the garden or a container and are excellent sliced into salads, soups, and stir-fries.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant 1–2 weeks after frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm, moist starting mix about 6–8 weeks before transplanting. Plant at least two plants for cross-pollination, which is necessary for fruit set. Allow the husk to fully enclose the fruit and turn papery before harvesting. Fruit is ready when it fills the husk and the husk begins to split.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant 1–2 weeks after last frost.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix and keep at 70–85°F until sprouts emerge. Transplant after nights reliably stay above 50°F, burying the stem up to the lowest leaves to encourage a strong root system. Provide a cage or stake for support, and water consistently at the base to reduce the risk of blossom-end rot. Harvest when fruit is fully colored and gives slightly to gentle pressure.

plant_note: Direct sow as soon as soil is workable; ideal for shallow beds and containers.
directions: Sow seed shallowly in loose soil as soon as the ground can be worked, keeping the bed evenly moist through the slow germination. This Italian heirloom, whose name means Round of Paris, forms deep-orange, golf-ball-shaped roots that are sweet and tender. Its short, round shape means shallow roots, so it thrives in shallow beds and containers where longer carrots would struggle; harvest young for the best texture.

plant_note: Direct sow or transplant in spring or late summer; flavor sweetens after frost.
directions: Sow seed directly or start transplants for spring and late-summer crops, spacing plants 12 to 18 inches apart. This vigorous hybrid produces tall, upright plants with slightly savoyed leaves and regrows quickly for repeated cut-and-come-again harvests. Collards are cold hardy and grow well far beyond the South; a light frost actually sweetens the nutritious, vitamin-rich leaves, so leave plants standing into cold weather.

plant_note: Direct sow as soon as soil is workable; sow successions for a steady supply.
directions: Direct sow the corky seed clusters as soon as the soil can be worked, thinning seedlings to three or four inches apart since each cluster yields several plants. This golden beet holds its bright color when cooked and is notably sweet and mild, with less of the earthy flavor of red types. Harvest roots when they reach two to three inches across, and enjoy the nutritious tops as cooked greens.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring and again in late summer; more bolt tolerant than most.
directions: Direct sow seed in cool weather for spring and fall crops, thinning to about four to six inches apart. This miniature variety grows just 5 inches tall and is ready to harvest in as few as 30 days, with thick white stems and glossy dark green leaves. It tolerates warm weather better than most bok choy, but grow it quickly and keep it evenly watered to discourage bolting; its compact size suits containers well.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost; harvest fruit young and often.
directions: Sow seed directly in warm soil after all danger of frost, spacing plants for good airflow. This zucchini has an open growing habit on compact bush-type plants with reduced spines, making harvest much easier on the hands. It offers intermediate resistance to zucchini yellow mosaic virus and watermelon mosaic virus; pick the tender fruits young and frequently to keep plants producing.

plant_note: Eat young as summer squash or mature as winter squash.
directions: This Italian moschata squash produces long curved fruit eaten green like zucchini or ripened tan for winter storage. Direct sow in warm soil and trellis the vigorous climbing vines to save space and keep fruit straight. Harvested young at 8 to 12 inches, the dense flesh has few seeds and resists getting mushy when cooked. Its moschata parentage gives it strong resistance to squash vine borers. Left to ripen fully, the mature fruit stores for months like butternut.

plant_note: Start indoors; transplant out after frost and provide sturdy support.
directions: Start seed indoors about six weeks before your last frost and transplant seedlings out once nights stay warm. This indeterminate cherry vine keeps growing all season, so give it a tall stake or trellis and prune as needed. Each ripe 1.5 inch fruit is nearly translucent and golden with orange-to-pink stripes, bright, fruity, and very sweet with a long shelf life; harvest continuously to encourage new fruit set.

plant_note: Direct sow or transplant in cool weather; sow successions for a steady supply.
directions: Sow seed shallowly in cool weather, since lettuce needs light and cool soil to germinate; make successive sowings for a continuous harvest. This deep-red mini-romaine is compact and easy to grow in small spaces, with uniform, upright leaves that stay clean and are perfect for Caesar salads. It carries disease resistance to downy mildew and lettuce dieback, and part shade helps it hold quality in warmer weather.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost; plant in blocks for good pollination.
directions: Sow seed directly in warm soil after frost, planting in blocks of at least four short rows rather than one long row to ensure good wind pollination. This old-fashioned sugary (su) corn produces large 9 inch ears that are very sweet and tender. The plants resist stalk-lodging and are less likely to bend or kink in windy areas; keep them well watered and harvest at the milk stage for peak sweetness.

plant_note: Start rhizomes indoors 4–6 weeks before the last frost; move outside only when soil has warmed to 65°F and nights stay above 50°F.
directions: Select firm rhizome pieces each with at least one swollen bud, soak in water for an hour, then plant 2–3 in deep in loose, compost-rich soil once the ground has thoroughly warmed. Turmeric grows best with bright indirect light or filtered afternoon shade in hot climates and needs consistent moisture throughout the season — never let the soil dry out completely. Feed monthly with a balanced fertilizer; plants develop broad, tropical-looking leaves reaching 2–4 ft. Dig rhizomes after foliage yellows and dies back naturally, usually 8–10 months after planting, then cure at room temperature for a week before using fresh, drying, or storing.

plant_note: Sow 2–4 weeks before last frost for spring harvest, or 6–8 weeks before first frost for a fall crop.
directions: Broadcast or drill seed 1/4 to 1/2 in deep and thin to 4–6 in when seedlings are 2–3 in tall; use the tops as nutritious braising greens. Cool weather sweetens turnips considerably, and fall-sown roots are typically milder than spring ones. Harvest at 2–3 in diameter for the best tenderness; larger roots become woody and sharp in flavor. Flea beetles can disfigure foliage — row cover offers good protection early in the season.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost for spring; sow again in early fall for a second crop of sweet greens.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in fertile soil; turnips are among the fastest-germinating brassicas and begin producing harvestable greens within a month. Thin to 4–6 in if growing for both greens and roots, or leave closer together for greens-only production. Begin harvesting outer leaves when plants are 6–8 in tall, leaving the crown to continue growing. Fall-harvested greens are noticeably sweeter than spring greens due to the conversion of starches to sugars in cool temperatures.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant for spring or fall; a self-blanching type.
directions: Start seed indoors and transplant seedlings for a spring or fall crop, keeping plants evenly watered and never letting them check from stress. This self-blanching variety has well-wrapping leaves that curl over the developing curd, shielding the snowy-white heads from sun scald and rain discoloration. It reliably produces uniform, dense, medium-large heads and tolerates heat, so in mild climates it can even be grown nearly year-round.

plant_note: Plant tubers at or just before last frost in cool, moist soil; ulluco initiates tubers only as day length shortens in fall.
directions: Plant small seed tubers 3 inches deep in loose, fertile, well-drained soil as early in spring as soil can be worked; ulluco is an Andean root crop closely related to beets that prefers cool, foggy conditions and struggles in summer heat above 80°F. The attractive heart-shaped leaves are glossy and edible as salad greens throughout the season, adding a mild, slightly mucilaginous element similar to malabar spinach. Tuber formation is triggered by short days in autumn, so harvest cannot begin until after the first frost kills the foliage, typically in October or November. The small, colorful tubers (yellow, orange, red, or purple-streaked) are waxy and hold their shape when cooked; boil, roast, or stir-fry in the same way as fingerling potatoes.

plant_note: Sow densely in trays indoors year-round; harvest at the first true-leaf stage.
directions: Sow the seed densely across a shallow tray of moist growing mix and keep it evenly moist under bright light. This Asian blend of savory and spicy brassica-type greens is harvested very young, when the first true leaves appear, roughly one to three weeks after sowing. Snip the tender shoots at soil level with scissors and use the complex, umami-rich mix as a garnish for Asian dishes; sow in succession for a steady supply indoors year-round.

plant_note: Sow in trays indoors, or direct sow outdoors in cool weather as a leafy green.
directions: Sow seed densely in a tray for microgreens or thinly outdoors in cool soil to grow as a peppery leafy green. Also called creasy greens, upland cress is high in vitamins A and C and delivers a sharp, watercress-like bite without needing running water. Keep it consistently moist, since dryness makes it more pungent and prone to bolting; harvest microgreens at the true-leaf stage or pick mature outer leaves for sandwiches and salads.

plant_note: Start indoors early; celery needs a long, mild, moist growing season.
directions: Start the tiny seed indoors 10 to 12 weeks before transplanting, pressing it into the surface since it needs light to germinate and is slow to sprout. Celery grows best in climates with long, mild-temperature seasons and demands consistently rich, moist soil to develop tender stalks. Harvest individual stalks when they reach about 6 inches, or wait for the full bunch to mature; keep the plants well watered throughout to avoid stringy, bitter stems.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost; give vines room to run.
directions: Sow seed directly in warm soil after all danger of frost, giving the vines plenty of room to spread. This heirloom is unique among winter squashes: when cooked, the flesh separates into spaghetti-like strands that stay tender yet al dente. Let fruits fully ripen to a hard, golden rind and cure them before storing; the mild, low-calorie flesh is delicious with a bit of butter or a favorite sauce.

plant_note: Direct sow or transplant in cool weather; bolt resistant for later sowing.
directions: Sow seed shallowly in cool weather, keeping the soil moist and cool for good germination, and make succession sowings for continuous salads. This emerald-green romaine forms tight heads that reach a striking 12 to 16 inches tall and 4 to 6 inches across, giving large, sturdy leaves ideal for salads and lettuce wraps. Its bolt resistance and vigorous growth let it hold longer into warm weather than many romaines.

plant_note: Start indoors or overwinter in mild areas; a long-day type that matures early.
directions: Start seed indoors in late winter and transplant out once the soil is workable, or sow in fall to overwinter in mild-winter regions. This famous long-day onion traveled from Italy to Walla Walla, Washington, and produces enormous, juicy, sweet bulbs that are one of the best for eating fresh. It matures early enough to grow as an intermediate-day type; these sweet onions do not store long, so enjoy them soon after harvest.

plant_note: Start indoors for spring, or sow in summer for a fall crop; adapted to fall.
directions: Start seed indoors for a spring crop or sow in mid to late summer for a fall harvest, spacing transplants 18 to 24 inches apart. This heirloom is specially adapted to fall growing and produces 4 to 6 inch blue-green central heads followed by a prolific flush of side shoots. Cut the central head while the buds are still tight, then keep harvesting the smaller side shoots for weeks; the plants tolerate frost well.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost; give vines plenty of space to run.
directions: Sow seed directly in warm soil after all danger of frost, allowing the long vines room to spread. This classic butternut yields four to five tan, richly sweet fruits per plant, and its solid stems help resist squash vine borers. Let the squash mature fully to a hard rind and cure it in a warm, dry spot; properly cured butternut stores for months and is excellent roasted, baked, or pureed into soup.

plant_note: Direct sow as soon as the soil can be worked; more heat tolerant than most peas.
directions: Direct sow seed as soon as the ground can be worked in spring, since peas germinate and grow best in cool soil. Introduced in 1943 as a cross between Laxton's Progress and Perfection, this heirloom is both cold tolerant for early sowing and more heat tolerant than most peas, extending the season on both ends. The sturdy 24 to 30 inch plants carry seven to nine medium peas per pod; provide light support and pick regularly to keep them producing.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring; cold hardy and can overwinter in many areas.
directions: Direct sow seed in spring for summer harvests, thinning to about an inch apart, or leave clumps to overwinter. This 2016 All-America Selections winner is a cold-hardy bunching onion with crisp texture and a strong, pungent flavor, and it resists pink root rot. Harvest by pulling whole clusters of upright green stalks, or cut individual stems and let the plants regrow for repeated cut-and-come-again picking.

plant_note: Plant sprouted corms 2–4 weeks after last frost once water temperatures exceed 60°F in a container or shallow pond.
directions: Sprout small corms in a tray of moist soil indoors a few weeks before transplanting; plant sprouted corms 1–2 inches deep in the mud bottom of containers, tubs, or shallow water features kept filled to 2–4 inches above the soil surface. Water chestnuts are tropical grass-like sedges grown for their crunchy, sweet corms that form in the mud at the base of the rush-like stems. Drain the growing container in early fall before frost and allow the foliage to die back completely, then harvest corms by sifting through the mud; the crisp, mild corms can be eaten raw or cooked and retain their crunch even after stir-frying. In frost-prone climates store harvested corms in moist sand in a cool (40°F) location and re-plant the following spring.

plant_note: Direct sow or transplant stem cuttings 2–4 weeks after last frost once soil and water temperatures are consistently warm.
directions: Sow seeds 1/2 inch deep or root stem cuttings in moist to waterlogged soil; this semi-aquatic vine thrives in boggy beds, the margins of ponds, or in containers of shallow water. Harvest young shoot tips and tender leaves 4–6 inches long by snipping with scissors; plants regrow quickly and can be cut every 2–3 weeks throughout the warm season. The hollow stems and arrowhead-shaped leaves are a staple of Thai, Filipino, Chinese, and Indonesian cooking, most famously stir-fried with garlic and shrimp paste. Note that cultivation is restricted in some U.S. states due to invasive potential in warm climates; check local regulations before planting outdoors.

plant_note: Sow in cool, wet conditions 2–4 weeks before last frost; can also be started from cuttings rooted in water.
directions: Sow seed on the surface of consistently moist, fertile soil near a stream, pond edge, or other reliably wet site; watercress requires more water than almost any other salad green. In garden beds without standing water, water deeply every day and mulch heavily to retain moisture. Harvest stem tips of 4–6 in regularly to encourage bushy growth; the plant becomes bitter and coarse after flowering. Succession plantings from rooted stem cuttings placed in shallow water provide a continuous supply through the cool season.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–3 weeks after last frost once soil temperature reaches at least 70°F; or start indoors 2–3 weeks early.
directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm, sandy loam hills with generous compost worked in; watermelons thrive with long, hot summers. Avoid overhead irrigation once fruits are sizing up, as uneven moisture causes cracking. Ripeness indicators include a dull surface sheen replacing the glossy skin, a cream or yellow ground spot on the underside, and a dry tendril nearest the fruit. Thump the fruit and listen for a deep, hollow resonance to confirm ripeness.

plant_note: Sow 8–10 weeks before first fall frost for the sweetest, most vibrant roots; spring plantings are possible but fall is strongly preferred.
directions: Direct sow seeds 1/2 inch deep in loose, deeply prepared, stone-free soil in late summer or early fall; thin seedlings to 4–6 inches for proper root development. Watermelon radish is a Chinese heirloom daikon type with a pale green exterior that conceals a vivid magenta-to-pink interior resembling watermelon flesh. Fall planting is strongly preferred over spring — cool nights concentrate sugars and deepen the interior color while reducing pungency. Harvest when roots reach 2–3 inches in diameter; slice thinly for salads, pickle in rice vinegar, or roast where the color intensifies to brilliant crimson.

plant_note: Sow 4–6 weeks before last frost in spring; fall sowing in mild climates produces overwintered clumps ready for early spring harvest.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in rows and thin to 4–6 inches, or start in trays and transplant in clusters of 3–5 seedlings. Welsh onion does not form a bulb but produces clumps of hollow, cylindrical green stalks that are harvested like scallions; the entire green top and white base are edible. Harvest outer stalks from established clumps as needed, leaving the center to continue growing; clumps also divide easily and can be split every 2–3 years. Extremely cold-hardy and virtually maintenance-free once established, it serves as a permanent perennial scallion in most temperate gardens.

plant_note: Soak seed, then sow densely in trays indoors year-round for juicing.
directions: Soak the hard red winter wheat seed overnight, then spread it densely over a tray of moist growing mix and keep it evenly moist and bright. The seed sprouts rapidly into lush green blades that are ready to cut in about eight to twelve days, when they reach 6 to 9 inches tall. Snip the grass at the base for juicing, which yields a drink rich in vitamins, minerals, and chlorophyll; sow fresh trays in succession for a continuous supply.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring and again in late summer for fall harvest.
directions: Direct sow seed in cool weather for spring and fall crops, thinning seedlings to two to four inches apart. This premium Japanese salad turnip forms pure white, smooth-textured roots that are mild and sweet, delicious raw in salads and crudite trays as well as pickled or cooked. Grow it quickly with steady moisture and harvest young as a baby turnip for the best crisp, tender quality.

plant_note: Direct sow in succession spring through summer; heat and cold tolerant.
directions: Direct sow seed in succession from spring through summer, thinning to about an inch apart for a steady supply of scallions. Popular since the 1700s, this dependable heirloom green onion thrives in a wide range of soils and tolerates both heat and cold. Blanch the stems by hilling soil around the bases for longer white shanks, and harvest young for mild flavor before any bulb forms.

plant_note: Tolerates cooler soil than common beans but still frost tender.
directions: White-flowered runner bean prized for its large white seeds and vigorous ornamental vines. Sow seeds 1.5 inches deep at the base of a tall trellis or teepee once soil warms to 60 F. Keep soil evenly moist during flowering to ensure good pod set, as heat above 90 F can cause blossom drop. Harvest young pods for snap use or allow to dry on the vine for shell beans. In mild climates the fleshy roots may overwinter and resprout the following spring.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring or late summer; flavor sweetens with cold weather.
directions: Direct sow or transplant this kale in spring or late summer, spacing plants 12 to 18 inches apart. Introduced in 1994, White Russian is a white-ribbed cross between Red Russian and Siberian kale that grows especially sweet in cool weather and stands up well to frost. Harvest young leaves for tender baby greens or let plants mature for full-sized leaves; a hard frost only improves the sweet flavor.

plant_note: Deep sandy loam gives the straightest, smoothest roots.
directions: A creamy white carrot with crisp, sweet, mild flesh and no pigment, popular in mixed rainbow bunches. Sow shallowly in deeply worked sandy loam and maintain constant surface moisture until emergence. Thin carefully to prevent crowding, which causes twisted or stunted roots. Keep beds weeded early since carrot seedlings are easily overtaken. Harvest at full size, wash gently, and store in cold, humid conditions to retain crispness.

plant_note: Direct sow very early in spring, or in fall in mild-winter regions.
directions: Direct sow seed very early in spring as soon as the soil can be worked, or in fall where winters are mild, since fava beans need cool weather to set pods. The sturdy, upright plants bear ornamental white-and-black flowers and reach several feet tall. Eat the young pods like snap beans, pinch the tender growing tips as a spring green, or shell the beans and cook them while still green for a sweet, buttery treat.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks after last frost once soil reaches 70°F; a truly tropical legume requiring a long, warm, humid season.
directions: Scarify or soak seeds 12–24 hours before sowing 1 inch deep in warm, well-drained, low-fertility soil; inoculate with cowpea rhizobium to promote nitrogen fixation. Provide a sturdy trellis or fence at least 6 feet tall as soon as plants emerge, as vines climb rapidly and are very productive when vertical. Virtually every part of the winged bean is edible — harvest young pods at 3–4 inches with frilly wings still tender, leaves and shoot tips as greens, flowers as a colorful salad garnish, and mature dried seeds as high-protein pulses. In the tropics, tuberous roots also form and can be harvested and eaten like yam.

plant_note: Start indoors 3–4 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes and soil is warm.
directions: Sow 1/2 inch deep in rich, warm soil after last frost; winter melon is a large-fruited cucurbit that needs a long warm season and a very sturdy trellis or ample ground space. The developing fruits are covered in fine white hairs that mature into a waxy white coating, which gives the fruit exceptional storage life of up to a year at room temperature. Harvest mature fruits when the waxy coating is fully developed and the fruit sounds hollow when tapped. Young fruits can also be harvested at 6–8 inches for immediate cooking in soups and stir-fries.

plant_note: Plant sprouted crown divisions at or just after last frost once soil is 60°F; start in pots indoors 4–6 weeks earlier in short-season climates.
directions: Divide the crown (the reddish propagating buds at the top of the storage root cluster) in early spring, allowing cut surfaces to dry for a day before planting 3–4 inches deep. Yacon grows into a large, sunflower-family plant producing crisp, sweet, apple-flavored storage tubers at the base. Harvest after the first frost kills the tops, digging the entire root cluster carefully; separate the large tan storage roots from the reddish crown pieces, saving the crowns in a frost-free location for replanting. Let harvested storage roots cure in sunlight for a few weeks to convert starch to fructooligosaccharides and intensify sweetness.

plant_note: Sow 2–6 weeks after last frost once soil is warm; yardlong beans love Gulf Coast heat and humidity and outperform regular beans all summer.
directions: Sow seeds 1 in deep once soil is reliably warm; yardlong beans are vigorous climbers that need a sturdy trellis or fence at least 6 feet tall. Unlike common beans, they thrive in high heat and set pods prolifically during summer weather that causes snap beans to drop their blossoms. Harvest when pods are pencil-thin and roughly 12–18 in long, well before they reach their potential 36 in — longer pods become fibrous and bitter. Check daily once pods begin forming, as they move from prime to over-mature in just 2–3 days.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil temperature reaches at least 65°F.
directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in hills of 2–3, thinning to one plant once established, in soil amended with compost for steady nutrient supply. Water consistently at the base to keep foliage dry and reduce disease. The distinctive curved neck makes ripe fruit easy to spot. Harvest fruits at 4–6 inches long with the skin still tender enough to dent with a fingernail for the best eating quality.

plant_note: Start indoors in winter; a short-day type for southern gardens.
directions: Start seed indoors in winter and transplant to the garden while still cool, since this is a short-day onion that bulbs best in southern regions. For really sweet, mild bulbs, grow it in soil that is low in sulfur; this pink-root-resistant type is among the few approved to be sold as a Vidalia onion. Keep plants weed-free and evenly watered, and cure the harvested bulbs before use, as sweet onions do not store long.

common_name: Yellow Sweet Spanish Utah Bulb Onion
plant_note: Start indoors and transplant out; a long-day type for northern gardens.
directions: Start seed indoors in late winter and transplant out as soon as the soil can be worked, spacing plants four to six inches apart. This long-day heirloom, grown in the U.S. since at least 1916, forms large 3 to 6 inch globe-shaped bulbs with mild, sweet flavor and grows best in states north of the 37th parallel. Keep the soil evenly moist during bulbing, then cure the bulbs in a dry, airy place before storing.

plant_note: Grown mainly as a dry bean; needs full season to mature seed.
directions: Also called orca or calypso bean, this dry bush bean bears striking black-and-white seeds resembling the yin-yang symbol. Direct sow 1 inch deep after frost danger passes, spacing 4 inches apart in rows. Allow pods to dry fully on the plant until they rattle before harvesting. Pull whole plants and cure them in a dry, airy spot if wet weather threatens the maturing crop. Reduce watering as pods fill to encourage even drying and prevent mold.

plant_note: Harvest flowering stems just as buds open for best flavor.
directions: Grown for its tender flowering stems, yu choy is a fast staple of Cantonese cooking with a mild, sweet flavor. Direct sow and thin to about 4 inches, keeping the soil consistently moist for quick, succulent growth. Harvest the main stem with a few leaves and a cluster of yellow buds when the first flowers just begin to open. Cutting the central stalk encourages tender side shoots for a second picking. Grow in cool weather to avoid the bitterness and toughness that come with heat.

plant_note: Start indoors from seed in late winter and transplant out in early spring.
directions: Start seed indoors in late winter and transplant seedlings out in early spring, spacing them several inches apart. This eschalion, or banana-type shallot, produces large, elongated, easy-to-peel bulbs with coppery-pink skin and a sweet, mild flavor prized by chefs. Grow it much like an onion with steady moisture and weed control, then cure the harvested bulbs in a dry, airy spot; they store well for months.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil is warm; can also start indoors 2–3 weeks before transplanting.
directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm, compost-enriched soil arranged in hills of 2–3 seeds, then thin to the strongest plant. Water at the soil level rather than overhead to reduce the risk of powdery mildew, which is the most common problem. Check plants daily once fruiting begins, as zucchini grows very rapidly. Harvest fruits at 6–8 inches for the best texture, leaving a short stem attached.
